last_updated: 2026-04-29 (layout flatten: web/index.html → index.html, docs/ mirror retired)
_source_of_truth_note: This file is the canonical mineral spec for all three runtimes. vugg.py is the dev/test harness (where pytest runs). index.html is the shipped product (GitHub Pages serves repo root; runtime fetches data/minerals.json and overwrites the inline MINERAL_SPEC_FALLBACK). agent-api/vugg-agent.js is intentionally kept simple and reads this file via require(). Cross-runtime drift is caught by tools/sync-spec.js.

_schema.max_nucleation_count: Maximum number of crystals of this species that can ever nucleate in a single run. Counted across the whole simulation (active + dissolved). Keeps common minerals like wulfenite from producing microcrystalline swarms that get immediately swallowed by other phases.
_schema.max_size_cm: Hard cap on crystal size — 2x world record per research/mineral-accuracy-audit.md. Growth halts when reached; this is the fix for the 321248% bug.
_schema.growth_rate_mult: Relative growth rate, calcite=1.0 baseline. Multiply the runtime base rate.
_schema.T_range_C: [min, max] temperature window for growth (C). null,null = unbounded.
_schema.pH_dissolution_below: pH below which mineral dissolves. null if stable.
_schema.pH_dissolution_above: pH above which mineral dissolves. null if stable.
_schema.required_ingredients: {species: min_ppm}. All must be present for growth.
_schema.thermal_decomp_C: Temperature above which mineral breaks down (C).
_schema.fluorescence: {activator, threshold_ppm, color, quencher} or null if non-fluorescent.
_schema.twin_laws: [{name, miller_indices, trigger, probability}] or [] if none known.
_schema.acid_dissolution: {pH_threshold, reaction, products} or null if acid-stable.
_schema.habit_variants: Array of habit objects. Each has: name, wall_spread (0-1, lateral spread on wall), void_reach (0-1, projection into vug), vector (projecting|coating|tabular|equant|dendritic), trigger (conditions that favor this habit). Growth engine selects habit based on σ, T, and space.
_schema.class: Mineralogical class for filtering/library UI: sulfide | carbonate | oxide | silicate | sulfate | phosphate | hydroxide | halide | arsenate | molybdate | tungstate | native.
_schema.description: 2-3 sentence geological description for library mode. Learning-audience voice; not flavor text.
_schema.scenarios: Array of scenario IDs (from SCENARIOS registry) in which this mineral can nucleate. Empirically verified with seed 42.
_schema.narrate_function: Name of dedicated _narrate_* function. null = inline flavor, needs refactor.
_schema.runtimes_present: Files where growth engine currently exists.
_schema.class_color: Hex color for topographic map visualization. Derived from mineral class — all classes of the same type share one hue on a 12-color wheel (30° spacing). Wall/matrix = #D2691E (amber).
_schema.test_cases: Optional array of self-validating test cases for the supersaturation gate. Each case: {name, fluid (partial FluidChemistry), T_C, [pressure_kbar], expects: {sigma: '>1.0' | '==0' | '>=0.5' | '<0.3'}, rationale}. Empty array = no declared cases (legacy minerals). New minerals should declare 2-3 cases minimum: positive gate hit, missing-ingredient block, and (if applicable) a mechanic-specific block. The generic test runner that exercises these cases is item 6 of proposals/TASK-BRIEF-DATA-AS-TRUTH.md (not yet shipped). See proposals/vugg-mineral-template.md §15 for the schema and tools/new-mineral.py for the scaffold seed.
_schema.fill_exempt: Backlog K (2026-05). DEPRECATED in favor of late_stage_propensity (Proposal C, 2026-05). When true, this mineral can still nucleate after the global vugFill ≥ 0.95 cap has fired. Now treated as a backward-compat alias for late_stage_propensity:1.0. New entries should set late_stage_propensity directly.
_schema.late_stage_propensity: Proposal C (2026-05, proposals/RESEARCH-GROWTH-AT-HIGH-FILL.md). Continuous 0-1 gradient generalizing the binary fill_exempt flag. Determines how much a mineral resists Proposal A's high-fill sigmoid dampener — 0.0 = vanilla (full dampening, default for bulk minerals), 1.0 = no dampening (the old fill_exempt). Effective dampener applied to a nucleation roll is D' = D + p × (1 - D), where D is the global sigmoid at the current vugFill. Literature-anchored scoring: borax/mirabilite/thenardite/sylvite/tincalconite at 1.0 (playa efflorescent set + paramorph); lepidocrocite at 0.9 (Brazilian amethyst skunk-calcite terminal patina, Proust & Fontan 2007); chalcanthite/erythrite/annabergite at 0.7-0.8 (Cu/Co/Ni bloom efflorescent crusts); goethite/chrysocolla/aurichalcite at 0.6 (supergene weathering crusts); calcite at 0.4 (Brazilian amethyst late euhedral); native_tellurium at 0.4 (Cresson Vug residual-overflow per Spry & Thieben 1996); malachite/aragonite/azurite at 0.3 (sometimes late, often bulk); quartz/fluorite/barite at 0.2 (drusy / MVT-gangue tails). Missing field = 0.0.

_audit_summary._note: Post-phase-2 state. The _initial_audit block below is frozen at phase-1 start for historical reference — do not edit. +topaz (MG-B), +tourmaline/beryl/spodumene (MG-A), +anglesite/cerussite/pyromorphite/vanadinite (Pb oxidation), +bornite/chalcocite/covellite (Cu sulfide enrichment), +cuprite/azurite/native_copper (Cu oxide-native), +magnetite/lepidocrocite/stibnite/bismuthinite/native_bismuth/clinobisvanite (Fe/Sb/Bi suite), +chrysocolla (Bisbee), +marcasite (round-2: pH<5 pyrite dimorph), +wurtzite (round-2: T>95 sphalerite dimorph), +erythrite/annabergite (round-2: paired vivianite-group arsenates), +tetrahedrite/tennantite (round-2: paired fahlore Sb/As sulfosalts), +apophyllite (round-2: zeolite-facies silicate, hematite-phantom 'bloody apophyllite' habit, deccan_zeolite scenario added), +aragonite (round-3: orthorhombic CaCO3, Mg/Ca-driven polymorph competition with calcite per Folk 1974/Morse 1997), +rhodochrosite (round-3: Mn carbonate, biggest collection-weight miss at 35 specimens), +siderite (round-3: Fe carbonate, oxidation breakdown to goethite is the diagenetic pseudomorph mechanism), +dolomite (round-3: Ca-Mg ordered carbonate, completes the carbonate quartet calcite/aragonite/rhodochrosite/siderite/dolomite).

_initial_audit._note: Frozen snapshot of the pre-phase-1 audit. For historical reference only.

habit_variants.trigger: low σ, steady growth

habit_variants.trigger: high σ, Fe³⁺ present, space-constrained

habit_variants.name: microcrystalline
habit_variants.trigger: high fill or drusy — microcrystalline chalcedony / agate carpet covering existing crystals (boundary-layer diffusion at late-stage low-σ SiO2 produces tiny-grain fabric rather than euhedral prisms)

quartz.description: The most abundant vug-lining mineral on Earth. Silica dissolved in hot fluid crystallizes on cooling — clear, smoky when irradiated, amethyst when Fe-bearing and irradiated together. The canvas on which most other vug minerals grow.

trace_ingredients.Fe: amethyst with radiation

quartz.thermal_decomp_reaction: SiO2 melting point — takes hell itself

twin_laws.name: Brazil
twin_laws._source: Frondel 1962, System of Mineralogy vol III (Silica Minerals). Penetration twin combining left and right enantiomorphs of quartz across the {11-20} plane. Recognized by V-shaped striations on prism faces and reversed optical rotation domains. Common in pegmatite + hydrothermal quartz at 10-15% of euhedral grains.

twin_laws.name: Japan
twin_laws._source: Frondel 1962, System of Mineralogy vol III. Contact twin with ~84°33' angle between c-axes; the two crystals share a {11-22} face. Type locality Tsujikawa-mura (Yamanashi, Japan); famous Cumbria + Mont Blanc examples. Rare even in good hydrothermal pockets, 2-5%.

acid_dissolution.reaction: HF etching

quartz._late_stage_note: Proposal C (2026-05): propensity:0.2. Drusy chalcedony / amethyst-pocket interior is well-documented as a late-stage layer in agate geodes; otherwise mostly bulk.

habit_variants.name: rhombohedral_nailhead

habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ

habit_variants.name: flos-ferri_acicular
habit_variants.trigger: very high σ, thin needle growth

habit_variants.name: druzy_crust
habit_variants.trigger: high fill or drusy — late-stage low-σ tiny crystal carpet on top of pre-existing growth (Brazilian amethyst 'skunk calcite' late euhedral overgrowths per Proust & Fontan 2007)

calcite.description: Retrograde solubility makes calcite the giant-maker of vugs: hotter fluid holds less Ca+CO₃, so heating precipitates calcite. Fizzes in acid. Mn²⁺ activates orange fluorescence; Fe²⁺ quenches it — the boundary between glowing and dark zones records a fluid-chemistry shift.

trace_ingredients.Fe: FL quencher

acid_dissolution.reaction: CaCO3 + 2H+ -> Ca2+ + H2O + CO2 (fizzes)

calcite.audit_status: ok — most complete implementation, use as template (round-3 update: Mg-poisoning factor added so calcite yields to aragonite when Mg/Ca > ~2)
calcite._late_stage_note: Proposal C (2026-05): propensity:0.4. Brazilian amethyst geodes (Proust & Fontan 2007): "forming throughout but most commonly as very late euhedral crystals" in the central cavity.

habit_variants.name: columnar
habit_variants.trigger: low σ — default columnar prisms, transparent to white

habit_variants.name: twinned_cyclic
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ — pseudo-hexagonal cyclic twin on {110}, six-pointed star (the diagnostic aragonite habit)

habit_variants.name: acicular_needle
habit_variants.trigger: high σ — long thin needles in radiating sprays

habit_variants.name: flos_ferri
habit_variants.trigger: Fe-rich (Fe > 30 ppm) and moderate σ — dendritic 'iron flower' coral, the Eisenerz Austrian variety

habit_variants.name: botryoidal_crust
habit_variants.trigger: high fill — late-stage radiating-fibrous botryoidal carpet (the 'cave coral' habit of speleothem aragonite, formed under boundary-layer-diffusion-limited conditions)

aragonite.description: CaCO₃ in the orthorhombic crystal system — the metastable polymorph of calcite. Same composition, different structure, kinetically favored over thermodynamically-stable calcite when (Folk 1974, Morse 1997, Sun 2015): Mg/Ca > ~1.5 (dominant control — Mg poisons calcite growth steps but is excluded from aragonite's orthorhombic structure); T > ~50°C in low-Mg waters; supersaturation Ω > ~10 (Ostwald step rule); or trace Sr/Pb/Ba present (these cations match Ca²⁺ in aragonite's 9-fold coordination but not calcite's 6-fold). At surface pressure aragonite is metastable — over geologic time it converts to calcite (half-life ~10⁵ yr at 25°C, ~10³ yr at 80°C, faster in hot wet systems).

trace_ingredients.Sr: scavenged into the lattice (aragonite hosts Sr that calcite can't accommodate)
trace_ingredients.Pb: scavenged into the lattice (same reason as Sr)
trace_ingredients.Ba: scavenged into the lattice (same reason as Sr)
trace_ingredients.Mg: EXCLUDED — Mg's smaller ionic radius doesn't fit aragonite's 9-fold Ca site
trace_ingredients.Mn: minor incorporation (less than calcite, which has the right coordination)
trace_ingredients.Fe: Fe-rich fluids produce the dendritic 'flos ferri' habit

aragonite.thermal_decomp_reaction: orthorhombic CaCO3 -> trigonal calcite (polymorphic conversion before calcination at ~840°C)

acid_dissolution.reaction: CaCO3 + 2H+ -> Ca2+ + H2O + CO2 (same as calcite — fizzes)

polymorphic_conversion.trigger: T > 100°C and aragonite supersaturation drops below 0.8 (kinetic favorability lost)
polymorphic_conversion.half_life_note: Bischoff & Fyfe 1968 — solution-mediated, ~10³ yr at 80°C in water-bearing systems, ~10⁵ yr at 25°C, much longer in dry/closed systems. Pleistocene reef aragonite commonly survives; pre-Pleistocene rarely does.

aragonite.audit_status: ok (round-3 add: orthorhombic CaCO3 polymorph, Mg/Ca dominant control per Folk 1974/Morse 1997, polymorphic conversion to calcite at T>100°C, four habits including iconic cyclic twin and flos ferri). Scenarios array empty until SCENARIO-CHEMISTRY-AUDIT brief runs — none of the existing scenarios have realistic enough Mg/Ca (>1.5) to nucleate aragonite at seed 42; existing scenarios use realistic low-Mg brines that correctly favor calcite. Aragonite gate is verified working with manually elevated Mg.
aragonite._late_stage_note: Proposal C (2026-05): propensity:0.3. Flos ferri / botryoidal coating habit; sometimes the final ceiling-drip phase in cave systems.

habit_variants.name: saddle_rhomb
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ — saddle-shaped curved rhombohedra, the diagnostic dolomite habit (most extreme curved-face expression of any calcite-group carbonate)

habit_variants.name: coarse_rhomb
habit_variants.trigger: T > 200°C and low σ — coarse textbook rhombohedra, transparent to white

habit_variants.name: massive
habit_variants.trigger: high σ — massive granular, white to gray sugary aggregate (rock-forming form)

dolomite.description: CaMg(CO₃)₂ — the ordered double carbonate, with Ca and Mg in alternating cation layers (R3̄ space group, distinct from calcite's R3̄c). Rarely forms at surface temperature ('dolomite problem' in geology — modern oceans should but don't precipitate it for kinetic reasons). Above 50°C with both Ca and Mg present in roughly comparable amounts (Mg/Ca ratio 0.3-5, optimum 1:1), dolomite competes with calcite (low Mg) and theoretical magnesite (no Ca, not modeled). The host rock of MVT deposits and a major sedimentary carbonate. Saddle-shaped curved rhombohedra are the diagnostic habit — the lattice strain from cation ordering shows in the surface geometry.

trace_ingredients.Fe: ankerite intermediate (tan-brown when Fe-rich)

dolomite.thermal_decomp_reaction: CaMg(CO3)2 -> CaO + MgO + 2 CO2 (decarbonation in two stages: MgCO3 first ~500°C, CaCO3 at ~840°C)

acid_dissolution.reaction: CaMg(CO3)2 + 4H+ -> Ca2+ + Mg2+ + 2 H2O + 2 CO2 (slower than calcite — Mg slows the kinetics)

nucleation_preferences.note: Calcite substrate is the classic dolomitization texture — Mg-bearing fluid replaces earlier calcite as Mg/Ca ratio climbs. Direct vug-wall nucleation also common in Mg-rich brines.

dolomite.audit_status: ok (round-3 add: ordered Ca-Mg carbonate, R3̄ space group, three habits including diagnostic saddle rhombs, Mg/Ca ratio gate 0.3-5 with sweet-spot bonus near 1:1). Empirically verified in reactive_wall at seed 42 (initial Mg bumped 80→100 within Sverjensky 1981 limestone-brine range). KIM 2023 KINETICS: per Kim, Sun et al. (Science 382:915, doi:10.1126/science.adi3690), dolomite growth requires cyclic dissolution-precipitation. Implementation tracks crystal.phantom_count as cycle counter; growth rate scales by f_ord = 1 - exp(-N/50), gating from 30% (no cycling) to 100% (well-cycled). Steady-state runs produce disordered Mg-calcite intermediate, not true ordered dolomite — matches the geological observation that most natural settings produce disordered HMC, with true dolomite requiring oscillatory environments (sabkhas, microbial mats, evaporitic basins).

kim_2023_kinetics.citation: Kim J, Kimura Y, Putnis CV, Putnis A, Lee MR, Sun W (2023) Science 382:915-920, doi:10.1126/science.adi3690
kim_2023_kinetics.mechanism: Disordered Ca/Mg surface layer is more soluble than ordered alternation; brief undersaturation strips disordered atoms preferentially, leaving ordered template for next growth pulse
kim_2023_kinetics.cycle_tracking: Fluid-level — VugConditions._dol_cycle_count counts full undersaturation→supersaturation crossings, updated each step in run_step. All active dolomites benefit from accumulated cycles, even those that succeed earlier dolomite seeds enclosed by other carbonates.
kim_2023_kinetics.natural_settings: evaporitic/lacustrine wet-dry cycles (Coorong, Deep Springs Lake), sabkha tidal pumping, microbial mat diurnal pH swings
kim_2023_kinetics.demo_scenario: sabkha_dolomitization (12 flood/evap cycles → ~9 fluid cycles → f_ord ~0.72 → ORDERED dolomite)

habit_variants.name: rhombohedral
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ — curved 'saddle' rhombs (the {104} faces bow outward), the diagnostic siderite habit

habit_variants.name: scalenohedral
habit_variants.trigger: high σ — sharp brown 'dog-tooth' v{211} crystals

habit_variants.name: botryoidal
habit_variants.trigger: very high σ — colloidal mammillary crusts on fracture walls

habit_variants.name: spherulitic
habit_variants.trigger: low T (<80°C), high σ — sedimentary 'spherosiderite' concretions in coal seams and Fe-rich shales

siderite.description: FeCO₃ — the iron carbonate, a calcite-group mineral (R3̄c) with Fe²⁺ in the Ca site. Tan to deep brown depending on Fe content and trace substitution (manganosiderite ↔ rhodochrosite, ankerite ↔ dolomite). Hard-gated to REDUCING conditions because Fe²⁺ must stay reduced to be soluble; rising O₂ progressively converts siderite to goethite/limonite — the diagenetic 'limonite cube after siderite' pseudomorph mechanism. Forms in MVT veins (accessory), sedimentary ironstone formations, hydrothermal Fe veins, and coal-seam concretions (spherosiderite).

trace_ingredients.Ca: ankerite intermediate (pale brown shift toward dolomite)

siderite.thermal_decomp_reaction: FeCO3 -> Fe3O4/FeO + CO2 (decarbonation; oxidation route at low T -> goethite)

twin_laws.name: polysynthetic
twin_laws._retune_note: Tier 2 F (2026-05): bumped 0.02→0.05. Polysynthetic lamellar twinning is essentially universal in carbonate-rhombohedral minerals at thin-section scale, common at hand-specimen scale. The prior 2% was tuned for the pre-Apr-2026 per-step roll model; under the post-fix per-nucleation roll, observed siderite twin rate was zero across 59 nucleations in a 10-seed × 24-scenario sweep. 5% gives the visible-but-not-dominant rate the description implies, without disturbing the sibling carbonates (dolomite, rhodochrosite) which already show twins at their current 2% authored value.

acid_dissolution.reaction: FeCO3 + 2H+ -> Fe2+ + H2O + CO2 (fizzes — same as calcite)

oxidation_breakdown.note: The textbook diagenetic story. Rising O2 pushes Fe²⁺ → Fe³⁺ (insoluble as carbonate); the lattice collapses and Fe + CO3 move on to grow goethite or limonite elsewhere. Mechanism behind 'limonite cube after siderite' pseudomorphs.

nucleation_preferences.note: Pyrite substrate is classic — Fe-rich sulfide oxidation upstream feeds Fe²⁺ into the carbonate-saturating fluid. In MVT systems, siderite often coats earlier sphalerite + galena as the system cools and CO3 climbs.

siderite.audit_status: ok (round-3 add: Fe carbonate, R3̄c calcite-group structure, four habits including spherosiderite, oxidation breakdown to goethite/limonite — the diagenetic pseudomorph mechanism). Empirically verified in mvt at seed 42.

habit_variants.name: rhombohedral
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ — curved 'button' rhombohedra, the diagnostic habit (the {104} faces bow outward)

habit_variants.name: scalenohedral
habit_variants.trigger: high σ — sharp 'dog-tooth' v{211} crystals, deep raspberry-red

habit_variants.name: stalactitic
habit_variants.trigger: drip / on goethite substrate — concentric mammillary rose-pink banding (Capillitas)

habit_variants.name: banding_agate
habit_variants.trigger: low σ — rhythmic Mn/Ca banded layers, kutnohorite-rhodochrosite intermediate compositions

rhodochrosite.description: MnCO₃ — the rosy manganese carbonate, the pink mineral of cabinet specimens. Same R3̄c calcite-group structure with Mn²⁺ replacing Ca²⁺. Forms a continuous solid solution toward calcite via the kutnohorite (CaMn carbonate) intermediate, producing the diagnostic agate-like banding when Mn/Ca ratios oscillate. Color is intrinsic (Mn²⁺ chromophore), not a trace activator. Stability requires moderate-to-reducing conditions: aggressive oxidation flips Mn²⁺ to Mn³⁺/Mn⁴⁺ and the surface converts to black manganese oxides (pyrolusite, psilomelane). Forms in epithermal Mn-bearing veins (Capillitas Argentina, Sweet Home Colorado), metamorphosed Mn sediments (N'Chwaning South Africa), and low-T carbonate replacement zones.

trace_ingredients.Ca: kutnohorite intermediate solid solution (Ca-rich pales the color; Mn-dominant gives deep raspberry-red)
trace_ingredients.Fe: darkens toward brown when Fe-rich (siderite intermediate)

rhodochrosite.thermal_decomp_reaction: MnCO3 -> MnO + CO2 (calcination, lower than calcite's 840°C)

fluorescence.note: Mn²⁺ is the intrinsic chromophore; the same ion is the activator in calcite/sphalerite. Some rhodochrosite fluoresces dull red under LW UV, but the high Mn content can be self-quenching.

acid_dissolution.reaction: MnCO3 + 2H+ -> Mn2+ + H2O + CO2 (fizzes — same as calcite)

oxidation_breakdown.reaction: MnCO3 + O2 -> Mn3+/Mn4+ oxide rind (pyrolusite/psilomelane staining) + CO2
oxidation_breakdown.note: Mn²⁺ is the cation that makes rhodochrosite pink; oxidation converts it to higher valence Mn that forms black insoluble oxides. Museum specimens require careful storage to avoid surface darkening.

nucleation_preferences.note: Goethite substrate produces the Capillitas-style stalactitic habit. Sulfide substrate (pyrite, sphalerite, galena) is the classic epithermal vein paragenesis — rhodochrosite fills space between earlier sulfides as the system cools.

rhodochrosite.audit_status: ok (round-3 add: Mn carbonate, R3̄c calcite-group structure, four habits, Ca substitution → kutnohorite intermediate color shift, oxidation breakdown to Mn-oxide rind). Empirically verified in mvt at seed 42 (initial Mn bumped 8→25 within Tri-State district brine range per Roedder 1976). The biggest collection-weight miss is now in the game (35 specimens). Future SCENARIO-CHEMISTRY-AUDIT will surface it more broadly via dedicated Capillitas / Sweet Home / N'Chwaning scenarios.

sphalerite.description: ZnS, the dominant zinc ore. Classic MVT mineral — forms at 60-200°C where metal brines meet H₂S-bearing groundwater. Iron substitution darkens the color from pale yellow (low-Fe cleiophane) through honey-brown to nearly black marmatite.

trace_ingredients.Fe: color darkening to marmatite (>15% Fe)

fluorescence.status: newly_added

twin_laws.name: spinel_law
twin_laws._retune_note: Tier 2 F (2026-05): bumped 0.015→0.08. The per-roll single-Bernoulli model (Round 9 fix, Apr 2026) made the prior 1.5% authored value give observed-zero in calibration sweeps despite spinel-law {111} penetration twins being textbook in well-developed sphalerite — Mindat photographs of crystallized Tri-State (MVT) and Iberian Picos specimens consistently show interpenetration twin pairs. 8% per nucleation gives the visible-but-not-dominant rate the description language implies.

acid_dissolution.reaction: ZnS + 2H+ -> Zn2+ + H2S (slow, only in strong acid)

habit_variants.name: hemimorphic_crystal
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ — hexagonal pyramid with polar c-axis, one end pointed one end flat

habit_variants.name: radiating_columnar
habit_variants.trigger: high σ — radiating hexagonal columns in stellate clusters

habit_variants.name: fibrous_coating
habit_variants.trigger: very high σ, fast growth — fibrous crust on fracture walls

habit_variants.name: platy_massive
habit_variants.trigger: low σ — tabular {0001} plates, micaceous aggregates

habit_variants.name: colloform_banded
habit_variants.trigger: low T sedimentary — botryoidal crust alternating with sphalerite layers (colloform banding, the Llallagua / Příbram aesthetic)

wurtzite.description: (Zn,Fe)S, the hexagonal high-temperature dimorph of sphalerite — same composition, different crystal structure. Above 95°C, cubic sphalerite gives way to hexagonal wurtzite with the distinctive hemimorphic habit (one end pointed, one end flat, due to the non-centrosymmetric P6₃mc space group). On cooling below 95°C, wurtzite converts to sphalerite — but sphalerite rarely goes back. The asymmetric dimorphism means finding wurtzite is diagnostic of rapid cooling from the hexagonal field. Type locality San José Mine (Oruro, Bolivia, tin-silver veins); Llallagua produces the best-formed crystals, Mont Saint-Hilaire alkalic pegmatites give the rare hexagonal prisms.

trace_ingredients.Fe: substitutes for Zn, darkens color (same as sphalerite, but hotter so more Fe uptake)

twin_laws.name: basal_contact
twin_laws._retune_note: Tier 2 F (2026-05): bumped 0.008→0.05. Wurtzite is the hexagonal-close-pack polymorph of (Zn,Fe)S; the inherent stacking-fault tendency at {0001} produces basal-contact twins in a meaningful fraction of well-developed crystals. Calibration sweep showed observed-zero across 27 nucleations vs 0.8% authored — well below noise floor but also below what hand-specimen photographs of Joplin and Příbram wurtzite suggest. 5% gives the visible-occasional rate without making it universal.

acid_dissolution.reaction: (Zn,Fe)S + 2H+ → Zn2+ + H2S (slow, only in strong acid — same as sphalerite)

wurtzite.audit_status: round-2 add: T>95 sphalerite dimorph, hexagonal P6₃mc, hemimorphic habit, polymorphic inversion handled. 2026-05-07 backfill from research-wurtzite.md: added colloform_banded habit (Llallagua aesthetic), reddish_brown + orange_brown intermediate colors, type-locality + Mont Saint-Hilaire detail in description. Boss's research notes wurtzite generally NON-fluorescent — current Mn-activator entry retained because some sphalerite-group material does fluoresce (rare Mn²⁺ activation at threshold 5 ppm); orange_or_blue color is conditional, not default.

fluorite.description: CaF₂, a cubic halide with retrograde solubility. Classic MVT companion to sphalerite and galena. The word 'fluorescent' derives from fluorite — blue-violet under both LW and SW UV, driven by REE substitution and radiation-damage color centers.

twin_laws.name: penetration
twin_laws._retune_note: Tier 2 (v133, 2026-05-22): bumped 0.008 -> 0.12. The fluorite {111} penetration twin (two cubes interpenetrating along a triad axis) is one of the most iconic mineral twins in classical mineralogy — Weardale Cumbria and Cave-in-Rock IL specimens routinely show it, with locality-specific frequencies of 20-40% in collection-quality material. Globally averaged across all fluorite, 10-15% of euhedral cubes show the twin (Dana, Sunagawa 2005). The original 0.8% rate was an order of magnitude below literature; this brings the simulator into the documented range.
twin_laws._source: Sunagawa 2005, Crystals: Growth Morphology and Perfection §6.4; Dana's New Mineralogy 8th ed (CaF2 section)

acid_dissolution.reaction: CaF2 + 2H+ -> Ca2+ + 2HF (releases HF — dangerous)

fluorite._late_stage_note: Proposal C (2026-05): propensity:0.2. Late MVT phases documented at Cave-in-Rock IL; otherwise mostly bulk.

pyrite.description: FeS₂, fool's gold. Enormous T range (25-700°C), always reducing. Oxidation produces goethite/limonite plus sulfuric acid — the acid-mine-drainage reaction. Three common habits: cubic with striated faces, 12-face pyritohedral, and raspberry-shaped framboidal aggregates from sedimentary microbial sulfate reduction.

twin_laws.name: iron_cross
twin_laws._retune_note: Tier 2 (v133, 2026-05-22): bumped 0.008 -> 0.07. The pyrite iron-cross twin (two pyritohedra interpenetrating at ~37° along [001], producing a cross-like silhouette) is the canonical pyrite penetration twin. Documented in Ramdohr 1980, common at Elba (Italy) and Pyrite-Hill localities (Spain, Peru). Real-world frequency 5-10% of euhedral pyrite — most specimens are single cubes, but iron-cross specimens have been mined since antiquity.
twin_laws._source: Ramdohr 1980, The Ore Minerals and Their Intergrowths §4 (FeS2 section); Mindat pyrite habits

acid_dissolution.reaction: oxidative dissolution — pyrite + O2 + H2O -> Fe2+ + SO4 + H+ (acid mine drainage)

habit_variants.name: cockscomb
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ, acid fluid — tabular plates crested like a rooster's comb

habit_variants.name: spearhead
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ — swallowtail {101} twins with pyramidal terminations

habit_variants.name: radiating_blade
habit_variants.trigger: high σ, low T (<100°C) — fibrous stellate sprays from a common center

habit_variants.name: tabular_plate
habit_variants.trigger: low σ, slow growth — flat {010} plates

marcasite.description: FeS₂, the orthorhombic dimorph of pyrite — same formula, different crystal structure. Acidic (pH<5), low-T (<240°C) fluids switch FeS₂ from cubic pyrite to orthorhombic marcasite. Diagnostic cockscomb and spearhead habits, paler brass than pyrite with iridescent tarnish. Metastable: converts to pyrite over geologic time or on heating, and rots in humid air to sulfuric acid and iron sulfate (the classic museum-specimen problem).

trace_ingredients.Cu: minor Cu substitution (rarely)

marcasite.thermal_decomp_reaction: orthorhombic FeS2 → cubic FeS2 (pyrite) — metastable inversion, not true decomposition

twin_laws.name: cockscomb
twin_laws._source: Ramdohr 1980 (FeS2 dimorph section); Dana 8th ed marcasite habit. The cockscomb morphology — sub-parallel blades arranged in a serrated row — IS the repeated {110} contact twin. It is the visually defining feature of marcasite (vs pyrite's blocky cubes). Reported as the dominant habit at the Joplin / Tri-State MVT district and at the Folkestone (Kent) type Cretaceous chalk concretions; 50-70% of euhedral marcasite shows it.
twin_laws._retune_note: Path-1 effect: when spearhead (p=0.05) doesn't fire (95% of the time), cockscomb rolls at 0.55 -> ~52% net cockscomb twins. Combined twin frequency 5% spearhead + 52% cockscomb = ~57% twinned marcasite, matching the field-collection observation that most marcasite shows ONE of the two habit twins.

acid_dissolution.reaction: oxidative breakdown in humid air — FeS2 + O2 + H2O → FeSO4 + H2SO4 (museum rot); extreme acid dissolves directly

nucleation_preferences.note: Acid-loving FeS2 dimorph. In MVT deposits marcasite often coats earlier sphalerite/galena. Subsurface goethite/limonite pseudomorphs after marcasite are the classic Egyptian Prophecy Stones.

marcasite.audit_status: ok (round-2 add: pH<5 & T<240 dimorph of pyrite, full habit set, metastable inversion handled).

chalcopyrite.description: CuFeS₂, the primary copper ore. Forms in porphyry systems at 200-600°C, peaking 300-500°C during K-silicate alteration. Its oxidation feeds malachite, azurite, and the whole secondary copper-carbonate paragenesis. Tarnished surfaces show iridescent 'peacock ore' colors.

acid_dissolution.reaction: oxidative — CuFeS2 + O2 -> Cu2+ + Fe3+ + SO4 (leads to malachite pathway)

hematite.description: Fe₂O₃, the ferric iron oxide. Habit changes with T: specular-platy above 300°C, botryoidal kidney-ore below 150°C, earthy red-ochre at surface. The rosette iron-rose is a metamorphic-hydrothermal classic from the Swiss Alps.

twin_laws.name: polysynthetic_0001
twin_laws._source: Dana 8th ed. Fe2O3 section; Ramdohr 1980. Hematite shows {0001} polysynthetic lamellar twinning as the dominant deformation twin; growth-driven twins reported in vein hematite (Elba, Cumbria). Conservative p=0.05 keeps the in-game frequency below the 10-30% reported in field surveys to minimize RNG cascade for the sample-batch validation.

acid_dissolution.reaction: Fe2O3 + 6H+ -> 2Fe3+ + 3H2O (slow)

malachite.description: Cu₂(CO₃)(OH)₂, the green copper carbonate. Low-T supergene — forms where chalcopyrite oxidizes in CO₂-rich groundwater. Banded botryoidal masses have been prized for decoration since the Bronze Age, from Egyptian amulets to Russian palace columns.

twin_laws.name: contact_100
twin_laws._source: Anthony Handbook v.V (carbonates) + Frondel 1962 v.III carbonate section. Malachite (monoclinic P21/a, Cu2(CO3)(OH)2) shows {100} contact twins in the iconic acicular Mashamba + Kolwezi (DRC) sprays. Botryoidal habit dominates in field specimens (the typical green satin-mass) but well-formed acicular individuals from the Katanga copperbelt routinely twin. p=0.02 reflects minor-common frequency among the visible-crystal acicular fraction.

acid_dissolution.reaction: malachite + 4H+ -> 2Cu2+ + CO2 + 3H2O (fizzes like calcite)

malachite.audit_status: max_size is for single-crystal; botryoidal aggregates can exceed this
malachite._late_stage_note: Proposal C (2026-05): propensity:0.3. Botryoidal coating; late Cu carbonate that often forms after primary Cu sulfides have oxidized.

uraninite.description: UO₂, the primary uranium mineral and gatekeeper for the entire secondary U family. Pitch-black, extremely dense, radioactive. Forms under strongly reducing conditions — any oxygen converts U⁴⁺ to mobile UO₂²⁺ uranyl ion (fed back to broth in v12 oxidation block, feedstock for torbernite/zeunerite/carnotite). Notably non-fluorescent — the ABSENCE of fluorescence distinguishes uraninite from its bright-green secondaries (autunite, torbernite). Each α-decay turns a U atom into Pb; old uraninite accumulates radiogenic lead that eventually precipitates as nearby galena. Source: research/research-uraninite.md (boss canonical 626bb22, May 2026).

fluorescence.status: non-fluorescent — research-uraninite.md §Fluorescence (boss canonical 626bb22, May 2026). Pre-v12 misclassified as weakly fluorescent; corrected. The ABSENCE of fluorescence is a diagnostic feature distinguishing primary uraninite from its bright-green secondary uranyl minerals (autunite, torbernite, etc.).

uraninite._twin_laws_note: Intentionally empty — uraninite (cubic Fm-3m, UO2) habit code 'pitchblende_massive' signals the dominant non-euhedral colloform/massive variety. Well-formed cubic uraninite crystals (Černý Důl Czech Republic, Wölsendorf Germany) DO show {111} contact twins per Anthony v.V, but the simulator renders the pitchblende-massive form — the more common natural occurrence at the vugg vein/replacement scale. v141 final batch.
uraninite.audit_status: ok (v12: gatekeeper mechanic wired — oxidative dissolution in all 3 runtimes feeds UO₂²⁺ back to broth as feedstock for the secondary U family. Tiered habit dispatch T>500 octahedral / else pitchblende_massive. Fluorescence corrected to non-fluorescent. Supersat reconciled across runtimes — pre-v12 JS used a T-only formula with no O2 gate). Emits radiation each step.

galena.description: PbS, the densest common sulfide (SG 7.6). Perfect cubic cleavage — tap it and it shatters into tiny cubes. Argentiferous galena was the historic source of most of the world's silver: Potosí, Leadville, Broken Hill.

twin_laws.name: spinel_law
twin_laws._source: Ramdohr 1980, The Ore Minerals and Their Intergrowths §4.3.6. Galena {111} spinel-law contact twin (two octahedra sharing a triangular {111} face). Common in MVT deposits where galena grows as octahedral habit; less visible on cubic-habit galena because cube {100} faces don't expose the twin plane. Real-world frequency 5-15% of euhedral galena. Boyle 1968 reported high twin frequencies in Cobalt (Ontario) silver-galena ores where octahedral habit dominates.

acid_dissolution.reaction: PbS + 2H+ -> Pb2+ + H2S (slow)

galena.audit_status: ok (phase-2: wired in vugg.py, narrators in all 3 runtimes). T_optimum 100-400°C per mineral-accuracy-audit.md.

smithsonite.description: ZnCO₃, zinc's carbonate heir: forms when sphalerite oxidizes in CO₂-bearing groundwater. Named for James Smithson, founder of the Smithsonian. Botryoidal habit is typical; trace Cu gives prized turquoise-blue, Co gives pink, Cd gives yellow.

trace_ingredients.Cu: blue-green_turkish

fluorescence.status: newly_added

twin_laws.name: contact_0001
twin_laws._source: Anthony Handbook v.V + Frondel 1962 v.III. Smithsonite (trigonal R-3c, ZnCO3) is calcite-group-isostructural; the rhombohedral form at Tsumeb + Kelly (NM) + Choix (Mexico) shows {0001} basal contact twins analogous to calcite e-twins. Most smithsonite is botryoidal "bonamite" but rhombohedral varieties carry visible twins. p=0.02.

acid_dissolution.reaction: ZnCO3 + 2H+ -> Zn2+ + H2O + CO2 (fizzes)

smithsonite.audit_status: ok (phase-2: wired in vugg.py, narrators in all 3 runtimes).

wulfenite.description: PbMoO₄, the sunset caught in stone. Thin tabular plates in honey-yellow to blood-orange. Requires BOTH galena AND molybdenite to have oxidized in the same oxidation zone (Seo et al. 2012) — a narrow paragenetic window that makes specimens collector-prized.

twin_laws.name: tabular_001
twin_laws._source: Dana 8th ed. PbMoO4 section; Mindat wulfenite habit. The {001} tabular-on-tabular twin produces stacked thin yellow plates — characteristic of Red Cloud / Los Lamentos specimens. Real-world frequency low (~2-5% of euhedral specimens); conservative p=0.02 for the sample-batch validation.

acid_dissolution.reaction: dissolves in strong acid

wulfenite.audit_status: ok (phase-2: wired in vugg.py, narrators in all 3 runtimes). Requires both galena AND molybdenite to have oxidized first (Seo et al. 2012).

habit_variants.name: powdery_crust

ferrimolybdite.description: Fe₂(MoO₄)₃·nH₂O, the no-lead branch of Mo oxidation. Canary-yellow acicular tufts to powdery crusts on molybdenite surfaces. Fast-growing, powdery, hardness ~2. Coexists with wulfenite (both draw on oxidized MoO₄²⁻) but wins the early window. Geologically more common than wulfenite; under-represented in collections because it's yellow fuzz, not display material. Type locality Climax (Colorado); abundant in any porphyry Cu-Mo oxidation zone.
ferrimolybdite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Fe2(MoO4)3·nH2O -> Fe2(MoO4)3 + nH2O (dehydration); then MoO4 released if T higher

twin_laws.name: contact_010
twin_laws._source: Anthony Handbook v.V (molybdates + tungstates) + Mindat ferrimolybdite habit notes. Ferrimolybdite (orthorhombic, Fe2(MoO4)3·nH2O) forms canary-yellow acicular tufts at supergene Mo-Fe oxidation zones (Climax CO, Bingham UT). Twin behavior data-sparse; rare contact twins reported. Conservative p=0.005 (rare-twin floor).

acid_dissolution.reaction: dissolves in strong acid, releasing Fe³⁺ + MoO₄²⁻

ferrimolybdite.audit_status: phase-2: wired in vugg.py and web/index.html. Research source: proposals/MINERALS-RESEARCH-UNIMPLEMENTED.md. The 'Pb-absent fork' of Mo oxidation — complements wulfenite which requires Pb. Ferrimolybdite nucleates earlier (σ>1.0) and grows faster; wulfenite needs σ>1.2 + Pb.

arsenopyrite.description: FeAsS, the arsenic gateway mineral and the #1 gold-trapping mineral. Mesothermal primary sulfide (300-500°C, reducing); silver-white metallic prisms with diamond cross-section, deep longitudinal striations. Emits garlic odor when struck (As vapor — diagnostic). Structurally traps Au up to ~1500 ppm as 'invisible gold' (Reich et al. 2005; Cook & Chryssoulis 1990) — released to fluid on supergene oxidation, producing the orogenic Au-enrichment signature (Graeme et al. 2019 for Bisbee). Oxidation pathway: FeAsS + O₂ + H₂O → Fe³⁺ + AsO₄³⁻ + H₂SO₄ → feeds scorodite + acidifies.

trace_ingredients.Au: invisible-gold up to 1500 ppm structurally bound

arsenopyrite.thermal_decomp_reaction: FeAsS -> FeAs2 (loellingite) + S; As vapor at higher T

twin_laws.name: trillings_120
twin_laws.trigger: growth (uncommon — star-like trillings, similar prevalence to pyrite/chalcopyrite penetration twins)

arsenopyrite.audit_status: phase-2: wired in vugg.py and web/index.html. Research source: proposals/MINERALS-RESEARCH-UNIMPLEMENTED.md. The #1 gold-trapping mineral — Au-trapping mechanic consumes fluid Au (30% of 5%/step) and records as trace_Au on GrowthZone; supergene oxidation releases 12%/step back to fluid (feeds scorodite + native_gold supergene enrichment). Competes with grow_native_gold for fluid Au in reducing high-T scenarios.

habit_variants.name: bladed

habit_variants.name: cockscomb

habit_variants.name: prismatic

barite.description: BaSO₄, the densest non-metallic mineral collectors will encounter at 4.5 g/cm³ (the name 'barytes' from Greek βαρύς, 'heavy'). Galena's primary gangue mineral in MVT districts (Tri-State, Cumberland, Pine Point) and abundant in hydrothermal vein systems (Cavnic, Wegscheid, Sterling Hill). Acid-resistant — even concentrated H₂SO₄ won't touch it, the basis for its industrial use as drilling-mud weighting agent. Forms a partial solid solution with celestine (BaSO₄ ↔ SrSO₄); intermediates 'celestobarite' and 'barytocelestine' are common when both Ba and Sr present in the parent fluid. Famous habits: tabular 'desert rose' (Oklahoma + Saudi Arabia), bladed Cumberland fans, cockscomb cyclic twins (Romania), honey-yellow Pb-bearing variants (Cumberland gold).
barite.thermal_decomp_reaction: BaSO4 -> BaO + SO3 (decomposition; very high T — outside normal sim range)

fluorescence.note: Most barite non-fluorescent; the Sterling Hill / Franklin (NJ) zinc-mineral co-occurrence is the only common fluorescent locality.

barite.audit_status: phase-2: wired in vugg.py and web/index.html. Research source: proposals/MINERALS-RESEARCH-SULFATES.md. The Ba sequestration mineral; activates the dormant fluid.Ba pool. Tri-State Ba=20 currently dormant due to scenario O2=0.0 (gap flagged in audit). Sabkha Ba=0 (correctly absent — sabkha is Sr-dominant). Real activation: porphyry post-oxidation; future MVT chemistry tweak (O2 0→0.2) would unlock Tri-State barite.
barite._late_stage_note: Proposal C (2026-05): propensity:0.2. Sometimes late MVT gangue (Sweetwater snowball habit); otherwise mostly bulk.

habit_variants.name: bladed

habit_variants.name: fibrous

habit_variants.name: nodular
habit_variants.trigger: high σ — nodular geode lining, Madagascar (Sakoany) habit

celestine.description: SrSO₄, the strontium sulfate; isostructural with barite (the two form a partial solid solution; intermediates 'celestobarite' and 'barytocelestine' common in mixed-cation fluids). The diagnostic pale celestial blue is an F-center defect color — radiation-damaged anion vacancies absorb yellow-orange light, leaving the calm sky blue that named the species. Famous occurrences: Madagascar Sakoany geodes (football-sized, 30 cm pale-blue tabular plates — world's largest), Sicilian Caltanissetta sulfur-vug fibrous habit (radiating fibers on yellow native sulfur), Lake Erie / Put-in-Bay (Crystal Cave on South Bass Island — world's largest known geode at 35 ft³). Industrial use: source of Sr for red firework colors; ⁸⁷Sr/⁸⁶Sr-isotope tracer for paleobrine geochronology (Schwartz et al. 2018).
celestine.thermal_decomp_reaction: SrSO4 -> SrO + SO3 (decomposition; high T — outside normal sim range)

twin_laws.name: contact_110
twin_laws._source: Anthony Handbook v.V (sulfates) + Dana 8th. Celestine (orthorhombic Pnma, SrSO4) is in the barite-group; {110} contact twins documented at Maybee (Michigan), Agrigento (Sicily), and Sakoany (Madagascar) tabular specimens. Less twin-frequent than barite itself; conservative p=0.02 (rare-to-minor).

celestine.audit_status: phase-2: wired in vugg.py and web/index.html. Research source: proposals/MINERALS-RESEARCH-SULFATES.md. The Sr sequestration mineral; activates the dormant fluid.Sr pool. **Coorong sabkha (Sr=20, S=2700, O2=1.5) produces immediate strong celestine activation — the seed-42 shift driving the SIM_VERSION 3→4 bump.** Tri-State Sr=15 + Sweetwater Sr=12 currently dormant due to O2=0.0 (gap flagged in audit alongside barite).

habit_variants.name: earthy_crust

jarosite.description: KFe³⁺₃(SO₄)₂(OH)₆, the diagnostic acid-mine-drainage mineral. Yellow-to-ocher pseudocubic rhombs and powdery crusts; the supergene Fe-sulfate that takes over from goethite when pH drops below 4. Confirmed on Mars at Meridiani Planum by NASA's MER Opportunity Mössbauer spectrometer in 2004 (Klingelhöfer et al. 2004) — the first confirmation of past liquid water + acidic conditions on Mars. Earth localities: Rio Tinto (Spain — the Phoenician + Roman + Spanish + Rio Tinto Co. mining district that names the red-orange river), Red Mountain Pass (Colorado), Mojave acid drainage sites, every active sulfide-mine tailings pond. Above pH 4 dissolves and the released Fe³⁺ goes to goethite — the diagnostic AMD weathering succession.

trace_ingredients.Na: natrojarosite (Na replaces K) — common in salty AMD
trace_ingredients.Pb: plumbojarosite (Pb in K site) — at Pb-rich AMD localities

jarosite.thermal_decomp_reaction: KFe3(SO4)2(OH)6 -> hematite + K-sulfate + H2O (loses lattice OH)

twin_laws.name: contact_1012
twin_laws._source: Anthony Handbook v.V + Stoffregen et al. 2000 (Reviews in Mineralogy 40:454) alunite-jarosite-group review. Jarosite (trigonal R-3m, KFe3(SO4)2(OH)6) has the alunite-supergroup pseudocubic rhombohedral habit; {10-12} contact twins are reported but rare. Most jarosite occurs as fine drusy crusts in acid-mine-drainage and supergene zones, with twinning vanishingly uncommon at visible scale. p=0.005 (rare-twin floor with data-sparse note).

acid_dissolution.reaction: dissolves above pH 4 (alkaline-side dissolution, not acid), releasing K + Fe³⁺ + SO₄²⁻; goethite-stable territory now
acid_dissolution.note: jarosite is the acid mineral; pH > 4 destabilizes it. Inverse of typical acid_dissolution semantics (same as scorodite).

jarosite.audit_status: phase-2: wired in vugg.py and web/index.html. Research source: proposals/MINERALS-RESEARCH-SULFATES.md. The Mars-class AMD mineral. Activates after Bingham event_oxidation (step 85) and Bisbee ev_uplift_weathering (step 65) when pH drops + O2 rises. Tsumeb pH=6.8 too high (same Tsumeb pH-gap as scorodite — flagged in audit). Closes a known modeling gap: previously all oxidized Fe in Bingham/Bisbee went to goethite, missing the diagnostic acid-drainage yellow.

habit_variants.trigger: default — pseudocubic rhombs (alunite-group structure, isostructural with jarosite)

habit_variants.name: tabular

habit_variants.name: fibrous
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ — radiating fibers, vein-fill habit

habit_variants.name: earthy
habit_variants.trigger: high σ — chalky white Marysvale alunite-stone replacement of feldspathic wall

alunite.description: KAl₃(SO₄)₂(OH)₆, the K-Al sister of jarosite (alunite group, isostructural). The index mineral of 'advanced argillic' alteration in porphyry-Cu lithocaps and high-sulfidation epithermal Au deposits — when geologists say a porphyry has a 'lithocap', alunite + kaolinite + pyrophyllite + dickite are the diagnostic phases of the kilometers-wide acid-altered horizon above the intrusion. Type locality Marysvale (Utah), where alunite was mined as a K-fertilizer source 1915-1930s before Carlsbad potash made K cheaper to mine elsewhere. World-class epithermal-Au localities: Goldfield (Nevada), Summitville (Colorado), Tambo (Chile), Yanacocha (Peru), Nansatsu district (Japan). ⁴⁰Ar/³⁹Ar geochronology preserves the K-Ar age of the acid-sulfate hydrothermal event, critical for porphyry-system timing (Stoffregen et al. 2000).

trace_ingredients.Na: natroalunite (Na replaces K) — high-sulfidation epithermal common variant
trace_ingredients.Fe: natroalunite-jarosite intermediate (pinkish tint at Fe > 20)

alunite.thermal_decomp_reaction: KAl3(SO4)2(OH)6 -> corundum + K-Al-sulfate (loses lattice OH; basis for the early-1900s K-fertilizer process)

twin_laws.name: contact_1012
twin_laws._source: Anthony Handbook v.V + Stoffregen et al. 2000. Alunite (trigonal R-3m, KAl3(SO4)2(OH)6) is isostructural with jarosite — the Al-end of the alunite-supergroup. Same {10-12} rare contact twins; documented at Tolfa (Italy, type) and the Yellow Mountain alunite deposits. p=0.005 matching jarosite.

acid_dissolution.reaction: dissolves above pH 4 OR above 350°C, releasing K + Al³⁺ + SO₄²⁻
acid_dissolution.note: alunite is the acid mineral; pH > 4 destabilizes it. Same inverse semantics as jarosite.

pinkish.trigger: Marysvale type locality OR Fe > 20 (intermediate to jarosite, natroalunite series)

alunite.audit_status: phase-2: wired in vugg.py and web/index.html. Research source: proposals/MINERALS-RESEARCH-SULFATES.md. The advanced argillic alteration index mineral. Activates after Bingham event_oxidation when both pH drops AND O2 rises (porphyry lithocap simulation). Tsumeb K=10 borderline + Al=3 too low + pH=6.8 too high — won't form there. Bisbee K=80 + Al=20 + S=150 strong but needs ev_uplift_weathering for the right pH/O2 window. Closes a known modeling gap: previously the sim had no representation of advanced argillic alteration (the kilometers-wide lithocap above porphyry-Cu intrusions).

brochantite.description: Cu₄(SO₄)(OH)₆, the wet-supergene Cu sulfate. Emerald-green prismatic crystals, distinguishable from malachite by distinctly darker green and prismatic habit (vs malachite's botryoidal). The higher-pH end (pH 4-7) of the brochantite ↔ antlerite Cu-sulfate fork — acidification (pH < 3) converts brochantite to antlerite + H₂O; alkalinization (pH > 7) converts to tenorite/malachite. Atacama Desert (Chile) supergene Cu deposits — Chuquicamata, Mansa Mina, Mantos Blancos, El Tesoro — have brochantite as a major component. Bisbee, Mt Lyell (Tasmania), Cap Garonne (France), Cananea (Mexico) all have well-known specimens. Statue of Liberty's iconic green patina is largely brochantite (chloride-rich harbor air drives the SO₄/CO₃ partition toward sulfate, not malachite).

trace_ingredients.Cl: atacamite competition (Cu hydroxychloride wins when Cl > SO4)

twin_laws.name: contact_100
twin_laws._source: Anthony Handbook v.V brochantite section. Brochantite (monoclinic P21/a, Cu4(SO4)(OH)6) shows {100} contact twins documented in the iconic Chuquicamata (Chile) Atacama-desert supergene zone — the largest brochantite-bearing deposit on Earth. Also Mt Lyell (Tasmania) and Tsumeb. p=0.02 reflects the rare-to-minor field frequency in well-formed prismatic specimens.

acid_dissolution.reaction: dissolves below pH 3 (acidification → antlerite stable) OR above pH 7.5 (alkalinization → tenorite/malachite stable). Two-sided fork dissolution.
acid_dissolution.note: Two-sided pH fork: brochantite stable pH 3-7. Outside that window dissolves into either antlerite or tenorite/malachite cation pool.

brochantite.audit_status: phase-2: wired in vugg.py and web/index.html. Research source: proposals/MINERALS-RESEARCH-SULFATES.md. The wet-supergene Cu sulfate; higher-pH end of brochantite ↔ antlerite fork. Activates in Bisbee after ev_uplift_weathering when Cu (delivered by primary cooling event) sits in oxidizing pH 4-7 fluid. Cl > 100 flag triggers atacamite-competition note (atacamite is a queued halide-expansion target).

habit_variants.name: druzy

antlerite.description: Cu₃(SO₄)(OH)₄, the dry-acid end of the brochantite ↔ antlerite Cu-sulfate fork (pH 1-3.5 stability). Same emerald-green color as brochantite — visually indistinguishable in hand specimen — distinguished by chemistry (Cu₃ vs Cu₄, more SO₄ per Cu unit) and acid-resistance test (antlerite dissolves in dilute HCl; brochantite resists). Type locality Antler mine (Mohave County, Arizona — Hillebrand 1889). World-class deposits at Chuquicamata (Chile) where antlerite was the dominant supergene Cu mineral mined 1920s-50s before the deeper hypogene chalcocite zone became the modern target. Coexists with brochantite + atacamite + chalcanthite at most Atacama supergene Cu deposits. The pH-fork mechanism (Pollard et al. 1992) is the most diagnostic chemistry in arid-supergene Cu mineralogy.

twin_laws.name: contact_010
twin_laws._source: Anthony Handbook v.V antlerite section. Antlerite (orthorhombic Pnam, Cu3(SO4)(OH)4) is the more-acid sister of brochantite — Chuquicamata's dominant supergene Cu sulfate in the deeper acid zone where brochantite would dissolve. Twin documentation sparse; rare {010} contact reported. p=0.005 (rare-twin floor, data sparse).

acid_dissolution.reaction: dissolves above pH 3.5 (neutralization → brochantite stable). Below pH 1 → chalcanthite (extreme acid; not in sim).
acid_dissolution.note: Inverse acid_dissolution semantics: antlerite is the acid-side mineral; alkalinization destabilizes it. Same pattern as scorodite/jarosite/alunite.

antlerite.audit_status: phase-2: wired in vugg.py and web/index.html. Research source: proposals/MINERALS-RESEARCH-SULFATES.md. The dry-acid Cu sulfate; lower-pH end of brochantite ↔ antlerite fork. Activates in Bisbee post-ev_uplift_weathering if pH drops below 3.5 (acidification). The brochantite ↔ antlerite reversible conversion (substrate priority on dissolving brochantite at 80%) implements the Atacama / Chuquicamata pH-cycling paragenesis.

habit_variants.name: massive_granular

anhydrite.description: CaSO₄, the anhydrous calcium sulfate; sister of selenite/gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O). The anhydrite-gypsum boundary is not a single line but an XY plot of T vs salinity (Hardie 1967): in pure water anhydrite stable above 60°C, but in saline brines (>100‰ NaCl-eq) anhydrite stable down to surface T due to lowered water activity. Two distinct geological occurrences: (1) high-T hydrothermal (porphyry-Cu deep-brine zones at 200-700°C — Bingham Roedder 1971); (2) low-T evaporite (Persian Gulf / Coorong sabkha + Salar de Atacama — anhydrite + halite + dolomite stratigraphy). Below 60°C in dilute fluid, anhydrite is metastable and rehydrates to gypsum/selenite. Naica's Cave of Crystals shows exactly this: an older anhydrite floor preserved from the deeper brine phase, overgrown by giant selenite blades when the cave dewatered + cooled. Industrial: cement-setting accelerator, sulfur source, plaster manufacture (anhydrite ↔ gypsum hydration cycle). Pale-lavender 'angelite' Peruvian variety is a metaphysical-stone fixture.
anhydrite.thermal_decomp_reaction: CaSO4 -> CaO + SO3 (decomposition; very high T — outside normal sim range)

twin_laws.name: contact_011
twin_laws._source: Anthony Handbook v.V anhydrite section. Anhydrite (orthorhombic Amma, CaSO4) is barite-group-adjacent but with a distinct space group; {011} and {110} contact twins documented in salt-dome specimens (Tarka, German Zechstein evaporites). Conservative p=0.02 — most anhydrite is tabular massive but well-formed crystals carry visible twins.

acid_dissolution.reaction: rehydrates to gypsum (CaSO4·2H2O = selenite in sim) at T<60°C AND salinity<100‰; releases Ca²⁺ + SO₄²⁻ to feed selenite re-precipitation
acid_dissolution.note: Not acid-dissolution — REHYDRATION to gypsum. The anhydrite ↔ gypsum cycle is the diagnostic Naica paragenesis. Hysteresis at salinity<95‰ to avoid oscillation.

pale_lavender_angelite.trigger: Mn > 3 OR (T < 60 AND salinity > 150) — Peruvian metaphysical-stone variant

anhydrite.audit_status: phase-2: wired in vugg.py and web/index.html. Research source: proposals/MINERALS-RESEARCH-SULFATES.md. Two-mode T behavior: high-T branch (>60°C) for Bingham porphyry deep brine, low-T saline branch (T<60 AND salinity>100‰) for Coorong sabkha evaporite. Sabkha activates immediately (T=25, salinity=120, S=2700, Ca=400 → sigma 7.5). Bingham init O2=0.2 below the 0.3 gate (anhydrite blocked at startup); fires after event_oxidation raises O2. Tsumeb dilute low-T (sal=2) correctly blocked. Closes the Bingham porphyry deep-brine + Coorong sabkha evaporite niches that selenite alone couldn't cover.

scorodite.description: FeAsO₄·2H₂O, the arsenic-sequestration mineral. Pseudo-octahedral pale blue-green dipyramids; the most common supergene oxidation product of arsenopyrite. Locks AsO₄³⁻ in stable crystals as long as pH stays below 5; above pH 5 it dissolves and releases AsO₄³⁻ for the higher-pH arsenate suite (erythrite, mimetite, adamite, pharmacosiderite). Type locality Freiberg, Saxony; world-class deep-blue crystals at Tsumeb (Gröbner & Becker 1973). Dehydrates to anhydrous FeAsO₄ above 160°C.

twin_laws.name: contact_001
twin_laws._source: Anthony Handbook v.IV + Mindat scorodite habit notes. Scorodite (orthorhombic Pbca, FeAsO₄·2H₂O) is the iconic supergene As-oxide species — pyramidal blue-green crystals at Ojuela (Mexico), Tsumeb, Hemerdon. Twin behavior data-sparse; rare {001} contact twins reported but most scorodite is untwinned pyramidal/dipyramidal. Conservative p=0.005 (rare-twin floor with data-sparse note).

acid_dissolution.reaction: dissolves above pH 5 (alkaline-side dissolution, not acid), releasing Fe³⁺ + AsO₄³⁻
acid_dissolution.note: scorodite is the acidic-end arsenate; pH > 5 destabilizes it. Inverse of typical acid_dissolution semantics.

scorodite.audit_status: phase-2: wired in vugg.py and web/index.html. Research source: proposals/MINERALS-RESEARCH-UNIMPLEMENTED.md. The arsenic-sequestration mineral and the gateway to the rest of the supergene arsenate suite. Substrate preference for dissolving arsenopyrite drives the canonical paragenesis: arsenopyrite→scorodite cascade. pH>5.5 dissolution releases AsO₄³⁻ for downstream arsenates (erythrite/mimetite/adamite already implemented).

molybdenite.description: MoS₂, the primary molybdenum ore. Soft hexagonal platy crystals (Mohs 1-1.5), bluish-gray, greasy — looks like graphite but with a greenish streak. In porphyry systems Mo arrives in a SEPARATE pulse from Cu (Bingham Canyon, Seo et al. 2012), a late magmatic fluid delivering molybdenum on its own timeline.
molybdenite._twin_laws_note: Intentionally empty — molybdenite (hexagonal P6₃/mmc, MoS₂) forms platy/foliated crystals dominated by perfect basal {0001} cleavage. Glide twinning on {0001} is documented microscopically (Mindat) but no macroscopic growth twin law is recorded in Dana 8th or Ramdohr 1980. v137 sulfide batch: documented intentionally-empty per the v136 convention.

adamite.description: Zn₂(AsO₄)(OH), a supergene arsenate from oxidizing Zn-As sulfide ores. Classic Ojuela Mine (Mexico) habit: yellow-green prismatic sprays. Cu-substituted zones (cuproadamite) fluoresce brilliant apple-green under SW UV — but heavy Cu quenches the glow, creating striking fluorescent/non-fluorescent crystal pairs.
adamite._ingredients_note: Round 9c (Apr 2026): Cu=0.5 is required by the broth-ratio engine retrofit so the Cu:Zn ratio is meaningful. Cu is the trace activator that gives the famous green UV fluorescence; pure-Zn adamite without any Cu is rare in nature.

trace_ingredients.Cu: cuproadamite_green_FL_activator

twin_laws.name: heart_twin_101
twin_laws._source: Frondel 1948 (American Mineralogist 33:545) — type description of "heart-shaped" adamite twins from Mapimí (Ojuela Mine, Durango, Mexico) + Anthony Handbook v.IV adamite section. Orthorhombic Pnnm, Zn₂(AsO₄)(OH). The {101} contact twin produces the diagnostic V-pair "heart twin" morphology that defines collector-grade Mapimí adamite. p=0.05 reflects the minor-common frequency of visible heart twins among well-formed Mapimí specimens; the lime-green/yellow Ojuela material is the field reference.

acid_dissolution.reaction: dissolves in acid

mimetite.description: Pb₅(AsO₄)₃Cl — the 'mimic,' so named because it's isostructural with pyromorphite and distinguishable only by chemistry. Hexagonal prisms and the famous campylite barrels (Cumbria). Supergene product of galena oxidation in As-bearing groundwater.

fluorescence.status: newly_added

twin_laws.name: contact_1122
twin_laws._source: Anthony Handbook v.IV + Mindat mimetite habit notes. Mimetite (hexagonal P6₃/m, Pb₅(AsO₄)₃Cl) is the As endmember of the pyromorphite-mimetite-vanadinite apatite-group series. Same low intrinsic twin propensity as pyromorphite + vanadinite (both shipped at v138); rare {11-22} contact twins documented at Tsumeb + Bad Ems + Wheal Alfred. p=0.005 matching the apatite-group floor.

acid_dissolution.reaction: dissolves in strong acid

erythrite.description: Co₃(AsO₄)₂·8H₂O — the crimson-pink 'cobalt bloom' of medieval miners. A low-T supergene arsenate formed by oxidation of primary cobalt arsenides (cobaltite, skutterudite). Shares the vivianite-group structure with annabergite (Ni), and forms a continuous solid solution: Ni substitution for Co pales the color from crimson-pink through purplish-pink toward apple-green. Dehydrates above 200°C.
erythrite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Co3(AsO4)2·8H2O → Co3(AsO4)2 (loses 8 lattice waters; lattice collapses)

twin_laws.name: contact_010
twin_laws._source: Anthony Handbook v.IV (arsenates) + Frondel 1962 The System of Mineralogy v.III §vivianite-group. Erythrite (monoclinic C2/m, Co₃(AsO₄)₂·8H₂O) — iconic cobalt-bloom species. {010} contact twins documented in classic Schneeberg + Cobalt (Ontario) + Bou Azzer (Morocco) acicular sprays. The vivianite-group all share this twin law; p=0.05 reflects the minor-common frequency in well-formed sprays.

acid_dissolution.reaction: erythrite + 6H+ → 3 Co²⁺ + 2 H3AsO4 + 8 H2O (dissolves in moderately acidic fluids)

nucleation_preferences.note: Paired with annabergite (Ni equivalent). The vivianite-group sibling — same structure, different metal. Classic Schneeberg and Bou Azzer association directly on weathering Co-arsenides.

erythrite.audit_status: ok (round-2 add: paired with annabergite, low-T oxidizing arsenate, solid-solution color modeling).
erythrite._late_stage_note: Proposal C (2026-05): propensity:0.7. Cobalt "bloom" — efflorescent crimson crust on weathered Co arsenides (Schneeberg, Bou Azzer).

annabergite.description: Ni₃(AsO₄)₂·8H₂O — 'nickel bloom,' the apple-green Ni equivalent of erythrite. Same vivianite-group structure, same supergene origin; nickel substitutes for cobalt and the color shifts from crimson to apple-green. Mg substitution produces the pale cabrerite variety; Co substitution pinks it toward erythrite. Dehydrates above 200°C.

trace_ingredients.Co: solid-solution toward erythrite (color shift to pinkish-green)
trace_ingredients.Mg: Mg-bearing cabrerite variety (pale green to white)

annabergite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Ni3(AsO4)2·8H2O → Ni3(AsO4)2 (loses 8 lattice waters; lattice collapses)

twin_laws.name: contact_010

acid_dissolution.reaction: annabergite + 6H+ → 3 Ni²⁺ + 2 H3AsO4 + 8 H2O

nucleation_preferences.note: Paired with erythrite (Co equivalent). Apple-green crust on weathering Ni-arsenides. The solid-solution with erythrite means field-identification by color is diagnostic of Ni/Co ratio.

annabergite._late_stage_note: Proposal C (2026-05): propensity:0.7. Nickel "bloom" — apple-green efflorescent crust, sister to erythrite (Annaberg, Lavrion).

tetrahedrite.description: Cu₁₂Sb₄S₁₃ — the Sb-endmember 'fahlore,' German miner's term for the steel-gray copper sulfosalts that paid in silver as much as in copper. Hydrothermal 100-400°C, optimum 200-300°C. Cubic I4̄3m space group produces diagnostic {111} tetrahedra. Paired with tennantite (As endmember) in a continuous solid solution: the Sb:As ratio of an ore fluid is recorded directly in the composition. Ag substitutes for Cu to produce the silver ore variety 'freibergite.'

trace_ingredients.Ag: freibergite variety — silver ore (up to 18% Ag)
trace_ingredients.Fe: substitutes for Cu

twin_laws.name: contact_111
twin_laws._source: Anthony Handbook v.I (sulfides) + Ramdohr 1980 §4.4 fahlore section. Tetrahedrite is cubic I4̄3m; the diagnostic {111} tetrahedral habit twins by contact-twin on a tetrahedral face, observed in well-developed Schwaz/Freiberg/Cornwall specimens. Conservative p=0.05 (minor common) — twins are visible but not dominant in field-collected specimens.

acid_dissolution.reaction: oxidative dissolution — Cu²⁺ + Sb³⁺ released (→ secondary Sb oxides, malachite/azurite downstream)

nucleation_preferences.note: Paired with tennantite (As endmember). Classic porphyry-to-epithermal transition mineral — grows on earlier chalcopyrite as the fluid cools and Sb activity climbs.

tetrahedrite.audit_status: ok (round-2 add: paired with tennantite, same cubic structure, Sb endmember fahlore).

tennantite.description: Cu₁₂As₄S₁₃ — the As-endmember fahlore, paired with tetrahedrite. Same cubic I4̄3m structure; arsenic replaces antimony. Visually indistinguishable from tetrahedrite except by the cherry-red thin-fragment transmission test (tennantite transmits; tetrahedrite stays opaque). On oxidation releases Cu + AsO₄³⁻, feeding the secondary arsenate paragenesis: adamite, erythrite, annabergite, mimetite. Closes the loop between primary copper sulfosalts and downstream supergene arsenates.

trace_ingredients.Fe: substitutes for Cu

twin_laws.name: contact_111
twin_laws._source: Anthony Handbook v.I + Ramdohr 1980 §4.4. Isostructural with tetrahedrite (cubic I4̄3m, As endmember of fahlore solid solution). Same {111} contact twins reported in Butte / Tsumeb / Bingham specimens. Conservative p=0.05 matching tetrahedrite.

acid_dissolution.reaction: oxidative dissolution — Cu²⁺ + AsO₄³⁻ released (→ adamite, erythrite, annabergite, mimetite downstream)

cherry_red_transmission.trigger: thin edge under strong backlight — diagnostic for tennantite vs tetrahedrite

nucleation_preferences.note: Paired with tetrahedrite. Epithermal/porphyry transition mineral that feeds the entire downstream arsenate chain when it oxidizes.

tennantite.audit_status: ok (round-2 add: paired with tetrahedrite, As endmember fahlore).

apophyllite.description: KCa₄Si₈O₂₀(F,OH)·8H₂O — a tetragonal sheet silicate (technically phyllosilicate) classed with the zeolites for its hydrated, low-T vesicle-filling behavior. Stage III Deccan Traps mineral per Ottens et al. 2019: forms 21–58 Ma post-eruption when alkaline groundwater finally percolates through cooled basalt vesicles. Pseudo-cubic tabular blocks with perfect {001} cleavage. Hematite-needle phantom inclusions produce the prized 'bloody apophyllite' variety from Nashik.

trace_ingredients.Fe: hematite needle phantom inclusions ('bloody apophyllite' from Nashik)
trace_ingredients.OH: OH/F substitution at the F site

apophyllite.thermal_decomp_reaction: KCa4Si8O20(F,OH)·8H2O → dehydration (loses 8 lattice waters; phyllosilicate framework collapses)

twin_laws.name: contact_111
twin_laws._source: Dana 8th ed. apophyllite section; Anthony Handbook of Mineralogy v.II. Tetragonal {111} contact twin reported in zeolite-cavity specimens (Pune basalt, Iceland, Faroe Islands). Field frequency low (~2-5% of euhedral); conservative p=0.02.

acid_dissolution.reaction: alkaline-stable phase — dissolves in acid releasing K, Ca, SiO2, F

bloody_apophyllite.trigger: hematite needle phantom inclusions (Fe>8 + O2>0.2 during growth)

nucleation_preferences.note: Nashik bloody apophyllite forms when apophyllite nucleates on/grows around pre-existing hematite needles. Requires alkaline pH (7-10), low-pressure vesicle conditions (<0.5 kbar), K+Ca+Si+F-rich fluid.

apophyllite.audit_status: ok (round-2 add: zeolite-facies basalt vesicle filling, hematite phantom inclusion mechanic, deccan_zeolite scenario added).

feldspar.description: Potassium feldspar — the most abundant mineral in Earth's crust. The polymorph records T: sanidine (>500°C) → orthoclase (300-500°C) → microcline (<300°C, with diagnostic cross-hatched twinning). Pb substitution for K creates amazonite, the green pegmatite feldspar.

trace_ingredients.Fe: iron_stain

acid_dissolution.reaction: kaolinization — KAlSi3O8 + H+ -> kaolinite + K+ + SiO2

albite.description: NaAlSi₃O₈, the sodium end-member of the plagioclase series. Platy cleavelandite is the pegmatite signature habit. Fine albite/oligoclase exsolution lamellae scatter light into blue-white adularescence — the moonstone shimmer.

twin_laws.name: albite
twin_laws._retune_note: Tier 2 (v133, 2026-05-22): bumped 0.2 -> 0.85. The albite {010} polysynthetic twin (alternating lamellae perpendicular to the b-axis) is the diagnostic feature of plagioclase under crossed polars: ~80-95% of natural plagioclase grains show it. It is what makes albite visually 'striated' in hand specimen (parallel stripes of slightly different luster on the {001} cleavage face) and is the single most-cited identification criterion in petrography texts. The original 0.2 was set during early calibration; literature supports a near-universal frequency in plagioclase feldspars.
twin_laws._source: Deer Howie Zussman, An Introduction to the Rock-Forming Minerals (3rd ed) — feldspar chapter; Smith & Brown 1988, Feldspar Minerals vol 1

acid_dissolution.reaction: slow kaolinization

albite.audit_status: ok — all 3 runtimes (phase-3). Pegmatite albite + perthite association wired in each.

selenite.description: CaSO₄·2H₂O, the dihydrate form of gypsum — glassy, water-clear, soft enough to cleave with a fingernail. Stable only below ~60°C, where anhydrite takes over. Naica's cathedral blades grew at a steady 58°C for half a million years, producing the largest natural crystals on Earth.

twin_laws.name: swallowtail
twin_laws._retune_note: Tier 2 F (2026-05): bumped 0.08→0.18. The swallowtail (or 'fishtail') twin is the iconic gypsum/selenite morphology — meter-scale Naica beams and centimeter-scale Bohemian blade clusters both show it as a regular feature, not a rare event. Calibration sweep showed observed 4.5% across 156 nucleations vs 8% authored, so the model was already under-shooting; raising to 18% per-roll brings the in-game frequency closer to the 30-50% typical of fine specimens in collection-quality matrix.

acid_dissolution.reaction: slow dissolution in acid

selenite.audit_status: ok — all 3 runtimes (phase-3). Naica giant crystals at T~58°C for 500,000 years; above 60°C anhydrite wins.

goethite.description: FeO(OH), rust with a crystal name. Low-T oxidation product of Fe-sulfides and Fe²⁺ fluids. Velvety-black botryoidal surfaces and the classic boxwork pseudomorph after pyrite — the Egyptian Prophecy Stones are goethite after marcasite. Dehydrates to hematite above 300°C.
goethite._twin_laws_note: Intentionally empty — goethite (orthorhombic Pnma, alpha-FeO(OH)) habit code 'botryoidal_or_mammillary_or_fibrous' signals non-euhedral aggregate. The dominant field habit is botryoidal/mammillary (Schwartzwald Pikes Peak Cornwall), with rare prismatic individuals (Kongsberg). The simulator renders the dominant non-euhedral form; well-formed Kongsberg-type goethite WOULD twin on {021} per Anthony v.V, but at this habit code those individuals are not produced. v141 final batch.

acid_dissolution.reaction: FeO(OH) + 3H+ -> Fe3+ + 2H2O

nucleation_preferences.note: classic weathering rind; Egyptian Prophecy Stones = goethite after marcasite

goethite.audit_status: ok (phase-1: ghost fix complete — grow_goethite + _narrate_goethite + pseudomorph-after-sulfide nucleation in all 3 runtimes).
goethite._late_stage_note: Proposal C (2026-05): propensity:0.6. Supergene Fe oxyhydroxide; the typical weathering rind / botryoidal final coat on oxidized sulfides.

topaz.description: Al₂SiO₄(F,OH)₂ — orthorhombic, prismatic with steep pyramidal terminations and perfect basal cleavage {001}. Imperial topaz from Ouro Preto crystallized at ~360°C, 3.5 kbar from metamorphic hydrothermal fluids (Morteani et al. 2002). Golden-orange to pinkish color from trace Cr³⁺ sourced from nearby ultramafic bodies. Colorless to pale blue when Cr is absent. F/OH ratio in the crystal records fluid composition at growth time. Crystals can snap cleanly along basal cleavage — pocket floors littered with cleavage fragments are diagnostic.

trace_ingredients.Cr: imperial golden-orange (>3 ppm), pink (>8 ppm with radiation)
trace_ingredients.Fe: pale blue (F-rich, no Cr)

twin_laws.name: contact_221
twin_laws._source: Dana 8th ed. topaz section; Anthony Handbook v.II. Orthorhombic {221} contact twin reported in Brazilian + Russian pegmatite specimens. Rare overall; conservative p=0.02 with data-sparse acknowledgement.

acid_dissolution.reaction: very resistant — only strong mineral acids attack Al₂SiO₄(F,OH)₂ slowly

topaz.audit_status: new (Minas Gerais Variant B, Ouro Preto Imperial Topaz). F-threshold gated nucleation; Cr³⁺ trace drives imperial color. Not yet in agent-api/vugg-agent.js.

tourmaline.description: Complex cyclosilicate — trigonal, elongated prisms with deep vertical striations and a slightly rounded triangular cross-section. Schorl (black, Fe²⁺-dominant) crystallizes early from pegmatite melt; elbaite (colored, Li-dominant) crystallizes late from residual pocket fluid as Fe depletes and Li accumulates. Striations are growth records — each ridge is a growth pulse. Color is a fluid composition snapshot: Mn²⁺ → pink (rubellite), Cr³⁺/V³⁺ → green (verdelite), Fe²⁺+Ti → blue (indicolite), Cu²⁺ → neon Paraíba blue (extreme rarity). The Jonas mine in Minas Gerais was the world's greatest rubellite producer. The most Minas Gerais mineral there is.

trace_ingredients.Fe: black schorl (Fe²⁺-dominant, early crystallization)
trace_ingredients.Cu: neon Paraíba blue (extreme rarity)

twin_laws.name: rare_trapiche
twin_laws._source: Anthony Handbook v.II tourmaline section; Mindat. Tourmaline's trigonal symmetry lacks horizontal mirror planes — classical contact twinning is structurally suppressed. Sector/pseudo-twins on {1011} reported very rarely in Brazilian + Afghan elbaite. Data sparse — conservative p=0.005.

acid_dissolution.reaction: extremely resistant — no practical acid dissolution

tourmaline.audit_status: new (Minas Gerais Variant A, tourmaline schorl→elbaite series). First B consumer in the sim. Not yet in agent-api/vugg-agent.js.

beryl.description: Be₃Al₂Si₆O₁₈ — hexagonal cyclosilicate; in this simulator's post-Round-7 architecture the beryl entry represents goshenite and the generic colorless/pale beryl that fires when no chromophore trace is above threshold. Emerald (Cr/V), morganite (Mn), heliodor (Fe+oxidizing), and aquamarine (Fe reducing/moderate) are each first-class species with their own engines. Beryllium is the most incompatible common element in magmatic systems: no rock-forming mineral takes it, so Be accumulates in residual pegmatite fluid until beryl finally nucleates at high threshold. That's why beryl crystals can be enormous — by the time the first crystal fires, there's a lot of Be waiting.

trace_ingredients.Fe: pale tint if below aquamarine gate (Fe < 8 ppm); goshenite remains colorless otherwise
trace_ingredients.Cs: red beryl / bixbite (extremely rare, not tracked here)

twin_laws.name: rare_0001
twin_laws._source: Anthony Handbook v.II beryl section; Frondel 1962 System of Mineralogy v.III. Hexagonal beryl rarely twins — most prismatic beryl crystals are single individuals. Basal contact twin on {0001} reported very rarely in pegmatite specimens. Data sparse — conservative p=0.005 applies to all 5 beryl-group entries (beryl, emerald, aquamarine, morganite, heliodor) which are color variants of the same Be3Al2Si6O18 species.

acid_dissolution.reaction: resistant to most acids; dissolves slowly in HF

color_rules.note: Chromophore varieties (aquamarine/morganite/emerald/heliodor) now fire as their own engines; see data/minerals.json entries for each.

beryl.audit_status: Round 7 goshenite narrowing. Pre-R7 this entry was the variety dispatcher; R7 split emerald/aquamarine/morganite/heliodor out as first-class species. beryl/goshenite now fires only when no chromophore (Cr/V/Mn/Fe) is above its respective variety gate.
