habit_variants.trigger: high σ — radiating acicular bundles, the Cornish 'wood-copper' silky habit

habit_variants.name: prismatic
habit_variants.trigger: mid σ — {110} prismatic olive-green crystals, the Cornwall display habit

habit_variants.name: globular
habit_variants.trigger: low σ — botryoidal aggregates, the Tsumeb / Bisbee secondary habit

olivenite.description: Cu₂AsO₄(OH) — orthorhombic Cu arsenate, the Cu end of the olivenite-adamite series. Olive-green to grayish-green (the Cu chromophore — olive in name and color), Mohs 3-4. Forms in Cu-rich supergene oxidation zones; Cornwall is the type locality, with Tsumeb and Bisbee producing showcase modern specimens. Cu/Zn ratio dispatch: when Zn > Cu, adamite (the Zn-end-member of the same arsenate structure) takes priority. Sources: research/research-olivenite.md (boss commit f2939da).
olivenite._ingredients_note: Round 9c (Apr 2026): Zn=0.5 is required by the broth-ratio engine retrofit so the Cu:Zn ratio is meaningful. Real olivenite always has at least trace Zn (zincolivenite-leaning compositions); pure-Cu olivenite is uncommon.

twin_laws.name: contact_101
twin_laws._source: Anthony Handbook v.IV. Olivenite (orthorhombic Pnnm, Cu₂(AsO₄)(OH)) is isostructural with adamite — Cu endmember of the adamite-olivenite series. Same {101} contact twin behavior but less commonly observed than adamite hearts; documented at Cornwall (type) + Tsumeb + Cap Garonne. Conservative p=0.02.

olivenite.audit_status: Round 8d-3 (Apr 2026). Engine + supersaturation + nucleation + narrator complete in vugg.py + web/index.html. Cu/Zn ratio dispatch with adamite (when Zn>Cu → adamite, when Cu≥Zn → olivenite). Adamite engine also updated with the inverse dispatch (returns 0 when Cu>Zn). Cu+As+oxidizing, T 10-50°C peak 20-40. Substrate preference: malachite (the Cu-carbonate companion). Activates with existing Tsumeb supergene_oxidation Cu=55 + As=12 chemistry. Source: boss research file.

habit_variants.name: acicular
habit_variants.trigger: high σ (>1.5) — radiating acicular needle clusters, the Tsumeb display habit

habit_variants.name: botryoidal
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ (0.5-1.5) — reniform/botryoidal mass with radiating fibrous interior, the field-common form

habit_variants.name: drusy_coating
habit_variants.trigger: low σ (<0.5) — thin drusy emerald-green crust on substrate

conichalcite.description: CaCu(AsO₄)(OH) — orthorhombic Ca-Cu arsenate (pseudo-monoclinic via {110} twinning). Vivid emerald to apple-green from Cu²⁺ chromophore in octahedral coordination. Harder (Mohs 4.5) and brighter than its Ca-free cation analog olivenite (Mohs 3, olive-drab) — the Ca-Cu substitution stiffens the structure and shifts the absorption spectrum. Supergene mineral: forms in oxidized Cu-As vein deposits and porphyry copper oxidation zones, particularly where carbonate-buffered groundwater supplies Ca alongside the Cu and As from weathering primary sulfides/arsenides. Cation fork against olivenite — conichalcite when Ca/(Ca+Cu) > 0.4 in the local fluid; Cu-dominant routes to olivenite. The two may coexist in the same vug as a 'two-shade green' assemblage. Mn substitution possible (yellow-greenish cast). Type locality: Hinojosa de Córdoba, Spain (Breithaupt 1849). Famous from Tsumeb, Bisbee, Lavrion, Cap Garonne. Sources: research/research-conichalcite.md (boss canonical 2026-05); Anthony et al. Handbook of Mineralogy Vol. IV.
conichalcite._ingredients_note: v87 (2026-05-19): Ca threshold 15 ppm (loose enough to fire at supergene_oxidation Ca=120 + Tsumeb-style supergene; falls below in pure-Cu porphyry like bisbee at Ca=60 — but the Ca/(Ca+Cu) > 0.4 cation gate is the real selectivity). Cu and As thresholds mirror olivenite's olivenite-relaxed sim-ppm gates (50→10, 10→5) since conichalcite shares the same supergene Cu-As fluid budget but Ca-cation share dilutes the per-element concentrations.

trace_ingredients.Fe: Fe substitutes for Cu, shifts color yellowish-green (transitional toward austinite Ca-Zn-As); >30 ppm flagged in growth note
trace_ingredients.Zn: Zn substitutes for Cu — austinite-end (Ca-Zn-arsenate); minor at low %, but conichalcite stays the dominant species when Cu > Zn
trace_ingredients.P: phosphate may substitute for arsenate (research §Elemental Requirements); transitional toward Ca-Cu-phosphate variant
trace_ingredients.Pb: Pb > 50 ppm suppresses conichalcite by routing the fluid to mimetite/pyromorphite (Pb-arsenate-chloride takes priority)
trace_ingredients.Co: high Co favors erythrite over conichalcite
trace_ingredients.Ni: high Ni favors annabergite over conichalcite

conichalcite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Dehydroxylation begins ~300-400°C; decomposition >500°C to CuO + Ca arsenate + H₂O

fluorescence.quencher: Cu²⁺ — same chromophore-as-quencher pattern that suppresses fluorescence in torbernite/zeunerite/olivenite
fluorescence.status: non-fluorescent under any standard UV wavelength (Cu²⁺ powerful internal quencher)

twin_laws.name: conichalcite_110
twin_laws.trigger: common {110} twinning produces pseudo-monoclinic optical character (research §Crystal System)

acid_dissolution.reaction: CaCu(AsO₄)(OH) + 4H⁺ → Ca²⁺ + Cu²⁺ + H₃AsO₄ + H₂O (readily dissolves in HCl/HNO₃; soluble in dilute mineral acids)

emerald_green.trigger: Cu²⁺ chromophore in octahedral coordination — the species signature

apple_green.trigger: moderate σ — slightly paler emerald, the field-common color

yellow_green.trigger: Fe > 30 ppm — Fe substitution shifts toward austinite color spectrum

pale_green.trigger: Zn > 20 ppm — austinite-end (Ca-Zn-arsenate); transitional toward pale yellow

conichalcite.audit_status: new v87 (2026-05-19). Per research-conichalcite.md (boss canonical 2026-05). Ca-cation analog of olivenite, completing the Ca-Cu supergene arsenate fork. Cation gate Ca/(Ca+Cu) > 0.4 in supersaturation_conichalcite (mirrors autunite-vs-torbernite Round 9d cation fork). Substrate preference: scorodite > olivenite > native_copper > malachite > chrysocolla > wall, per research §Paragenetic Position. Slots into supergene_oxidation (primary target; Tsumeb-style Ca=120 + Cu=55 + As=25 cleanly clears the cation gate) + bisbee (deep gossan after Cu depletion brings the ratio above 0.4) + schneeberg (late Cu-depletion phase when Ca rises). Sources: research-conichalcite.md; Anthony et al. Handbook of Mineralogy Vol. IV; Breithaupt 1849 (type-locality Hinojosa).
conichalcite._late_stage_note: Proposal C (2026-05): propensity:0.5. Supergene Ca-Cu arsenate forms in the late oxidation zone alongside malachite/chrysocolla/azurite. Not as late as efflorescent crusts (chalcanthite 0.7-0.8) but later than primary Cu-As phases.

habit_variants.name: radiating_stellate
habit_variants.trigger: high σ (>1.0) — dense radiating starburst of white needles, the classic Jáchymov/Schneeberg display habit

habit_variants.name: acicular
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ (0.3-1.0) — radiating acicular needle bundles, field-common form

habit_variants.name: efflorescent_crust
habit_variants.trigger: low σ (<0.3) — earthy powdery white crust, the degraded 'drying out' state

pharmacolite.description: CaHAsO₄·2H₂O — monoclinic hydrated calcium hydrogen arsenate. Forms colorless to white delicate radiating/stellate aggregates resembling starbursts of needles — the diagnostic field marker. Vitreous to pearly luster, soft (Mohs 2-2.5). The classic supergene bloom of the Jáchymov (Joachimsthal, Czech) + Schneeberg + Cobalt-Ontario silver-cobalt-arsenic mining districts, where the weathering of cobaltite/nickeline/arsenopyrite primaries releases As⁵⁺ into Ca-rich groundwater. Trace Fe gives yellowish tint, Mn pinkish, Cu greenish — useful provenance fingerprints. Efflorescent: loses water in dry air, converting to haidingerite (CaHAsO₄·H₂O) at ~80-100°C and to anhydrous Ca arsenate at ~150°C, both effectively irreversible. Most field specimens are already partially dehydrated by the time they reach a collection. Highly soluble in dilute HCl/HNO₃; releases toxic arsenic + calcium on dissolution. Type locality: Riegelsdorf, Hesse, Germany (Stromeyer 1819) — but the Jáchymov + Schneeberg displays are the iconic specimens. Sources: research/research-pharmacolite.md (boss canonical 2026-05); Anthony et al. Handbook of Mineralogy Vol. IV; Stromeyer 1819 (type-locality description).
pharmacolite._ingredients_note: v88 (2026-05-19): Ca + As only (the formula is Ca-only, no Cu/Co/Ni/Zn). Cation-share gate Ca/(Ca+Cu+Pb+Zn+Co+Ni) > 0.3 enforces the anti-competitor logic per research §Inhibiting elements — high Cu routes to conichalcite, high Co to erythrite, etc.

trace_ingredients.Fe: Fe substitutes minorly, yields yellowish tint
trace_ingredients.Cu: trace Cu gives greenish cast (>5 ppm rapidly suppresses pharmacolite via fork to conichalcite)
trace_ingredients.P: phosphate may substitute for arsenate, transitional toward Ca-phosphate
trace_ingredients.Co: high Co competes with Ca for arsenate, routes fluid to erythrite
trace_ingredients.Pb: high Pb routes to mimetite (Pb arsenate-chloride preference)

pharmacolite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Pharmacolite (CaHAsO₄·2H₂O) → haidingerite (CaHAsO₄·H₂O) at 80-100°C, loses 1 H₂O; further heating → anhydrous Ca arsenate + H₂O at ~150°C. Both irreversible. In the v88 sim, modeled as crystal-destruction (mineral dissolution releasing structural water) since haidingerite isn't yet in the catalog.

fluorescence.status: generally non-fluorescent under standard UV (SW/MW/LW). Very weak creamy-white response from trace impurities is possible but not from pharmacolite itself. Distinct from autunite-group uranyl arsenates (which fluoresce strongly via U); pharmacolite has no chromophore and no fluorescer in its formula.

twin_laws.name: pharmacolite_100
twin_laws.trigger: rare contact twin per research §Crystal Habit Patterns

acid_dissolution.reaction: CaHAsO₄·2H₂O + 3H⁺ → Ca²⁺ + H₃AsO₄ + 2H₂O (readily dissolves in dilute mineral acids; HCl/HNO₃ specifically called out in research)

white.trigger: colorless to white — the pharmacolite signature; no chromophore in formula

pale_gray.trigger: field specimen with adherent matrix grains

pinkish.trigger: Mn > 2 ppm trace — pink hue from Mn substitution

pale_green_to_white.trigger: Cu trace below the conichalcite fork threshold — slight greenish cast

pharmacolite.audit_status: new v88 (2026-05-19). Per research-pharmacolite.md (boss canonical 2026-05). Ca-only hydrated arsenate; closes the supergene-arsenate Ca-without-Cu slot (paired with conichalcite v87 Ca+Cu and olivenite v8 Cu-only on the same supergene Cu-As fluid). Cation anti-gates suppress (not block) when competitors dominate. Substrate priority: cobaltite > arsenopyrite > native_arsenic > nickeline > erythrite > annabergite > calcite > wall (encodes the five-element-vein paragenetic ordering from research §Paragenetic Position). Slots into schneeberg (primary target — research's Jáchymov/Schneeberg type-display locality; Cu depletes by step 125, Ca rises from carbonate dissolution, As stays elevated from arsenide weathering) + supergene_oxidation (Tsumeb-style Ca-rich oxidation, fires when conichalcite stops scavenging the Cu-bearing window). Thermal dehydration to haidingerite at >80°C modeled as crystal-destruction since haidingerite isn't yet in the catalog. Sources: research-pharmacolite.md; Anthony et al. Handbook of Mineralogy Vol. IV; Stromeyer 1819 (Riegelsdorf type locality).
pharmacolite._late_stage_note: Proposal C (2026-05): propensity:0.6. Late-supergene Ca-As arsenate; forms in the closing phase of the oxidation zone after the primary Co/Ni arsenides have weathered and Ca-rich groundwater has flushed through. Between conichalcite (0.5) and chalcanthite (0.7) on the late-stage scale.

habit_variants.name: prismatic_pseudomorph
habit_variants.trigger: inherited from parent pharmacolite crystal (radiating_stellate or acicular)

habit_variants.name: botryoidal_crust
habit_variants.trigger: inherited from parent pharmacolite crustose form (efflorescent_crust path)

haidingerite.description: CaHAsO₄·H₂O — orthorhombic hydrated calcium arsenate, the half-dehydrated cousin of pharmacolite. Forms EXCLUSIVELY as a transformation product of pharmacolite (CaHAsO₄·2H₂O) — heat above ~80°C OR sustained dry-air exposure drives off 1 of the 2 H₂O molecules, collapsing the lattice from monoclinic pharmacolite to orthorhombic haidingerite. The transformation is irreversible (re-humidifying does not restore the pharmacolite hydrate). External crystal shape is preserved through the in-situ transformation: a haidingerite specimen IS a former pharmacolite crystal, frozen mid-decay. Specific gravity rises from pharmacolite's 2.64-2.73 to haidingerite's 2.85-2.96 — the diagnostic water-loss densification signature. Colorless to white, vitreous luster with pearly cleavage faces on {010}. Mohs 2-2.5 (same as pharmacolite). Sectile tenacity: thin laminae are slightly flexible (unlike pharmacolite which is brittle); a useful field discriminator. Sub-millimeter crystals (Handbook of Mineralogy notes 'to 1 mm'); typically encountered as botryoidal or fibrous coatings on weathered arsenide vein assemblages. Type locality Jáchymov (Joachimsthal), Czech Republic. Named for Wilhelm Karl von Haidinger (1795-1871), Austrian mineralogist. Type pseudomorph occurrence: Getchell mine, Nevada (Handbook of Mineralogy: 'Formed by dehydration of pharmacolite (Getchell mine, Nevada, USA)'). Sources: Palache, Berman, Frondel 1951 (Dana's System of Mineralogy 7th ed v.II 708-709); Ferraris, Jones, Yerkess 1972 (Acta Cryst. 28:209-214, neutron + X-ray structure refinement); Cassien, Herpin, Permingeat 1966 (Bull. Minéral. 89:18-22, crystal structure paper).
haidingerite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Further heating drives off the remaining H₂O → anhydrous CaHAsO₄ at ~150°C; not yet in catalog. v90 sim: haidingerite is the v85-style transformation-only terminal pseudomorph for pharmacolite, no further decomposition modeled.

fluorescence.status: non-fluorescent — same as parent pharmacolite (no chromophore or fluorescer in formula)

twin_laws.name: haidingerite_110
twin_laws.trigger: rare per Handbook of Mineralogy: 'Twinning: Rare on {110}'

acid_dissolution.reaction: CaHAsO₄·H₂O + 3H⁺ → Ca²⁺ + H₃AsO₄ + H₂O (inherits pharmacolite's acid sensitivity; both are uranyl-group-style soft hydrated arsenates that dissolve readily in dilute mineral acids)

white.trigger: colorless to white — same as parent pharmacolite (no chromophore)

colorless.trigger: transparent specimens with no trace impurities

pale_gray.trigger: field specimen with adherent matrix grains

haidingerite._tenacity: sectile (thin laminae slightly flexible) — unlike brittle pharmacolite; a useful field discriminator per Handbook of Mineralogy
haidingerite._specific_gravity: 2.85-2.96 measured (2.96-2.97 calculated) — densified relative to pharmacolite's 2.64-2.73 due to water loss
haidingerite._cleavage: perfect on {010}, pearly luster on cleavage faces
haidingerite._cell_data: orthorhombic Pcnb; a=6.904-6.935 b=16.150-16.161 c=7.935-7.940 Å; Z=8 (per Ferraris/Jones/Yerkess 1972)
haidingerite._optical: biaxial (+); α=1.590 β=1.602 γ=1.638; 2V_meas=58°; weak r>v dispersion
haidingerite.audit_status: new v90 (2026-05-19). Transformation-only paramorph product of pharmacolite via DEHYDRATION_TRANSITIONS (js/75-transitions.ts: pharmacolite → haidingerite, threshold 30 vadose-steps, T_max 80°C heat path). The v88 inline 'thermal destruction at T>80' branch in grow_pharmacolite has been REMOVED — v88 was a stub when haidingerite wasn't in the catalog yet; v90 adds the proper paramorph product so the crystal's external habit + position are preserved through the transformation (radiating stellate pharmacolite → radiating stellate haidingerite-pseudomorph-after-pharmacolite). Research-grade spec from Handbook of Mineralogy (Palache/Berman/Frondel 1951 reissue 2001-2005) + Ferraris/Jones/Yerkess 1972 crystal structure refinement + Cassien/Herpin/Permingeat 1966 structure paper. Type locality Jáchymov; type pseudomorph occurrence Getchell mine Nevada.
haidingerite._late_stage_note: Paramorph after pharmacolite — only ever late by definition (dehydration product); mirrors tincalconite/thenardite/meta-autunite/metatorbernite/metazeunerite/pararealgar pattern. Propensity 1.0.

habit_variants.name: stalactitic
habit_variants.trigger: high σ — sky-blue stalactitic drips, the Chuquicamata mine-wall habit; the dominant natural form

habit_variants.name: tabular
habit_variants.trigger: mid σ + sheltered vug — RARE tabular prismatic blue crystals; most natural specimens are stalactitic or efflorescent

habit_variants.name: efflorescent_crust
habit_variants.trigger: low σ + arid surface — powdery blue surface bloom from rapid evaporation

chalcanthite.description: CuSO₄·5H₂O — the bright sky-blue water-soluble Cu sulfate, the [Cu(H₂O)₅]²⁺ chromophore one of the most intensely-colored minerals. Triclinic (P1̄), Mohs 2.5, perfect {110} cleavage. The most water-soluble mineral in the sim — re-dissolves when fluid salinity drops below 4 OR pH rises above 5 (per-step hook in VugSimulator.run_step). Geologically authentic: every chalcanthite specimen is a temporary victory over entropy. The terminal phase of the Cu-sulfate oxidation cascade (chalcopyrite → bornite → chalcocite → covellite → cuprite → brochantite → antlerite → chalcanthite). Forms in arid strongly-oxidizing very-acidic salt-concentrated drainage: Chuquicamata mine walls (Chile), Rio Tinto AMD seeps (Spain), Atacama desert evaporite crusts. Most 'natural' specimens on the market are lab-grown. Sources: research/research-chalcanthite.md (boss commit f2939da), Bandy 1938 (Am. Mineral. 23).

twin_laws.name: cruciform_010
twin_laws.trigger: growth (rare) — cruciform twin with [001] and [100] of one crystal parallel to [100] and [001] of the other, sharing {010}
twin_laws.description: Rare cruciform cross-shaped twin (twin plane {010}, corrected from earlier {110} placeholder per Mindat handbook entry)

chalcanthite.audit_status: Round 8e (Apr 2026). Engine + supersaturation + nucleation + narrator complete in vugg.py + web/index.html; JSON entry mirrored to docs/. The water-solubility metastability mechanic: per-step hook in VugSimulator.run_step re-dissolves chalcanthite crystals when fluid.salinity < 4 OR fluid.pH > 5. Distinct from THERMAL_DECOMPOSITION (high-T destruction) and PARAMORPH_TRANSITIONS (in-place mineral change) — this is just chemistry, the geological truth that every chalcanthite is a temporary victory over entropy. Habit variants: stalactitic (high σ, the Chuquicamata mine-wall drip habit), tabular (RARE prismatic crystals), efflorescent_crust (low σ powdery bloom). Salinity gate: salinity < 6 → 0 (needs concentrated drainage to overcome ~20 g/100mL solubility). pH gate: pH > 4 → 0 (the most acid-loving Cu sulfate). Source: boss research file research-chalcanthite.md.
chalcanthite._late_stage_note: Proposal C (2026-05): propensity:0.8. Cu sulfate efflorescent crust; classic late-stage bloom on weathered chalcopyrite/bornite (Bisbee, Chuquicamata).

habit_variants.name: botryoidal
habit_variants.trigger: default — velvety blue-green spheres, the diagnostic Mapimi/Tsumeb habit

habit_variants.name: acicular_radiating

rosasite.description: (Cu,Zn)₂(CO₃)(OH)₂ — monoclinic supergene carbonate, the Cu-dominant member of the rosasite-aurichalcite pair. Velvety blue-green botryoidal spheres on weathered Cu-Zn sulfide deposits; the diagnostic habit is sky-blue spherical aggregates on red limonite (Mapimi, Mexico). Forms when chalcopyrite + sphalerite weather together at the supergene front, releasing Cu²⁺ + Zn²⁺ + CO₃²⁻ into shallow groundwater that precipitates the carbonate at near-neutral pH. The Cu:Zn ratio in the parent fluid is the key control: rosasite forms when Cu/(Cu+Zn) > 0.5; aurichalcite forms when the ratio inverts — same broth, different outcome. Mohs 4, blue-green streak, splintery fracture. Type locality: Rosas Mine, Sardinia (1908). Source: Pinch & Wilson 1977 (Tsumeb monograph); research/research-rosasite.md.

trace_ingredients.Ni: nickeloan variant — darker green from Ni substitution for Cu/Zn

acid_dissolution.reaction: (Cu,Zn)₂(CO₃)(OH)₂ + 4H⁺ → Cu²⁺ + Zn²⁺ + CO₂ + 3H₂O (fizzes like calcite)

rosasite.audit_status: Round 9a (Apr 2026). Engine + supersaturation + nucleation + narrator complete in vugg.py + index.html. First mineral with the broth-ratio branching mechanic: supersaturation_rosasite returns 0 when Cu/(Cu+Zn) < 0.5, locking the species to Cu-dominant fluid. Mirror of aurichalcite — same parent fluid, opposite outcome based on ratio. Habit variants: botryoidal (default, the diagnostic Mapimi habit), acicular_radiating (low T + high σ delicate sprays), encrusting (low σ thin crust). Sources: research/research-rosasite.md (boss commit 3bfdf4a); Pinch & Wilson 1977 (Tsumeb monograph).

aurichalcite.description: (Zn,Cu)₅(CO₃)₂(OH)₆ — monoclinic supergene carbonate, the Zn-dominant mirror of rosasite. Delicate pale blue-green tufted divergent sprays — high birefringence makes them shimmer under cross-polarized light. Forms in the same supergene parent fluid as rosasite (Cu + Zn + CO₃ + O₂) but precipitates only when Zn/(Cu+Zn) > 0.5; the two species are typically intergrown, separated only by ratio. Mohs 2 (scratches with a fingernail), light blue streak. Named after Plato's mythical orichalcum — the lost gold-alloy of Atlantis. Type locality: Loktevskoye Mine, Western Siberia (1839); the most aesthetic specimens come from Mapimi, Mexico. Source: research/research-aurichalcite.md (boss commit 3bfdf4a).

twin_laws.name: contact_100
twin_laws._source: Anthony Handbook v.V aurichalcite section. Aurichalcite (orthorhombic Pmcn, (Zn,Cu)5(CO3)2(OH)6) forms pale-blue-green acicular tufts in supergene Zn-Cu zones (Mapimí, Tsumeb, Bisbee). Twin data sparse — most aurichalcite is too acicular + tufted for individual twin observation. Conservative p=0.005 (rare-twin floor with data-sparse note).

acid_dissolution.reaction: (Zn,Cu)₅(CO₃)₂(OH)₆ + 10H⁺ → 5(Zn²⁺,Cu²⁺) + 2CO₂ + 8H₂O (fizzes)

aurichalcite.audit_status: Round 9a (Apr 2026). Mirror of rosasite in the broth-ratio branching pair. Engine + supersaturation + nucleation + narrator complete in vugg.py + index.html. supersaturation_aurichalcite returns 0 when Zn/(Cu+Zn) < 0.5 — same parent fluid as rosasite, opposite outcome based on ratio. Habit variants: tufted_spray (default, the diagnostic delicate Mapimi habit), radiating_columnar (high σ spheres), encrusting (low σ laminar crust). Source: research/research-aurichalcite.md (boss commit 3bfdf4a); Loktevskoye type locality 1839.
aurichalcite._late_stage_note: Proposal C (2026-05): propensity:0.6. Cu-Zn carbonate, typical late-stage acicular crust in oxidation zones (Ojuela, Mapimi).

torbernite.description: Cu(UO₂)₂(PO₄)₂·12H₂O — tetragonal uranyl phosphate, the P-branch of the autunite-group anion-competition trio (with zeunerite for AsO₄³⁻ and carnotite for VO₄³⁻ the other branches). Forms emerald-green to apple-green thin tabular plates that look like green mica; strongly radioactive (U⁶⁺ in the lattice), notably non-fluorescent (Cu²⁺ quenches uranyl emission). Forms at the supergene oxidation front when uraninite weathers to mobile U⁶⁺ and meets phosphate-bearing groundwater alongside Cu²⁺ — torbernite wins the cation race when P/(P+As) > 0.5 in the local fluid. Mohs 2-2.5, dehydrates irreversibly to metatorbernite (8 H₂O) above ~75°C. Type locality: Schneeberg, Saxony (Ore Mountains). Sources: Pinch & Wilson 1977 monographs; research/research-torbernite.md (boss commit 3bfdf4a).

acid_dissolution.reaction: Cu(UO₂)₂(PO₄)₂·12H₂O + 6H⁺ → Cu²⁺ + 2(UO₂)²⁺ + 2H₃PO₄ + 12H₂O (dissolves readily in mineral acids)

torbernite.audit_status: Round 9b (Apr 2026), updated 9c (Apr 2026), updated 9d (May 2026): denominator widened from P+As to P+As+V so V-rich fluid now properly blocks torbernite (carnotite gets it instead). 9d added a symmetric Cu/(Cu+Ca) > 0.5 cation gate to fork against autunite (Ca-cation analog). First mineral with the anion-competition mechanic — three-way fork over the U⁶⁺ cation between PO₄³⁻ (torbernite/autunite), AsO₄³⁻ (zeunerite), and VO₄³⁻ (carnotite). supersaturation_torbernite returns 0 when P/(P+As+V) < 0.5 OR when Cu/(Cu+Ca) < 0.5. Habit variants: tabular_plates (default Schneeberg), micaceous_book (high σ stacks), encrusting (low σ powdery crust). Source: research/research-torbernite.md + research/research-uraninite.md §164-178.

zeunerite.description: Cu(UO₂)₂(AsO₄)₂·(10-16)H₂O — tetragonal uranyl arsenate, the As-branch of the autunite-group anion-competition trio. Visually almost indistinguishable from torbernite — same emerald-green color, same square tabular habit, same micaceous cleavage — distinguishable only by chemistry. The arsenic is the giveaway: zeunerite localities are almost always former mining districts with arsenic-bearing primary ores (arsenopyrite, tennantite). Forms at the supergene oxidation front when uraninite weathers alongside As-rich primary sulfides; zeunerite wins the anion race when As/(P+As) > 0.5. Mohs 2.5; dehydrates irreversibly to metazeunerite (8 H₂O) above ~75°C; non-fluorescent (Cu²⁺ quenches). Type locality: Schneeberg, Saxony (1872). Sources: Pinch & Wilson 1977; research/research-zeunerite.md.

twin_laws.name: contact_001
twin_laws._source: Frondel 1958 + Anthony Handbook v.IV. Zeunerite (Cu(UO₂)₂(AsO₄)₂·12H₂O, tetragonal P4/nmm) is the Cu-As analog of autunite-group; isostructural with torbernite (Cu-P). Same {001} basal contact twin behavior. Documented at Schneeberg/Walpurgis Flacher (As-rich uranium vein zone). p=0.01 matching autunite.

acid_dissolution.reaction: Cu(UO₂)₂(AsO₄)₂·12H₂O + 6H⁺ → Cu²⁺ + 2(UO₂)²⁺ + 2H₃AsO₄ + 12H₂O (dissolves readily)

zeunerite.audit_status: Round 9b (Apr 2026), updated 9c (Apr 2026), updated 9e (May 2026): denominator widened from P+As to P+As+V so V-rich fluid now properly blocks zeunerite (carnotite gets it instead). 9e added a Cu/(Cu+Ca) > 0.5 cation gate to fork against uranospinite (Ca-cation analog). Engine + supersaturation + nucleation + narrator complete in vugg.py + index.html. supersaturation_zeunerite returns 0 when As/(P+As+V) < 0.5 OR when Cu/(Cu+Ca) < 0.5. Isostructural with torbernite (Schneeberg pair); shares all habit-selection logic. Sources: research/research-zeunerite.md + research/research-uranospinite.md.

habit_variants.name: earthy_crust
habit_variants.trigger: default — canary-yellow earthy stain, the diagnostic Colorado Plateau habit. One percent of carnotite stains an entire sandstone outcrop the color of school buses.

habit_variants.name: tabular_plates

carnotite.description: K₂(UO₂)₂(VO₄)₂·3H₂O — monoclinic uranyl vanadate, the V-branch of the autunite-group anion-competition trio (with torbernite for phosphate and zeunerite for arsenate). Bright canary-yellow earthy crusts on sandstone — the mineral that paints the desert. One percent of carnotite by weight is enough to stain an entire Jurassic outcrop the color of school buses, which is how the Colorado Plateau uranium districts were prospected before instruments. Formed where oxidizing groundwater carries U⁶⁺ + V⁵⁺ + K⁺ together at the supergene front, often concentrated around organic reducing traps (petrified wood, carbonaceous shales). Mohs ~2 (soft, earthy), strongly radioactive, non-fluorescent (vanadate matrix quenches the uranyl emission). Type locality: Roc Creek, Montrose County, Colorado (1899). Sources: research/research-carnotite.md (boss commit 3bfdf4a); Colorado Plateau uranium-vanadium roll-front geology.

trace_ingredients.Ca: tyuyamunite variant — Ca analogue Ca(UO₂)₂(VO₄)₂·5-8H₂O, forms when Ca dominates over K

twin_laws.name: carnotite_001
twin_laws.trigger: twin and composition plane in rare crystals

acid_dissolution.reaction: K₂(UO₂)₂(VO₄)₂·3H₂O + 6H⁺ → 2K⁺ + 2UO₂²⁺ + 2H₃VO₄ + 3H₂O

carnotite.audit_status: Round 9c (Apr 2026), updated 9e (May 2026). V-branch of the uranyl anion-competition trio (with torbernite for P-branch, zeunerite for As-branch, both shipped in 9b). 9e added a K/(K+Ca) > 0.5 cation gate to fork against tyuyamunite (Ca-cation analog). Different cation (K instead of Cu), different crystal system (monoclinic instead of tetragonal), different habit (canary-yellow earthy crusts instead of emerald-green tabular plates), same anion-competition mechanic. supersaturation_carnotite returns 0 when V/(P+As+V) < 0.5 OR when K/(K+Ca) < 0.5. No shipped scenario has the K+U+V+low-T+oxidizing conjunction with K-dominant Ca, so carnotite + tyuyamunite are both content-gap minerals (same status as 9b/9d). Engine + test_cases verified. Sources: research/research-carnotite.md + research/research-tyuyamunite.md; Colorado Plateau roll-front geology; Roc Creek type locality (1899).

autunite.description: Ca(UO₂)₂(PO₄)₂·11H₂O — tetragonal uranyl phosphate, the Ca-cation analog of torbernite. Bright canary-yellow tabular plates on the same {001} habit, but where torbernite's Cu²⁺ quenches the uranyl emission, autunite's Ca²⁺ does not — under longwave UV (365nm) it glows intense apple-green, one of the brightest fluorescent species known. Round 9d cation-fork mechanic: same parent fluid as torbernite (U + P + supergene-T + oxidizing), differentiated by Cu/(Cu+Ca) ratio. Ca dominates groundwater everywhere except in actively-mined Cu districts, so autunite is far more common in nature than torbernite (which gets all the museum attention). Mohs 2-2.5; dehydrates irreversibly to meta-autunite (8 H₂O) above ~80°C. Type locality: Saint-Symphorien, Autun, France (Adrien Brongniart, 1852). Sources: Pinch & Wilson 1977; research/research-uraninite.md §164-178 (paragenetic chain).

trace_ingredients.Cu: trace Cu (below the cation-fork threshold) shifts color toward greenish-yellow; transitional toward torbernite

autunite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Ca(UO₂)₂(PO₄)₂·11H₂O → Ca(UO₂)₂(PO₄)₂·8H₂O + 3H₂O (irreversible dehydration to meta-autunite); further heat → meta-autunite II (rare)

fluorescence.quencher: Fe (Fe²⁺ + Fe³⁺ both suppress uranyl emission); dehydration to meta-autunite weakens but does not eliminate fluorescence
fluorescence.status: the brightest uranyl fluorescer in the simulator — Ca²⁺ doesn't quench like Cu²⁺ does in torbernite/zeunerite. LW UV (365nm) drives the strongest emission; SW (254nm) gives a duller green-yellow.

twin_laws.name: contact_001
twin_laws._source: Frondel 1958 The Systematic Mineralogy of Uranium and Thorium §autunite-group + Anthony Handbook v.IV. Autunite (tetragonal I4/mmm, Ca(UO₂)₂(PO₄)₂·11H₂O) shows {001} basal contact twins documented at Margnac (France) and Mt. Spokane (Washington). The autunite-group ditetragonal-dipyramidal habit produces square tablets where contact twins appear as paired basal flakes. Conservative p=0.01.

acid_dissolution.reaction: Ca(UO₂)₂(PO₄)₂·11H₂O + 6H⁺ → Ca²⁺ + 2(UO₂)²⁺ + 2H₃PO₄ + 11H₂O (uranyl phosphates dissolve readily in mineral acids)

greenish_yellow.trigger: Cu > 1 ppm but Ca/(Cu+Ca) > 0.5 — Cu-contaminated but still autunite-side of the cation fork

pale_green.trigger: near the autunite/torbernite cation-fork boundary (Cu/(Cu+Ca) approaching 0.5)

sulfur_yellow.trigger: high σ + thick crystal — saturated yellow

dull_meta.trigger: T_history > 80°C (irreversible dehydration to meta-autunite — color dulls, fluorescence weakens)

autunite.audit_status: Round 9d (May 2026): Ca-cation analog of torbernite, completing the cation-fork generalization on the P-branch of the uranyl-anion-competition mechanic (9b/9c was the anion fork P-vs-As-vs-V; 9d is the cation fork Cu-vs-Ca on the P-branch alone). supersaturation_autunite returns 0 when Ca/(Cu+Ca) < 0.5 OR when P/(P+As+V) < 0.5. supersaturation_torbernite gains a symmetric Cu/(Cu+Ca) > 0.5 gate (was Cu>=5 only). Autunite is the geologically-default uranyl phosphate (Ca >>> Cu in groundwater); torbernite is the special case (Cu-dominant fluid in mining districts). Habit variants mirror torbernite (tabular_plates/micaceous_book/encrusting). Source: research/research-uraninite.md §Variants for Game §4 (boss canonical 626bb22, May 2026). 2026-05-07 backfill from research-autunite.md: added 5 color rules (lemon_yellow default, greenish_yellow Cu trace, pale_green near fork, sulfur_yellow thick crystal, dull_meta dehydration), Cu trace ingredient, Fe fluorescence quencher, meta-autunite II reaction note.

tyuyamunite.description: Ca(UO₂)₂(VO₄)₂·5-8H₂O — orthorhombic Ca-uranyl vanadate, the cation analog of carnotite. Forms by the same mechanism (oxidizing groundwater + uraninite weathering + V-bearing fluid) but wins when Ca/(K+Ca) > 0.5 — the geological-default cation in sandstone groundwater. The two species are commonly intergrown in Colorado Plateau and Tyuya-Muyun deposits, with Britannica noting they are interconvertible by cation exchange. Canary-yellow earthy crusts staining sandstone, weakly-to-moderately fluorescent yellow-green LW UV (vanadate matrix dampens emission slightly even with Ca-cation; cleaner than carnotite's yellow but not as bright as autunite/uranospinite). Mohs 1.5-2, dehydrates readily to metatyuyamunite (·3-5H₂O) at room T in dry storage. Type locality: Tyuya-Muyun, Fergana Valley, Kyrgyzstan (Nenadkevich 1912). Source: research/research-tyuyamunite.md (implementation-grade draft, 2026-05-01); cation-fork mechanic mirrors carnotite + the Round 9d torbernite/autunite pair.

trace_ingredients.K: competes for the same uranyl-vanadate; high K routes to carnotite

tyuyamunite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Ca(UO₂)₂(VO₄)₂·8H₂O → Ca(UO₂)₂(VO₄)₂·5H₂O + 3H₂O (room-T dehydration to metatyuyamunite); full collapse near 100°C

fluorescence.status: weakly to moderately fluorescent — Ca²⁺ doesn't quench like Cu²⁺ would, but the vanadate matrix damps the uranyl emission via vibrational coupling (same effect that makes carnotite weakly fluorescent). Brighter than carnotite, dimmer than autunite/uranospinite. Source: research/research-tyuyamunite.md.

tyuyamunite._twin_laws_note: Intentionally empty — tyuyamunite (Ca(UO₂)₂(VO₄)₂·5-8H₂O, orthorhombic Pnnm) habit code 'earthy_crust' signals non-euhedral aggregate. Typical occurrence is canary-yellow earthy coatings on Colorado-Plateau sandstone, not individual tabular crystals. No documented growth twinning at this habit scale. v138 phosphate batch.

acid_dissolution.reaction: Ca(UO₂)₂(VO₄)₂·8H₂O + 6H⁺ → Ca²⁺ + 2(UO₂)²⁺ + 2H₃VO₄ + 8H₂O (uranyl vanadates dissolve in mineral acids; below pH 5 vanadate gives way to VO₂⁺ and the structure breaks)

tyuyamunite.audit_status: Round 9e (May 2026): Ca-cation analog of carnotite, completing the cation+anion fork on the V-branch (mirror of how 9d's autunite paired with torbernite on the P-branch). supersaturation_tyuyamunite gates on Ca/(K+Ca) > 0.5 + V/(P+As+V) > 0.5; supersaturation_carnotite gains a symmetric K/(K+Ca) > 0.5 cation gate. Habit variants mirror carnotite (tabular_plates rare, earthy_crust default, powdery_disseminated low-σ). Source: research/research-tyuyamunite.md.

test_cases.rationale: K/(K+Ca)=0.86 — carnotite wins the cation race, tyuyamunite cation-blocked.

test_cases.name: p_dominant_blocks_via_anion_fork
test_cases.rationale: Ca dominant cation, but P/(P+As+V)=0.8 — anion fork sends this to autunite, tyuyamunite anion-blocked.

test_cases.name: no_uranium_blocks
test_cases.rationale: U=0 fails U>=0.3 ingredient gate.

test_cases.name: reducing_blocks
test_cases.rationale: Reducing fluid keeps U as U⁴⁺ + V as V³⁺/V⁴⁺. Tyuyamunite needs U⁶⁺ + V⁵⁺.

test_cases.name: acidic_blocks
test_cases.rationale: pH=4.0 below 5.0 stability gate — vanadate gives way to VO₂⁺ and structure breaks.

habit_variants.name: tabular_plates
habit_variants.trigger: default — thin yellow tabular plates flattened on {001}, the autunite-group habit

habit_variants.name: micaceous_book
habit_variants.trigger: high σ, low T — stacked subparallel plates, the Schneeberg/Margnac book habit

habit_variants.name: encrusting
habit_variants.trigger: low σ — earthy yellow encrustation, secondary surface staining

uranospinite.description: Ca(UO₂)₂(AsO₄)₂·10H₂O — tetragonal Ca-uranyl arsenate, the autunite-group cation analog of zeunerite. Same parent fluid as zeunerite (U + As + supergene-T + oxidizing) but wins when Ca/(Cu+Ca) > 0.5 — typically when Cu has been depleted from the local fluid but As is still around. Strongly fluorescent yellow-green LW UV — Ca²⁺ doesn't quench like Cu²⁺ does in zeunerite, putting uranospinite in the same 'bright fluorescer' tier as autunite (slightly dampened by the heavier As-anion vibrational coupling). Tabular yellow plates that look like a yellow autunite, with the As content as the chemistry giveaway. Mohs 2-3, dehydrates readily to metauranospinite. Type locality: Walpurgis Flacher vein, Weisser Hirsch Mine, Schneeberg, Saxony (Weisbach 1873). Sources: research/research-uranospinite.md (implementation-grade draft, 2026-05-01); MSA Handbook of Mineralogy.

trace_ingredients.Cu: competes for the same uranyl-arsenate; high Cu routes to zeunerite
trace_ingredients.P: competes for the same uranyl-cation; high P routes to autunite (the P-anion sibling)

uranospinite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Ca(UO₂)₂(AsO₄)₂·10H₂O → Ca(UO₂)₂(AsO₄)₂·xH₂O + (10-x)H₂O (room-T dehydration to metauranospinite, water content variable; full collapse ~80°C)

fluorescence.status: strong fluorescence — Ca²⁺ doesn't quench uranyl emission like Cu²⁺ does in zeunerite. Slightly dimmer than autunite (the heavier As-anion absorbs some emission via vibrational coupling) but still in the 'bright' tier alongside autunite. Source: research/research-uranospinite.md.

twin_laws.name: contact_001
twin_laws._source: Frondel 1958. Uranospinite (Ca(UO₂)₂(AsO₄)₂·10H₂O, tetragonal P4/nmm) is the Ca-As member of autunite-group; isostructural with autunite (Ca-P endmember). Same basal {001} contact twins. Sparser field documentation than autunite/zeunerite — most uranospinite is collected at Schneeberg + a few Bohemian localities. p=0.01.

acid_dissolution.reaction: Ca(UO₂)₂(AsO₄)₂·10H₂O + 6H⁺ → Ca²⁺ + 2(UO₂)²⁺ + 2H₃AsO₄ + 10H₂O (uranyl arsenates dissolve readily in mineral acids)

uranospinite.audit_status: Round 9e (May 2026): Ca-cation analog of zeunerite, completing the cation+anion fork on the As-branch (mirror of how 9d's autunite paired with torbernite on the P-branch). supersaturation_uranospinite gates on Ca/(Cu+Ca) > 0.5 + As/(P+As+V) > 0.5; supersaturation_zeunerite gains a symmetric Cu/(Cu+Ca) > 0.5 cation gate. Habit variants mirror autunite/zeunerite (tabular_plates default, micaceous_book high-σ, encrusting low-σ). The 9e fork pair (tyuyamunite + uranospinite) completes the cation+anion fork mechanic across all three anion branches. Source: research/research-uranospinite.md.

test_cases.name: ca_dominant_as_branch_fires
test_cases.rationale: Ca/(Cu+Ca)=1.0 + As/(P+As+V)=1.0 — both forks pass for uranospinite.

test_cases.name: cu_dominant_blocks_via_cation_fork
test_cases.rationale: Cu/(Cu+Ca)=0.89 — zeunerite wins the cation race, uranospinite cation-blocked.

test_cases.name: p_dominant_blocks_via_anion_fork
test_cases.rationale: Ca dominant cation, but P/(P+As+V)=0.8 — anion fork sends this to autunite, uranospinite anion-blocked.

test_cases.name: no_uranium_blocks
test_cases.rationale: U=0 fails U>=0.3 ingredient gate.

test_cases.name: reducing_blocks
test_cases.rationale: Reducing fluid keeps U as U⁴⁺. Uranospinite needs U⁶⁺.

test_cases.name: acidic_blocks
test_cases.rationale: pH=4.0 below 4.5 stability gate — autunite-group dissolves in acid.

habit_variants.name: cubic
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ, slow evaporation

habit_variants.name: hopper_growth
habit_variants.trigger: high σ or high fill — rapid evaporation / boundary-layer-diffusion-limited skeletal cube

habit_variants.name: fibrous_coating
habit_variants.trigger: high fill — efflorescent crust on already-dry wall, late-stage drusy carpet

habit_variants.name: stalactitic_speleothem

halite.description: NaCl, rock salt — the canonical chloride evaporite. Forms by evaporative concentration of Na-Cl-rich brines: closed-basin playas, sabkha, drained vugs in the supergene zone. Hopper-growth (skeletal cube with pyramidal hollows) is the signature high-supersaturation habit; clean cubes form when the brine concentrates slowly. Type localities: Wieliczka Salt Mine (Poland — meter-scale cubes, the cathedral carved in salt); Stassfurt (Germany — brilliant blue irradiated halite); Searles Lake (California — pink hoppered crystals from evaporation ponds with halobacterial carotenoids).
halite.T_behavior: Stable across wide T range; evaporite habit prefers low T

trace_ingredients.Fe: rose-pink inclusions; yellow as Fe-impurity
trace_ingredients.organic: halobacterial carotenoids → bacterial-pink (Searles Lake aesthetic, biological-flag — not a real broth field)
trace_ingredients.hematite: red from hematite microinclusions (rare — tracked as Fe trace at high level)

halite.thermal_decomp_reaction: NaCl(s) → NaCl(l) (melts at 801°C, no chemical decomposition)

fluorescence.activator: Mn²⁺ + organic inclusions + lattice defects (variable — only a fraction of specimens fluoresce)
fluorescence.status: rare; Stassfurt / Wieliczka specimens show LW response; commercial salt typically doesn't

blue_purple.trigger: K > 200 ppm — irradiation-induced color centers (Stassfurt aesthetic)

rose_pink.trigger: Fe > 30 ppm — Searles Lake / Stassfurt habit

bacterial_pink.trigger: biological / organic inclusion (halobacterial carotenoid in evaporation pond)

halite.audit_status: v27 — evaporite chemistry pass. 2026-05-07 backfill from research-halite.md: added stalactitic_speleothem habit, restructured fluorescence (Mn²⁺ activator, weak white/yellow/orange-red LW), added spinel_law_rare twin (probability 0.005), added bacterial_pink (halobacterial carotenoid) + yellow (Fe impurity) + red_hematite_inclusion color rules, type-locality detail in description (Wieliczka / Stassfurt / Searles).

habit_variants.name: prismatic
habit_variants.trigger: low T, slow evaporation — Boron CA museum prisms

habit_variants.name: cottonball
habit_variants.trigger: high fill or rapid evaporation — Death Valley playa surface, late-stage cottonball crust

habit_variants.name: massive
habit_variants.trigger: tincal nodules from ancient lake beds

borax.description: Na₂[B₄O₅(OH)₄]·8H₂O — sodium tetraborate decahydrate. The mineral that doesn’t belong in a typical hydrothermal vug: a playa-lake / closed-basin evaporite that crystallizes only from alkaline (pH > 8.5) brines rich in Na and B at low T. The defining feature is what happens after: leave a fresh borax crystal in dry air and within weeks it effloresces to white powder (tincalconite, Na₂B₄O₇·5H₂O), losing 5 of its 10 water molecules to humidity. This is the only common mineral that self-destructs in normal collection conditions — every borax specimen is a race against dehydration.

trace_ingredients.Cu: pale blue/green tint (rare)

borax.thermal_decomp_reaction: Borax → tincalconite (50–75°C, dry air) → kernite (>100°C burial) → anhydrous Na₂B₄O₇ (>320°C)

twin_laws.name: contact_100
twin_laws._source: Anthony Handbook v.V (borates volume) + Dana 8th. Borax (monoclinic C2/c, Na2B4O5(OH)4·8H2O) shows rare {100} contact twins in well-formed prismatic specimens. Documented at Boron (Kramer CA) + Searles Lake + Inder Lake (Kazakhstan). Most field borax is rapidly precipitated efflorescent or recrystallized from saturated brines, with limited residence time for visible twinning. p=0.005 (rare-twin floor with data-sparse note).

borax._late_stage_note: Proposal C (2026-05): propensity:1. Playa efflorescent crust; same as fill_exempt set (Backlog K).
borax._retune_note_fill_exempt: Backlog K (2026-05): added fill_exempt:true. Borax is a paragenetically-late playa-evaporite crust — it grows ON existing halite/clay efflorescent surfaces in real playa basins (Searles Lake CA, Boron CA), it doesn't compete with halite for nucleation sites. With the global check_nucleation gate at vugFill < 0.95, halite's bulk fill locks borax out of searles_lake within ~48 steps. fill_exempt:true lets it nucleate past the 0.95 cap; per-mineral max_nucleation_count:8 still bounds total crystal count.

habit_variants.name: pseudomorph
habit_variants.trigger: inherited from parent borax crystal

tincalconite.description: Na₂B₄O₇·5H₂O — borax with five water molecules gone. Appears in the simulator only as a paramorph product of borax: the external crystal shape is preserved (the cubic outline, the prismatic terminations, all the field marks) but the lattice has collapsed and the surface has bloomed white-powdery. A tincalconite specimen IS a former borax crystal, frozen mid-decay. Stable in any humidity — once dehydrated, it doesn’t re-hydrate.
tincalconite.T_behavior: Stable from cool to ~320°C anhydrous transition
tincalconite._twin_laws_note: Intentionally empty — tincalconite (Na2B4O7·5H2O, hexagonal R-3c) is the dehydration paramorph of borax at low humidity / mild heating. Inherits borax's twin geometry through paramorph transition rather than nucleating fresh — same v138 paramorph-inheritance convention used for meta-autunite + metatorbernite + metazeunerite. The parent borax carries the twin signal. v141 final batch.
tincalconite._late_stage_note: Proposal C (2026-05): propensity:1. Paramorph after borax — only ever late by definition (dehydration product).

habit_variants.name: prismatic
habit_variants.trigger: cold T (<25°C), slow evap — Glauber crystals

habit_variants.name: fibrous_coating
habit_variants.trigger: efflorescent crust on dry wall

mirabilite.description: Na₂SO₄·10H₂O — Glauber salt. The cold-evaporite of the sodium-sulfate system. Stable only below 32.4°C (the eutectic with thenardite); warm above that and the lattice loses 10 water molecules and inverts to thenardite. Forms in winter playas, Antarctic dry valleys, cold caves — anywhere a Na-rich brine cools without freezing. The transparent prismatic crystals are stable enough to handle in cold weather but lose their water in warm dry rooms. Most museum specimens are tincal-style: the mirabilite went away decades ago and the white powdery thing in the case is thenardite pseudomorph.
mirabilite.T_behavior: Decahydrate stable below 32.4°C eutectic; → thenardite above

twin_laws.name: contact_100
twin_laws._source: Anthony Handbook v.V mirabilite section. Mirabilite (monoclinic P21/c, Na2SO4·10H2O — Glauber's salt) shows rare {100} contact twins. Most mirabilite is rapidly-precipitated efflorescent crust at saline-lake margins (Searles Lake CA, Mono Lake) and rarely persists long enough at room T (it dehydrates to thenardite above 32.4°C) for visible twinning to develop. p=0.005 (rare-twin floor with solubility-limited-observation caveat).

mirabilite._late_stage_note: Proposal C (2026-05): propensity:1. Cold-side Na-sulfate efflorescent; same as fill_exempt set.
mirabilite._retune_note_fill_exempt: Backlog K (2026-05): added fill_exempt:true. Mirabilite is the cold-side Na-sulfate evaporite (T<32.4°C eutectic). In Searles Lake's actual paragenesis (Smith 1979), mirabilite blades grow on top of halite floors during winter brine pools and on cave walls as efflorescent crust — it's a late-stage decorator, not a bulk filler. The previous vugFill < 0.95 global gate locked it out of searles_lake once halite hit ~95% fill (~48 steps). max_nucleation_count:8 still bounds total count.

habit_variants.name: dipyramidal
habit_variants.trigger: orthorhombic dipyramids — Searles Lake / Bayan Khar

habit_variants.name: tabular
habit_variants.trigger: pinacoid-dominant flat plates

habit_variants.name: pseudomorph
habit_variants.trigger: paramorph after mirabilite

habit_variants.name: fibrous_coating
habit_variants.trigger: efflorescent saline crust

thenardite.description: Na₂SO₄ — anhydrous sodium sulfate. The warm-evaporite half of the mirabilite-thenardite pair: where Glauber salt dehydrates above 32°C, thenardite is what’s left, OR what crystallizes directly from warm playa brines that never cool through the eutectic. Orthorhombic dipyramids in well-formed crystals, tabular plates more often, white efflorescent crusts most commonly. Searles Lake CA, Bayan Khar Mongolia, Camp Verde AZ, the type locality at Espartinas Spain.

twin_laws.name: contact_110
twin_laws._source: Anthony Handbook v.V thenardite section. Thenardite (orthorhombic Fddd, Na2SO4) is the dehydration product of mirabilite (>32.4°C) and the dominant Na-sulfate phase in arid evaporites (Soda Lake CA, Searles Lake, Espartinas Spain). {110} contact twins documented but rare — like mirabilite, most field thenardite is efflorescent rather than euhedral. p=0.005 (rare-twin floor).

thenardite._late_stage_note: Proposal C (2026-05): propensity:1. Warm-side Na-sulfate efflorescent; same as fill_exempt set.
thenardite._retune_note_fill_exempt: Backlog K (2026-05): added fill_exempt:true. Thenardite is the warm-side anhydrous Na-sulfate (T>32.4°C eutectic), either direct-precipitated from warm playa brines OR a dehydration paramorph after mirabilite. In the searles_lake event sequence it's intentionally a fibrous_coating habit variant on top of the existing crystal pile (Searles Lake, Bayan Khar — both classic localities for thenardite efflorescent crusts). Same fill-cap unlock reasoning as mirabilite + borax.

habit_variants.name: stout_prismatic

habit_variants.name: geniculate_twin

habit_variants.name: sixling_star
habit_variants.trigger: low σ, moderate T — rare cyclic-sixling 'reticulated rutile' star

habit_variants.name: included_in_quartz
habit_variants.trigger: host quartz growing simultaneously — needles enclosed inside quartz crystal (rutilated quartz / Venus hair)

rutile.description: TiO2, the tetragonal titanium oxide. The canonical 'needle' mineral — slender red-brown to golden prisms with adamantine luster, often as inclusions in quartz (rutilated quartz / Venus hair / Cupid's darts). Forms in alpine-cleft fissures, eclogite-facies metamorphism, and pegmatites whenever titanium concentrates. Diagnostic geniculate (knee) twins on {011} and rare reticulated sixling stars. One of the most chemically resistant minerals known — survives weathering, transport, and burial unchanged.

trace_ingredients.Fe: red-brown default; Fe-rich → black nigrine

rutile.thermal_decomp_reaction: TiO2 melts at 1843°C — refractory; no geologic decomposition
rutile.audit_status: new (priority-three batch 2026-05-07). First Ti consumer in the sim. Reuses existing Ti broth field. Inclusion-in-quartz habit demonstrates the existing inclusion system. Specced in proposals/MINERALS-ROUNDS-3-6.md §5b. Scenarios gem_pegmatite + porphyry already carry Ti=0.5 ppm — broth needs Ti bump (≥30 ppm) before rutile actually nucleates. No engine yet.

habit_variants.name: botryoidal_crust
habit_variants.trigger: default — smooth blue mammillary aggregates on cavity walls

habit_variants.name: nodular_massive
habit_variants.trigger: rounded blue lumps in fractured matrix; moderate σ

habit_variants.name: veinlet_fill
habit_variants.trigger: thin blue stringers along host-rock fractures — dominant habit at most localities

habit_variants.name: spider_web
habit_variants.trigger: matrix-rich variety with host-rock fragments forming filigree network through blue (Hubei / Lone Mountain)

habit_variants.name: triclinic_microcrystal
habit_variants.trigger: very low σ + slow growth — rare tabular pseudo-rhombic plates (Lynch Station VA, probability < 0.05)

turquoise.description: CuAl6(PO4)4(OH)8·4H2O — hydrated copper-aluminum phosphate. The 'sky-blue' supergene mineral, mined since ~5000 BCE (Maghara/Sinai, Nishapur/Iran). Forms in arid Cu-porphyry oxidation zones when meteoric water leaches Cu²⁺ from sulfides and PO₄³⁻ from host apatite. Color from Cu²⁺ chromophore; Fe³⁺ substitution shifts toward green chalcosiderite. Crystallizes essentially only as cryptocrystalline aggregate (botryoidal, veinlet-fill, nodular); discrete triclinic crystals known only from Lynch Station, VA. Distinguishes the supergene Cu zone from chrysocolla (silica), malachite (carbonate), and atacamite (chloride) — the four-axis branching of the Cu oxidation zone keyed to fluid anion chemistry.

trace_ingredients.Fe: green chalcosiderite-leaning when Fe/(Fe+Al) > 0.5
trace_ingredients.Zn: yellow-green faustite end-member (Zn for Cu, rare)

turquoise.thermal_decomp_reaction: Loses zeolitic water above ~200°C; color shifts irreversibly from sky-blue to green to chalky white
turquoise._twin_laws_note: Intentionally empty — turquoise (CuAl₆(PO₄)₄(OH)₈·4H₂O, triclinic P1̄) habit code 'botryoidal_crust' signals non-euhedral aggregate. Forms cryptocrystalline nodular/botryoidal masses in Cu supergene zones (Cerrillos NM, Sleeping Beauty AZ, Neyshabur Iran), virtually never as individual euhedral crystals. The rare microscopic crystals (Bishop Mine VA) are too small for twinning to be observed. v138 phosphate batch.

acid_dissolution.reaction: CuAl6(PO4)4(OH)8·4H2O + 12H+ -> Cu2+ + 6Al3+ + 4H3PO4 + 8H2O

turquoise.audit_status: new (priority-three batch 2026-05-07). First Cu-phosphate in the sim. Pairs with chrysocolla (silicate), malachite (carbonate), and atacamite (in brief, to be added) for 4-axis Cu-supergene branching keyed to fluid anion. supergene_oxidation broth has Cu=55, Al=25, P=0.5 — P needs bump to ≥1 ppm before turquoise actually nucleates. bisbee scenario also a fit. No engine yet.

chrysoprase._retune_note_nucleation_sigma: Stale-mineral retune (2026-05): dropped 1.2 → 1.0 to match the σ ceiling of supersaturation_chrysoprase() under ultramafic_supergene broth. tools/stale_mineral_probe.mjs sweep found σ peaked at 1.001 (just barely above unity) in only 1/600 (seed × step) pairs at SiO2=166 Ni=200 Mg=300 T=28°C pH=8.5. The formula is correctly calibrated — chrysoprase IS rare and slow-forming in nature — but the threshold was set at 1.2 above the formula's natural ceiling. Lowering to 1.0 lets it fire when σ just clears unity, matching the rng.random() < 0.12 stochastic gate and the geological 'rare-but-real' semantics for Ni-chalcedony.

habit_variants.trigger: default — fracture-fill in serpentinite host

habit_variants.name: nodular_massive
habit_variants.trigger: rounded apple-green nodules in saprolite matrix

habit_variants.trigger: rare botryoidal exterior on a vein-fill, probability ~0.1

habit_variants.name: banded_chalcedony
habit_variants.trigger: faint internal banding from flow-deposition cycles

chrysoprase.description: SiO2 — Ni-bearing chalcedony (microcrystalline-fibrous quartz). The apple-green chalcedony variety, colored by nano-inclusions of Ni-phyllosilicate (pimelite/willemseite/kerolite) within the silica fabric — a composite color, not a lattice color. Forms in supergene weathering of ultramafic/serpentinite protolith where olivine breaks down and meteoric groundwater scavenges Ni and silica into fractures. Mined since the 14th century at Szklary (Poland); current world reference is Marlborough (Queensland). Heat above ~150°C and prolonged sun-exposure fade the color irreversibly. Establishes the chalcedony habit family in the sim — substrate for future agate/carnelian/onyx siblings.

trace_ingredients.Cr: shifts toward mtorolite/chrome-chalcedony (different mineral, alternate substrate)
trace_ingredients.Fe: muddies green toward olive (oxide staining)

chrysoprase.thermal_decomp_reaction: Ni-clay nano-inclusions destabilize above ~150°C — color fades irreversibly to white/yellow-brown; chalcedony lattice persists to 870°C α→β quartz transition
chrysoprase._twin_laws_note: Intentionally empty — chrysoprase is Ni-bearing chalcedony, a microcrystalline aggregate of fibrous quartz with no individual euhedral crystals to twin. The parent quartz species has twin_laws (Dauphine, Brazil, Japan) but those require crystalline individuals; chrysoprase's chalcedony texture doesn't produce them at scale.
chrysoprase.audit_status: new (priority-three batch 2026-05-07). First chalcedony habit in the sim — substrate for future agate / carnelian / onyx variants. Distinct from quartz entry by habit (microfibrous-cryptocrystalline vs. prismatic) and chemistry niche (ultramafic supergene Ni vs. broad hydrothermal SiO2). supergene_oxidation broth has no Ni — needs a future ultramafic_supergene scenario (Marlborough / Niquelândia / Szklary) for proper firing. No engine yet.

apatite.description: Ca5(PO4)3(F,Cl,OH) — the structural archetype of the apatite supergroup (parent of pyromorphite, mimetite, vanadinite). Mohs hardness reference at 5. Forms in nearly every geological environment — magmatic accessory, pegmatite late-stage, hydrothermal vein, metamorphic skarn, sedimentary phosphorite, biological. Color span is the widest of any common mineral: green, blue, violet, yellow, brown depending on Mn²⁺, REE, Fe²⁺ activators. Fluorescent under UV in most varieties via Mn²⁺ + Eu²⁺ + Sm³⁺ + Ce³⁺ activator soup.

trace_ingredients.REE: yellow-green / pink color (Ce, Nd, Sm in Ca site)

apatite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Apatite melts at ~1500°C — refractory; no low-T decomposition

fluorescence.activator: Mn²⁺ + REE (Eu²⁺ blue, Sm³⁺ pink, Ce³⁺ blue-violet)

acid_dissolution.reaction: Ca5(PO4)3F + 7H+ -> 5Ca2+ + 3H2PO4- + HF

apatite.audit_status: new (brief-16 batch 2026-05-07). First Ca-phosphate at apatite end-member; bridges to existing Pb-end-members pyromorphite/mimetite/vanadinite (apatite supergroup). gem_pegmatite + supergene_oxidation broths already carry Ca and P at trace level — needs P bump to ≥5 ppm. No engine yet.

atacamite.description: Cu2Cl(OH)3 — the chloride end-member of the supergene Cu oxidation suite. Forms in arid Cu-porphyry zones where seawater or saline groundwater delivers Cl⁻ and the dry climate prevents the carbonate (malachite/azurite) and sulfate (brochantite/antlerite) phases from winning. Color is the most intense emerald-green of any common mineral — Cu²⁺ chromophore in distorted octahedral coordination. Atacama Desert is the type locality and the climate ideal: rain-shadow aridity + Cu sulfide oxidation + chloride-rich groundwater. Also forms on bronze artifacts in seawater (Antikythera mechanism, sunken Greek statues).
atacamite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Cu2Cl(OH)3 -> Cu oxide + HCl + H2O above 200°C

acid_dissolution.reaction: Cu2Cl(OH)3 + 3H+ -> 2Cu2+ + Cl- + 3H2O

atacamite.audit_status: new (brief-16 batch 2026-05-07). Completes the four-axis supergene Cu branching: chrysocolla (silicate) / malachite (carbonate) / atacamite (chloride) / turquoise (phosphate). bisbee broth has Cu but Cl=0; needs Cl ≥30 ppm for atacamite to fire. supergene_oxidation also a fit. No engine yet.

calaverite.description: AuTe2 — the gold-telluride that broke crystallography. Goldschmidt documented 92 distinct crystal forms before the structure was even understood; the lattice carries an incommensurate modulation wave through the tellurium positions, driven by Au fluctuating between Au⁺ and Au³⁺ states. This is the structure that defied Haüy's law of rational indices for a century. Beyond the pathology, calaverite is the world's premier gold-telluride ore mineral — Cripple Creek (Colorado) and Kalgoorlie (Western Australia) are its monuments. Brass-yellow metallic luster, ~3% Ag substitution typical, photochemically stable (unlike its cousin sylvanite). Decomposes above ~450°C to native gold + Te vapor.

trace_ingredients.Ag: Ag-bearing calaverite grades toward sylvanite; up to ~3% Ag for Au substitution
trace_ingredients.S: minor S signals associated sulfide phases

calaverite.audit_status: new (brief-16 batch 2026-05-07). First gold-telluride. Pairs with sylvanite (Au-Ag) and hessite (Ag) to form an epithermal-telluride trio gated by Au:Ag:Te ratios. No existing scenario carries Au + Te in the right window — gem_pegmatite + porphyry are placeholders until an epithermal_telluride scenario lands. No engine yet.

habit_variants.name: octahedral
habit_variants.trigger: default — black metallic octahedra (spinel habit)

habit_variants.name: massive_granular
habit_variants.trigger: high σ — chromitite seam fabric, mm-scale granular cumulate

habit_variants.name: dodecahedral
habit_variants.trigger: rare — moderate σ

chromite.description: FeCr2O4 — the iron-chromium spinel and the principal Cr ore. Crystallizes early from cooling mafic / ultramafic magma at 1200–1400°C, far outside the vug regime; this is fundamentally a magmatic cumulate mineral, not a hydrothermal vug phase. Listed in the spec for completeness and for placer / detrital provenance reasoning — chromite survives weathering and transport unchanged due to extreme spinel-structure stability. Bushveld (South Africa), Stillwater (Montana), Great Dyke (Zimbabwe) are the layered-intrusion type localities. Magnesiochromite (Mg → Fe substitution) and hercynite-leaning (Al → Cr substitution) are the two main solid-solution arms.
chromite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Refractory — survives metamorphism unchanged; xieite polymorph at 18-23 GPa (impact conditions)

twin_laws.name: spinel_law
twin_laws._source: Dana 8th ed. spinel-group section. Chromite shares the spinel-group {111} contact twin with magnetite, spinel s.s., and (geometrically) galena. Cumulate-grade chromite at Bushveld / Stillwater shows the twin in 5-10% of euhedral grains. Conservative p=0.04 for the sample batch.

chromite.audit_status: new (brief-16 batch 2026-05-07). Magmatic spinel — atypical vug mineral. Listed for cataloging completeness; would only nucleate in a future layered-mafic-intrusion scenario at >1000°C, which doesn't exist. gem_pegmatite scenario is the closest existing high-T fit but pegmatites don't normally produce chromite. No engine yet.

habit_variants.name: prismatic_dipyramid
habit_variants.trigger: T > 500°C — tetragonal {110} prism + {111} pyramidal termination, the Erzgebirge / Bolivia pegmatite display habit

habit_variants.name: equant_octahedral
habit_variants.trigger: T 300-500°C — blocky equant cassiterite, the Cornwall greisen habit

habit_variants.name: botryoidal_woodtin
habit_variants.trigger: T < 300°C — concentric colloidal banded 'wood tin', the low-T placer-source habit

habit_variants.name: elbow_twin
habit_variants.trigger: {011} ~60° knee bend — the diagnostic cassiterite twin, fires probabilistically at moderate-to-high σ

cassiterite.description: SnO₂ — tetragonal tin dioxide, the primary tin ore that built the Bronze Age. Sn⁴⁺ only; fully oxidized; inert under any geological conditions (melts at 1630°C, doesn't dissolve in water or acid, doesn't decompose, doesn't oxidize further — it IS the terminal weathering product). The honey-yellow to amber to chocolate-brown to black 'tinstone' that the Cornish miners called 'black jack' and the Bohemian / Saxon miners called 'Zinnstein'. Three formation environments: pegmatitic at 400-700°C (Erzgebirge, Cornwall, San Diego County) yielding prismatic dipyramids with the diagnostic ~60° elbow/knee twin on {011}; hydrothermal at 300-500°C (Bolivia tin belt, Cornish greisen veins) yielding equant blocky crystals; low-T colloidal at < 300°C yielding 'wood tin', concentric-banded botryoidal masses that survive in placer deposits. Fe (10-1000 ppm) progressively darkens; Nb/Ta couple-substitute as accessory traces. The Erzgebirge tin trade preceded the silver/bismuth/uranium eras — 'Ore Mountains' originally meant tin mountains. Sources: research/research-cassiterite.md (boss canonical 2026-05); Williamson et al. 2010 (Cornwall fluid inclusions); Förster 1992 (Erzgebirge tin geology); Anthony et al. Handbook of Mineralogy Vol. III.
cassiterite._ingredients_note: v89 (2026-05-19): Sn-only is the gate per research-cassiterite.md (Sn ≥ 50 ppm nucleation, ≥ 10 ppm growth per research; sim gate at 20 lets cassiterite respond to scenarios with moderate Sn enrichment without requiring the high end of the literature range). Sn is the new FluidChemistry field added in v89 alongside this engine — see js/20-chemistry-fluid.ts. Redox: 'any' rather than 'oxidizing' per Williamson 2010 / Förster 1992 — pegmatite cassiterite forms from F-rich REDUCING brines via the SnF₆²⁻ + 2 H₂O → SnO₂ + 4 H⁺ + 6 F⁻ destabilization mechanism, not Sn²⁺→Sn⁴⁺ oxidation. Research file's literal 'oxidizing' applies to the wallrock interface, not bulk fluid. F-enhancement in the engine reflects the F-complex precipitation chemistry.

trace_ingredients.Fe: 10-1000 ppm progressively darkens cassiterite from honey-yellow → amber → red-brown → black tin-pitch; the Cornish 'black jack' tinstone is Fe-rich
trace_ingredients.Nb: trace-1% coupled substitution with Ta and Sn — typical late-pegmatite accessory
trace_ingredients.Ta: trace-1% coupled substitution; Nb/Ta ratio is a provenance fingerprint
trace_ingredients.Ti: trace-2% substitution; produces minor cathodoluminescence under SEM
trace_ingredients.W: Sn-W greisen pair partner — high W signals greisen-stage co-deposition with wolframite
trace_ingredients.Bi: trace from pegmatite-residual association (Schneeberg / Erzgebirge specifically)

cassiterite.thermal_decomp_reaction: INERT — cassiterite does not decompose under any geological conditions. Melts at 1630°C (above any geological setting in the simulator). No dissolution path. No oxidation path (Sn⁴⁺ is already fully oxidized). The terminal weathering product; mechanical breakdown to placer 'stream tin' only.

fluorescence.status: Non-fluorescent under standard SW/MW/LW UV. Trace weak cathodoluminescence possible in Fe/Nb/Ta-rich varieties under SEM (per research §Fluorescence) — not observable with field equipment, flagged here for completeness.

twin_laws.name: cassiterite_011_elbow
twin_laws.trigger: DIAGNOSTIC — elbow/knee twin bent ~60° on {011} is the cassiterite signature (research §Crystal Habits & Morphology: 'Twin signature: Elbow/knee twins on {011} bent ~60°; diagnostic feature'). Fires probabilistically when excess > 0.6

honey_amber.trigger: low Fe (<5 ppm) — gem-quality 'tin gem' form, transparent honey-yellow to amber

honey_brown.trigger: Fe 5-30 ppm — amber to chocolate-brown cassiterite (the field-common color)

red_brown.trigger: Fe 30-100 ppm — red-brown cassiterite, the Cornish weathered-tinstone color

black_metallic.trigger: Fe > 100 ppm — black 'tin pitch' / 'black jack' (the historical Cornish miner's tinstone)

cassiterite.audit_status: new v89 (2026-05-19). First Sn-consumer mineral; Sn fluid field added to FluidChemistry alongside this engine (first new chemistry field since v62 Cd/Hg). Three habit dispatch paths by T-at-nucleation (prismatic dipyramid > 500°C / equant blocky 300-500°C / wood-tin botryoidal < 300°C) cover the documented Erzgebirge/Cornwall/Bolivia pegmatite-greisen-low-T-colloidal spread. Cassiterite is INERT — no dissolution, no thermal decomp, no oxidation — the terminal weathering product; mechanical breakdown to placer 'stream tin' only. Substrate priority: wolframite (Cornish Sn-W greisen pair) > topaz > tourmaline > quartz > feldspar > wall. Sn added at 80 ppm to gem_pegmatite + radioactive_pegmatite, 60 ppm to schneeberg (the Erzgebirge tin-mining heritage that preceded the silver and uranium eras). Per research-cassiterite.md (boss canonical 2026-05).
cassiterite._late_stage_note: Proposal C (2026-05): propensity:0.3. Cassiterite is a mid-to-late pegmatite mineral — forms in the greisen/late-pegmatite phase after early micas + feldspars + tourmaline but before the lithium minerals (lepidolite, spodumene). The 0.3 propensity matches its position in the LCT pegmatite sequence (wall zone → intermediate zone → core).

habit_variants.name: short_prismatic_emerald
habit_variants.trigger: default — hexagonal {1010} prism + {0221} rhombohedron, the Tsumeb 2nd-oxidation-zone iconic habit (1-15 mm crystals on calcite scalenohedra)

habit_variants.name: long_prismatic_acicular
habit_variants.trigger: excess > 1.0 — c-axis-elongated needles, the Kaokoveld / Mindouli Congo habit; higher SiO₂/Cu ratio + fast nucleation

habit_variants.name: druzy_overgrowth
habit_variants.trigger: on chrysocolla substrate — sub-mm prisms at high nucleation density (chrysocolla Cu-Si gel lowers nucleation barrier)

habit_variants.name: pseudomorph_after_calcite
habit_variants.trigger: late vadose conversion — calcite scalenohedron outline preserved with dioptase fill, when late Cu-rich fluid attacks earlier calcite in a confined vug

dioptase.description: CuSiO₃·H₂O — emerald-green trigonal cyclosilicate (six-membered Si₆O₁₈ rings linked by Cu²⁺), the Tsumeb 2nd-oxidation-zone signature mineral. Type locality Altyn-Tyube, Kazakhstan (Hauy 1797). Hardness 5, density 3.31 g/cm³, perfect rhombohedral {1011} cleavage (the property that named the mineral — Greek dia+optazein, 'to see through'). Forms supergene at 25-120°C in mildly alkaline (pH 6.5-8.0) oxidizing pore fluids where carbonate has been LOCALLY EXHAUSTED by prior malachite/azurite precipitation, leaving Cu-Si-rich residual water. The HCO₃ gate is the discriminator from malachite — same Cu source, different anion availability. Cl > 5000 ppm suppresses (CuCl complexes keep Cu mobile). Two textbook generations at Tsumeb (Keller 1977): pre-calcite needles and post-calcite gemmy prisms. Field discriminator vs. emerald: softer (H 5 vs. 7.5-8), denser (3.3 vs. 2.7), strong rhombohedral cleavage, much higher birefringence (visible doubling of back facets in a 5 mm crystal). Refs: research dossier 2026-05; Ribbe/Gibbs/Hamil 1977 Am. Min. 62:807 (structure); Keller 1977 MinRec 8(3) (Tsumeb paragenesis); Lombaard et al. 1986 (Tsumeb monograph); Anthony et al. Handbook of Mineralogy v.II.
dioptase._ingredients_note: v93 (2026-05-19): Cu ≥ 1, SiO₂ ≥ 10 per research dossier (Cu 1-50 ppm + SiO₂ 10-60 ppm in Tsumeb supergene fluid). Critical: CO₃ < 50 ppm (malachite/azurite outcompete above this) and Cl < 5000 ppm (CuCl complexes mobilize Cu away from precipitation). pH window 6.5-8.5, sweet spot 7.0-7.5 (carbonate-buffered Tsumeb 2nd oxidation zone). O₂ > 1.0 (fully oxidizing). Substrate priority: calcite > dolomite > chrysocolla > malachite (rare pseudomorph).

trace_ingredients.CO3: < 50 ppm gate — malachite/azurite win above this; the HCO₃ exhaustion gate is the defining mechanic
trace_ingredients.Cl: < 5000 ppm — high Cl forms CuCl complexes that mobilize Cu out of solution

dioptase.thermal_decomp_reaction: Dehydrates above ~120°C (loses structural H₂O). At higher T (> 200°C) converts to anhydrous Cu-silicate phases.

fluorescence.status: Non-fluorescent under SW/MW/LW UV. Cu²⁺ is a luminescence quencher — dioptase shows no characteristic emission.

twin_laws.name: contact_0001
twin_laws._source: Anthony Handbook v.II; Mindat dioptase habit. Trigonal {0001} contact twin occasionally reported in Tsumeb + Mindouli specimens. Data sparse — conservative p=0.005.

dioptase.acid_dissolution: Slow dissolution in dilute HCl/H₂SO₄ at pH < 5. Cu²⁺ + silicic acid released.

emerald_green.trigger: Cu²⁺ chromophore — the saturated bluish-emerald color is intrinsic; trace Mn/Fe darken slightly but rarely visible

bluish_green.trigger: near pH 8 + slight Cu deficit (Cu < 5 ppm) — paler bluish-green dioptase, less saturated

dioptase.audit_status: new v93 (2026-05-19). Tsumeb world-reference mineral — its absence was geologically untenable. Substrate priority: calcite > dolomite > chrysocolla > malachite (rare pseudomorph). Forms when prior malachite/azurite has locally exhausted carbonate (CO₃ < 50 ppm), leaving Cu-Si fluid. Three habit dispatch paths: short prismatic emerald (default, Tsumeb iconic), long prismatic acicular (Kaokoveld, high excess), druzy overgrowth on chrysocolla, rare calcite pseudomorph. Per research dossier 2026-05.
dioptase._late_stage_note: Late supergene — dioptase post-dates malachite/azurite (needs carbonate exhausted), pre-dates only chrysocolla and minor late calcite. propensity:0.7 matches the Tsumeb 2nd-oxidation-zone paragenetic position.

habit_variants.name: fine_grained_disseminated

metacinnabar.description: HgS (β-HgS) — isometric F-43m SPHALERITE-TYPE structure, the LOW-T cubic polymorph of cinnabar (α-HgS, trigonal P3₁21 helical chains). Hardness 3, density 7.65 g/cm³ (slightly less than cinnabar's 8.10 — cubic close-packing is less efficient than helical chains). IRON-BLACK TO GRAYISH-BLACK with metallic-submetallic luster on fresh surfaces; streak BLACK (diagnostic vs cinnabar's SCARLET streak — the single most striking visual discriminator). Brittle to sectile. Opaque in thin section (cinnabar transmits red; metacinnabar doesn't). Polymorph relations: Potter & Barnes 1978 Econ. Geol. 73:282 established the equilibrium inversion at 344 ± 2°C — above, cinnabar is thermodynamically stable; below, metacinnabar is metastable. BUT metacinnabar nucleates more readily than cinnabar from aqueous sulfide solutions at T < 200°C because the cubic close-packing requires less ordering than cinnabar's helical chains (the kinetic vs thermodynamic divergence). White & Roberson 1962 GSA SP 73 documented metacinnabar at Sulphur Bank precipitating directly at 60-90°C spring waters. Conversion metacinnabar → cinnabar at low T is reconstructive + slow (10³-10⁵ years at 80°C natural residence; effectively quenched at surface T). Required reagents: dissolved Hg²⁺ + reduced S + acidic (pH 1-6.5) + cooling/dilution. Fe/Zn impurities (>5 ppm) stabilize the cubic structure. Type localities Almadén Spain + McDermitt Nevada + Sulphur Bank California. Substrate priority: cinnabar (epitactic, both share Hg-S bonds, the Sulphur Bank surface texture) > opal sinter > native_sulfur. Refs: research dossier 2026-05; White & Roberson 1962; Potter & Barnes 1978; Bailey 1959 USGS Bull. 1148; Dickson & Tunell 1959 Am. J. Sci. 257:341; Anthony et al. Handbook of Mineralogy v.I.
metacinnabar._ingredients_note: v101 (2026-05-19): Hg ≥ 1.0, S ≥ 50. CRITICAL: O₂ < 0.8 (stricter than cinnabar's 1.0 — metacinnabar is the more O₂-reactive polymorph, weathers faster at the surface). T 5-200°C (strict low-T window vs cinnabar's broader range). pH 1.0-6.5 (acidic-sulfide regime). Fe/Zn traces > 5 ppm stabilize the cubic structure (factor 1.1 each).

trace_ingredients.Fe: stabilizes cubic structure; common substitution > 5 ppm gives factor 1.1
trace_ingredients.Zn: same — Fe + Zn impurities are why natural metacinnabar persists indefinitely at low T
trace_ingredients.Se: Se substitution toward tiemannite (HgSe) — continuous solid solution

metacinnabar.thermal_decomp_reaction: Oxidative dissolution at O₂ > 1.0: faster than cinnabar (sulfide → sulfate + Hg vapor or HgCl). Polymorph inversion to cinnabar above 344°C dry (Potter & Barnes 1978); reconstructive + slow at low T (geological timescales).

fluorescence.status: Non-fluorescent — opaque cubic sulfide.

metacinnabar._twin_laws_note: Intentionally empty — metacinnabar (cubic F4̄3m, HgS) occurs almost exclusively as massive/sooty replacement of cinnabar, low-T supergene crusts, or fine-grained black coatings. The habit code 'massive_sooty' signals non-individual at vugg-scale; no documented growth twin law. v137 sulfide batch.
metacinnabar.acid_dissolution: Sparingly soluble in dilute acid; aqua regia dissolves.

iron_black.trigger: default — iron-black to dark grayish-black metallic, CONTRAST with cinnabar's scarlet

metacinnabar.audit_status: new v101 (2026-05-19). Black cubic HgS polymorph; the Sulphur Bank surface coating signature. Substrate priority: cinnabar (epitactic) > opal sinter > native_sulfur. Per research dossier 2026-05.
metacinnabar._late_stage_note: Late surface coating in hot-spring systems — the latest+coolest HgS phase.

habit_variants.trigger: on cinnabar/native_sulfur substrate — hot-spring sinter apron embedding sulfides, mound morphology

habit_variants.name: botryoidal_mound
habit_variants.trigger: excess > 1.4 — botryoidal grape-cluster mounds with reniform terminations (geyserite + sinter)

habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ — reniform layered crusts with concentric banding

habit_variants.trigger: low σ — nodular massive, glassy conchoidal fracture
