habit_variants.trigger: very low σ — thin film coating substrate

opal.description: SiO₂·nH₂O — AMORPHOUS-to-short-range-ordered SILICA MINERALOID (no fixed composition; n typically 3-21 wt% H₂O, most commonly 6-10%). Hardness 5.5-6.5 (lower than quartz at 7 — diagnostic), density 1.9-2.3 g/cm³ (varies with hydration), refractive index 1.40-1.46. Color: white/gray/yellow/brown/red/green/blue depending on impurities (Fe → red-brown, organics → black, Cr/Ni → green); 'precious opal' shows play-of-color from regular ~150-400 nm SiO₂ sphere packing (Sanders 1964 Nature 204:1151); 'common opal' lacks this ordering. THREE STRUCTURAL VARIETIES (Jones & Segnit 1971 J. Geol. Soc. Aust. 18:57; Langer & Flörke 1974 Fortschr. Mineral. 52:17): opal-A (amorphous, broad hump near 4 Å, no Bragg peaks); opal-CT (disordered cristobalite-tridymite stacking); opal-C (sharper cristobalite peaks). Brittle CONCHOIDAL fracture (textbook reference example). NO cleavage. Forms at T < 100°C from silica-supersaturated waters (>200-300 ppm SiO₂ at 25°C per Fournier 1977 Geothermics 5:41), pH 7-9 optimal alkaline silica regime. DIAGENESIS LADDER: opal-A → opal-CT → opal-C → chalcedony → quartz; each step releases structural water + requires aging time / temperature. Engine tracks crystal._diagenesis_stage flag based on T + crystal_zones.length. Hot-spring sinter is the canonical habit — Yellowstone (geyserite), Steamboat Springs Nevada, Sulphur Bank California, Wairakei NZ all build sinter aprons from opal-A. Botryoidal + reniform + nodular + stalactitic forms. SPECTACULAR replacement habits: opalized wood (cell-by-cell), opalized bone, opalized shells. The 'universal binder' of hot-spring deposits — nucleates on rocks, plants, microbial mats (thermophilic cyanobacteria templating spheroidal opal at Yellowstone, well-documented), prior opal layers, sulfide grains. Field discriminator: H 5.5-6.5 (lower than quartz 7), conchoidal fracture without crystal faces, lower density + RI than quartz. Refs: research dossier 2026-05; Jones & Segnit 1971; Langer & Flörke 1974; White & Roberson 1962 GSA SP 73 (Sulphur Bank sinter); Fournier 1977 Geothermics 5:41 (silica solubility); Sanders 1964; Williams & Crerar 1985 J. Sediment. Petrol. 55:312 (diagenesis kinetics); Anthony et al. Handbook of Mineralogy v.II.
opal._ingredients_note: v101 (2026-05-19): SiO₂ ≥ 200 (Fournier 1977 amorphous silica solubility threshold at 25°C; rises to ~370 ppm at 100°C). pH 6.5-10, sweet spot 7-9 alkaline. T 5-100°C, sweet spot 30-85°C (geyser sinter regime). Redox tolerant (any). Diagenesis ladder via crystal._diagenesis_stage: opal-A (T<50 + young crystal) / opal-CT (T<100 + aged) / opal-C (older).

trace_ingredients.Fe: red-brown color modifier

opal.thermal_decomp_reaction: Acid dissolution below pH 5. Above 150°C: diagenesis to chalcedony/quartz (opal-A → opal-CT → opal-C → chalcedony → quartz ladder). Each step releases structural water: A→CT ~3-5 wt% H₂O; CT→C ~2-3%; C→chalcedony ~1%.

fluorescence.status: Most opal non-fluorescent. Some precious opal varieties show weak white/blue under SW UV from organic inclusions; common opal generally inert.

opal._twin_laws_note: Intentionally empty — opal is amorphous hydrated silica (opal-A is fully non-crystalline; opal-CT/opal-C have nano-crystalline silica spheres but no euhedral form). No crystal structure → no twinning. Per Iler 1979 The Chemistry of Silica + Anthony Handbook v.II opal section.
opal.acid_dissolution: Dissolves slowly in dilute HF + strong alkali (>pH 10); also dissolves in hot acidic groundwater.

common_white_brown.trigger: default — white to gray to brown common opal, hot-spring sinter color

play_of_color.trigger: precious opal — internal play-of-color from ordered sphere packing (sub-mm regular spheres)

opal.audit_status: new v101 (2026-05-19). Hot-spring sinter mineraloid + universal binder. Three structural varieties (A/CT/C) tracked via _diagenesis_stage flag. Substrate priority: cinnabar > native_sulfur > pyrite > vug wall (nucleates on anything in effluent path).
opal._late_stage_note: Hot-spring sinter is usually the FIRST phase deposited (the platform on which everything else grows). propensity:0.3 reflects host-mineral position.

habit_variants.name: on_galena
habit_variants.trigger: directly on galena substrate — the textbook habit, often with anglesite rind between

habit_variants.name: elongate_prismatic
habit_variants.trigger: excess > 1.2 — elongate [010] prisms 1-20 mm, {100} tabular faces, the Tsumeb 'blue blade'

linarite.description: PbCu(SO₄)(OH)₂ — monoclinic P2₁/m DEEP AZURE TO ULTRAMARINE BLUE (some authors call it the most intensely blue mineral known). Hardness 2.5, density 5.30-5.35 g/cm³ (HEAVY — Pb is diagnostic in hand). Streak pale blue. Adamantine to vitreous, subadamantine on cleavages. Perfect {100} cleavage with {001} interrupted — prisms split lengthwise. Forms strictly supergene at <60°C requiring SIMULTANEOUS oxidation of galena AND a Cu-sulfide (chalcocite/covellite/chalcopyrite) in close proximity (mm to cm). pH 4-7, sweet spot 5-6. ΣCO2 must be LOW (< 10⁻⁴ mol/L, < 6 ppm HCO3⁻; CO3:SO4 < 0.3 in fluid) — any carbonate diverts to caledonite/leadhillite. Cl < 100 ppm (otherwise boleite group wins). Often mistaken for azurite (same color range) but linarite is HEAVIER (5.3 vs 3.8), has NO HCl fizz (sulfate-hydroxide vs carbonate), sits directly on galena (azurite never directly on galena). Type locality Leadhills-Wanlockhead Scotland (Wilson & Dunn 1978 MinRec 9:251). Mid-to-late supergene Pb-Cu oxidation cycle. Substrate priority: galena (primary substrate, with anglesite rind) > anglesite > chalcocite > cerussite. NON-FLUORESCENT (Pb²⁺ + Cu²⁺ quench). Refs: research dossier 2026-05; Effenberger 1987 N. Jb. Mineral. Mh. (structure); Smith 1994 'Mineralogy of Tsumeb'; Wilson & Dunn 1978; Williams 1990 'Oxide Zone Geochemistry'; Graeme 1981 MinRec 12:258 (Bisbee); Dana 7th v.II; Handbook of Mineralogy.
linarite._ingredients_note: v100 (2026-05-19): Pb ≥ 30, Cu ≥ 10, S ≥ 50. CRITICAL: CO3:SO4 < 0.3 (above → caledonite/leadhillite). Cl < 100 (above → boleite group). pH 4-7, sweet spot 5-6. sulfateRedoxAvailable threshold 0.5.
linarite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Acid dissolution below pH 3.5; cycling humidity → may alter to brochantite + cerussite.

fluorescence.status: Non-fluorescent — both Pb²⁺ and Cu²⁺ quench.

linarite.acid_dissolution: Dissolves in dilute HCl below pH 3.5.

deep_azure.trigger: default — deep azure to ultramarine blue (the most intensely blue mineral known per some authors)

linarite.audit_status: new v100 (2026-05-19). Closes the Pb-Cu sulfate-hydroxide endmember (Leadhills type locality). Substrate priority: galena > anglesite > chalcocite > cerussite. Per research dossier 2026-05.
linarite._late_stage_note: Mid-to-late supergene Pb-Cu oxidation cycle. Forms after primary galena+Cu-sulfide oxidation.

habit_variants.name: epitactic_tuft_on_linarite
habit_variants.trigger: on linarite substrate — acicular tufts capping linarite prism cores (parallel [001]), the Tsumeb classic texture

habit_variants.name: prismatic_acicular
habit_variants.trigger: excess > 1.2 — elongate [001] prisms 1-10 mm (30 mm rare at Tsumeb), blue-green

habit_variants.name: divergent_groups
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ — radial tufts on galena/anglesite

habit_variants.name: tabular_aggregate
habit_variants.trigger: low σ — tabular on (010), compact aggregates

caledonite.description: Pb₅Cu₂(CO₃)(SO₄)₃(OH)₆ — orthorhombic Pmcn distinctive BLUE-GREEN TO VERDIGRIS-GREEN (bluer than malachite, greener than linarite). Hardness 2.5-3, density 5.60-5.76 g/cm³. Streak greenish-white. Resinous to vitreous, slightly pearly on cleavage. Perfect {010} cleavage. Forms supergene at <60°C requiring BOTH carbonate AND sulfate in fluid (CO3:SO4 ratio ~0.3-1 sub-equimolar to equimolar; the discriminator between linarite (CO3 < 0.3) and leadhillite (CO3 > 2)). pH 5-7, sweet spot 5.5-6.5. Like linarite, needs simultaneous galena + Cu-sulfide oxidation, but with carbonate from limestone host buffering. Often forms epitactic overgrowths on linarite (parallel [001] axes) — the linarite prism core capped by caledonite tuft is the Tsumeb signature (Smith 1994 figs. p. 110-112). Type locality Leadhills, Scotland (Brooke 1820, named after Caledonia — the Latin name for Scotland). Substrate priority: linarite (epitactic — strongest preference) > anglesite > partially-oxidized galena. NON-FLUORESCENT (Cu²⁺ quench). Field discriminator: blue-GREEN (the only blue-green of the Pb-Cu sulfate trio; linarite is pure blue, leadhillite white-grey). Weak HCl fizz (carbonate present, but less than leadhillite). SG 5.6 distinguishes from malachite (4.0). Refs: research dossier 2026-05; Giacovazzo et al. 1973 Acta Cryst. B29:1986 (structure); Brooke 1820 Annals of Philosophy 16:193 (original description); Wilson & Dunn 1978 MinRec 9:251 (Leadhills); Smith 1994 Mineralogy of Tsumeb; Williams 1990 Oxide Zone Geochemistry; Handbook of Mineralogy.
caledonite._ingredients_note: v100 (2026-05-19): Pb ≥ 30, Cu ≥ 10, S ≥ 50, CO3 ≥ 5. CRITICAL: CO3:SO4 between 0.1 and 2.0 (sweet spot 0.3-1.0). Below 0.1 → linarite, above 2.0 → leadhillite. Cl < 50. pH 5-7, sweet spot 5.5-6.5.
caledonite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Slow weathering to malachite + anglesite + cerussite over geological time. More stable than leadhillite under humidity cycling.

fluorescence.status: Non-fluorescent — Cu²⁺ quenches.

caledonite.acid_dissolution: Dissolves in dilute HCl below pH 4.

blue_green.trigger: default — distinctive blue-green to verdigris-green (the only blue-green of the Pb-Cu sulfate trio)

caledonite.audit_status: new v100 (2026-05-19). Caledonia type locality. Often epitactic on linarite — the Tsumeb textbook texture. Substrate priority: linarite > anglesite > galena.
caledonite._late_stage_note: Slightly later than linarite — caledonite typically forms on linarite as carbonate activity rises during continued limestone-host reaction.

habit_variants.name: tablet_on_Pb_oxide
habit_variants.trigger: on cerussite/anglesite/galena substrate — pearly white tablets on Pb-oxide host

habit_variants.name: pseudo_hexagonal_tablet
habit_variants.trigger: excess > 1.2 — pseudo-hexagonal tablets on {001}, polysynthetic twin striations on basal plates, 2-30 mm Leadhills classic

habit_variants.name: pearly_cleavable_mass
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ — pearly cleavable masses, flexible thin laminae

habit_variants.name: massive_granular_fill
habit_variants.trigger: low σ — massive granular vug filling

leadhillite.description: Pb₄(SO₄)(CO₃)₂(OH)₂ — monoclinic P2₁/a, PSEUDO-TRIGONAL (the pseudo-symmetry produces characteristic HEXAGONAL-LOOKING tablets but true symmetry is monoclinic). Hardness 2.5, density 6.55 g/cm³ (VERY heavy, distinctive in hand). Color colorless to white to pale yellow/green/grey; the pearly luster on {001} gives a milky appearance. Streak white. PERFECT {001} cleavage — peels in pearly sheets like mica. Polysynthetic twinning visible as striations on basal plates. Two polymorphs: leadhillite (monoclinic, common ambient) + susannite (trigonal P-3, higher-T variant); inversion near 80°C, reversible. METASTABLE at ambient — slowly transforms to anglesite + cerussite under humidity cycling (Pollard et al. 1992 Mineralog. Mag. 56:359). Museum specimens degrade over decades unless kept dry. Forms supergene at <60°C with mixed carbonate + sulfate; CO3:SO4 ratio > 2 (Mereiter, Völlenkle & Preisinger 1979 TMPM 26:79). pH 6-8, sweet spot 6.5-7.5 — the most alkaline of the trio. Cu must be LOW (< 50 ppm) — Cu inclusion drives to caledonite or linarite. Typical setting: galena oxidation in carbonate-host (limestone, dolostone) with well-buffered groundwater. Type locality Leadhills, Scotland (Beudant 1832; Wilson & Dunn 1978). Substrate priority: cerussite (epitactic) > anglesite > galena. NON-FLUORESCENT (Pb²⁺ quench; some specimens show weak yellow-orange SW UV from trace Mn²⁺, not diagnostic). Field discriminator: pearly white-grey tablets with mica-like cleavage + extremely high density (6.5+) + fizz weakly with WARM dilute HCl. The 'hexagonal-tablet but really monoclinic' habit is unmistakable. Refs: research dossier 2026-05; Steele et al. 1998 Mineralog. Mag. 62:451 (structure + polymorphism); Mereiter et al. 1979 TMPM 26:79; Pollard et al. 1992 Mineralog. Mag. 56:359 (alteration); Smith 1994 Mineralogy of Tsumeb; Wilson & Dunn 1978 MinRec 9:251 (Leadhills); Dana 7th v.II; Handbook of Mineralogy.
leadhillite._ingredients_note: v100 (2026-05-19): Pb ≥ 50, S ≥ 30, CO3 ≥ 30. CRITICAL: CO3:SO4 > 1.5 (CO3-DOMINANT discriminator from linarite/caledonite). Cu < 50 (above → caledonite). pH 6-8, sweet spot 6.5-7.5 (most alkaline of trio). sulfateRedoxAvailable threshold 0.5.

trace_ingredients.Mn: trace gives weak yellow-orange SW UV; not diagnostic

leadhillite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Reversible inversion to susannite near 80°C (same composition, polymorph). Slow ambient alteration: leadhillite + H₂SO₄ → anglesite + cerussite (humidity cycling drives this metastable transformation; museum specimens degrade over decades).

fluorescence.status: Usually non-fluorescent; some specimens show weak yellow-orange SW UV from trace Mn²⁺ activator. Not diagnostic.

twin_laws.name: leadhillite_140_polysynthetic
twin_laws.trigger: polysynthetic twinning on (140) — visible as striations on basal plates, diagnostic of leadhillite

leadhillite.acid_dissolution: Dissolves in WARM dilute HCl (carbonate fizz weakly).

pearly_white.trigger: default — pearly white-grey, milky appearance from {001} cleavage luster

leadhillite.audit_status: new v100 (2026-05-19). Leadhills type locality, the carbonate-dominant + Cu-poor end of the Pb-Cu sulfate trio. Substrate priority: cerussite > anglesite > galena. Metastable — degrades to anglesite+cerussite over geological time.
leadhillite._late_stage_note: Late supergene + carbonate-buffered. propensity:0.85 reflects the late-stage CO3-rich oxidation pocket.

habit_variants.name: fracture_replacement_after_uraninite
habit_variants.trigger: on uraninite/pitchblende substrate — microcrystalline coating along fractures (the canonical Stieff 1955 mode)

habit_variants.name: colloform_band
habit_variants.trigger: excess > 1.0 — colloform banded intergrown with pitchblende

habit_variants.name: sooty_coating
habit_variants.trigger: low σ — fine-grained black-tarry coating

coffinite.description: USiO₄·nH₂O — tetragonal I4₁/amd (ZIRCON-isostructural), 0 < n < ~0.25. Hardness 5-6 when crystalline (4-5 metamict); density 5.1 g/cm³ calc., 4.6-5.0 measured (hydration + metamictization lower it). Color BLACK to brownish-black to dark grey; streak dark brown to black. Brittle conchoidal fracture; metamict aggregates sectile-soft. Adamantine-greasy luster fresh, dull-earthy when oxidized. NO FLUORESCENCE — U is U⁴⁺ (lacks the uranyl UO₂²⁺ chromophore that fluoresces in U(VI) species like uranophane). PRIMARY hydrothermal mineral (not strictly supergene), forms 100-300°C in REDUCING fluids (logfO₂ < –40 at 200°C, below uraninite-haematite buffer) with aqueous SiO₂ above quartz saturation (Finch & Murakami 1999 RIMG 38:91-179 pp. 116-119). Replaces uraninite where pore-water silica activity rises (Pointer et al. 1988); also primary in roll-front sandstone-hosted U deposits. Critically, ΣCO2 must be LOW (< 10⁻³ mol/L) — high carbonate mobilizes U(IV) as uranyl-carbonate complexes and dissolves coffinite/uraninite. Phosphate < 1 ppm or ningyoite (U(IV) phosphate) competes. Type locality Cane Springs Canyon, Utah (Stieff et al. 1955 Science 121:608). Substrate priority: uraninite (replacement) > pyrite + organic matter (sandstone roll-front host) > vug wall. Refs: research dossier 2026-05; Stieff, Stern, Sherwood 1955; Finch & Murakami 1999; Burns 2005 Can. Mineral. 43:1839; Janeczek & Ewing 1992 J. Nucl. Mater. 190:157; Pointer et al. 1988 Mineralog. Mag. 52:553; Dana 7th v.IV (silicates); MinRec Schneeberg issue v.19 (1988).
coffinite._ingredients_note: v99 (2026-05-19): U ≥ 1, SiO₂ ≥ 60 (quartz saturation at 100°C). CRITICAL: O₂ < 0.3 (reducing — U must be U(IV)). CO₃ < 60 ppm (high CO3 → uranyl carbonate complexes mobilize U). P < 1 (P → ningyoite U(IV) phosphate). pH 5-8.
coffinite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Oxidative dissolution at O₂ > 0.5: U(IV) → U(VI) uranyl, releases (UO₂)²⁺ to feed uranophane / autunite / torbernite supergene cascade. Highly radioactive — emits alpha + gamma.

fluorescence.status: NON-FLUORESCENT. U(IV) does NOT have the (UO₂)²⁺ vibronic chromophore that gives U(VI) phases their characteristic yellow-green luminescence. Diagnostic distinction from uranophane (both look superficially similar but uranophane fluoresces bright yellow-green, coffinite does not).

coffinite._twin_laws_note: Intentionally empty — coffinite typically occurs as fracture-replacement, colloform bands, or sooty coatings; rarely as euhedral individuals. The habit_variants (fracture_replacement_after_uraninite, colloform_band, sooty_coating) all describe non-euhedral textures. Twin behavior in the rare euhedral cases is undocumented in the literature.

black.trigger: default — black to brownish-black, the U(IV) silicate signature

coffinite.audit_status: new v99 (2026-05-19). Primary U(IV) silicate, the uraninite-replacement phase along fractures + grain boundaries. Highly radioactive (~70% U by mass). Indistinguishable from pitchblende without XRD or microprobe — slight density difference (5.1 vs 10.5 for stoichiometric UO₂) is the field check. Per research dossier 2026-05.
coffinite._late_stage_note: Primary U(IV) — among the earliest U phases. propensity:0.1.

habit_variants.name: oxidation_front_replacement
habit_variants.trigger: on uraninite/coffinite substrate — acicular sprays at oxidation front, the canonical replacement habit

habit_variants.name: acicular_starburst
habit_variants.trigger: excess > 1.2 — radiating needles 0.1-5 mm, star-shaped clusters in vugs

habit_variants.name: radiating_fibrous_puffs
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ — silky sub-mm needles forming hemispherical 'puff' aggregates

habit_variants.name: powdery_yellow_coating
habit_variants.trigger: low σ — earthy yellow coating on uraninite weathering surfaces

uranophane.description: Ca(UO₂)₂(SiO₃)₂(OH)₂·5H₂O — monoclinic P2₁, uranyl-silicate chain structure (uranyl-silicate sheets linked by Ca + 5 H₂O). Hardness 2.5, density 3.83-3.90 g/cm³. Color BRILLIANT lemon to canary yellow; weathered surfaces straw-yellow. Streak pale yellow. Vitreous on fresh crystal faces, silky on fibrous aggregates, dull-earthy on powdery coatings. DIAGNOSTIC BRIGHT YELLOW-GREEN to apple-green FLUORESCENCE under both SW (254 nm) AND LW (365 nm) UV — persistent, not phosphorescent. The fluorescence is from the (UO₂)²⁺ uranyl ion vibronic structure (Burns 2005) and intensity often exceeds autunite's under SW. Forms SUPERGENE strictly at <50°C from oxidative weathering of uraninite/coffinite when Ca²⁺ AND dissolved SiO₂ are both available. pH window 5-8; above 8 carbonate-uranyl species (liebigite, andersonite) dominate; below 5 uranyl-sulfates (johannite, zippeite) win. Requires logfO₂ > –20 (atmospheric). Boltwoodite (K), sklodowskite (Mg), kasolite (Pb) are the cation analogs; uranophane wins when Ca is the available cation. The classic late-supergene uranyl silicate — replaces uraninite/coffinite/pitchblende along fractures + at the oxidation front. Type localities Schneeberg + Jáchymov (alpha form), Marysvale UT, Colorado Plateau. Acicular fibrous habit + bright yellow-green UV fluorescence is the unmistakable field combo. Refs: research dossier 2026-05; Ginderow 1988 Acta Cryst. C44:421 (structure); Finch & Murakami 1999 RIMG 38:91-179 (canonical paragenesis chart fig. 12); Burns 2005 Can. Mineral. 43:1839 (uranyl crystal chemistry); Stohl & Smith 1981 Am. Mineral. 66:610 (uranyl-silicate family); Handbook of Mineralogy 8th ed. v.II; MinRec Schneeberg issue v.19 (1988).
uranophane._ingredients_note: v99 (2026-05-19): U ≥ 0.5, Ca ≥ 20, SiO₂ ≥ 30. Critical: O₂ ≥ 0.5 (oxidizing, U(VI)). CO₃ < 60 (above → liebigite/andersonite). S < 1000 (high SO4 → johannite/zippeite). P < 5 (P → autunite/torbernite). pH 5-8, sweet spot 6.0-7.5. Quartz tolerant (saturation not required — amorphous silica or chalcedony source typical).
uranophane.thermal_decomp_reaction: Acid dissolution at pH < 4.5 — uranyl + Ca + Si released. Dehydration above ~100°C transforms toward dehydrated uranophane variants.

fluorescence.status: DIAGNOSTIC BRIGHT YELLOW-GREEN UV-FLUORESCENCE under both SW (254 nm) + LW (365 nm), persistent. The uranyl (UO₂)²⁺ vibronic chromophore. Intensity often exceeds autunite's; distinguishes uranophane from non-fluorescent yellow Fe-oxides (jarosite, limonite).

twin_laws.name: contact_001
twin_laws._source: Anthony Handbook v.IV uranophane section. Monoclinic {001} contact twin occasionally reported in radiating-acicular puffs at Colorado Plateau + Schneeberg. Rare; conservative p=0.01.

uranophane.acid_dissolution: Dissolves in dilute HCl below pH 4.5.

lemon_yellow.trigger: default — brilliant lemon to canary yellow

uranophane.audit_status: new v99 (2026-05-19). The classic late-supergene uranyl silicate — Schneeberg/Jáchymov + Marysvale + Colorado Plateau canonical. UV fluorescence is the diagnostic field test. Substrate priority: uraninite (replacement) > coffinite (replacement) > fluorite/chalcedony adjacent to U-primaries.
uranophane._late_stage_note: Latest U supergene — replaces uraninite + coffinite at the oxidation front. propensity:0.9.

habit_variants.name: fan_sheaves_on_smithsonite
habit_variants.trigger: on smithsonite substrate — radiating fan sheaves, the Tsumeb 'bowtie' habit

habit_variants.name: botryoidal_blue
habit_variants.trigger: excess > 1.2 + Cu trace — botryoidal-stalactitic crusts with fibrous radial structure, the Mapimi blue (trace Cu) variety

habit_variants.name: fan_sheaves
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ — radiating fan-shaped sprays + sheaves of bladed acicular crystals elongate on c

habit_variants.name: drusy_lining
habit_variants.trigger: low σ — drusy linings of vugs, colorless to pale blue-green

hemimorphite.description: Zn₄Si₂O₇(OH)₂·H₂O — orthorhombic SOROSILICATE, space group Imm2 (POLAR — the hemimorphic class, the textbook example of polar crystal symmetry: c-axis terminates differently top vs. bottom). Hardness 4.5-5, density 3.40-3.50 g/cm³. Color colorless to white to pale blue-green (Mapimi blue is from minor Cu²⁺); silky-vitreous on fibrous aggregates, pearly on cleavage. Forms STRICTLY supergene at <50°C in Zn-supergene zones with SiO₂ ≥ 200 ppm and CO₃ < SiO₂ (otherwise smithsonite wins). pH 5.5-8, sweet spot 6.0-7.5. The CO₃:SiO₂ ratio is the fork from smithsonite (smithsonite > 1, hemimorphite < 1). Above ~250°C dehydrates to willemite + quartz. PYROELECTRIC + piezoelectric — historical name source (Greek hemi-morphos, half-form). Type localities Tsumeb (world reference fan sheaves), Mapimi/Ojuela Mexico (blue botryoidal), Bisbee, Sterling Hill NJ, Wenshan Yunnan, Trepca Kosovo. Late in supergene Zn oxidation — post-smithsonite (Boni & Mondillo 2015 fig. 4). Substrate priority: smithsonite (epitactic, similar Zn-O coordination) > Fe-Mn gossan > relict sphalerite (in-situ replacement). Field discriminator: hemimorphic terminations diagnostic when visible; pyroelectric test (warm crystal picks up dust on one end). Distinguishes from prehnite (denser, hemimorphic vs. centrosymmetric). Refs: research dossier 2026-05; Hitzman et al. 2003 Econ. Geol. 98:685-714 (esp. fig. 9 silica-carbonate stability); Boni & Mondillo 2015 Ore Geol. Rev. 67:208-233; Takahashi 1960 Econ. Geol. 55:1084; Dana 7th v.II p.508; Handbook of Mineralogy v.II.
hemimorphite._ingredients_note: v98 (2026-05-19): Zn ≥ 10, SiO₂ ≥ 200 (Hitzman 2003 fig. 9 silica activity threshold for hemimorphite over smithsonite at 25°C). CRITICAL: CO₃ < SiO₂ (above → smithsonite wins). pH 5.5-8.0, sweet spot 6.0-7.5. O₂ ≥ 0.5 (oxidizing supergene). T strictly < 50°C. Cu trace gives the Mapimi blue color.

trace_ingredients.Cu: trace gives Mapimi-style pale-blue color via adsorbed or vacancy-substituted Cu²⁺

hemimorphite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Dehydration above ~250°C: 2 Zn₄Si₂O₇(OH)₂·H₂O → 4 Zn₂SiO₄ (willemite) + 2 H₂O + SiO₂. Acid dissolution below pH 5.

fluorescence.status: Weak inconsistent fluorescence — do NOT rely on UV for ID. Pyroelectric/piezoelectric tests are the diagnostic physical-property checks.

twin_laws.name: contact_001
twin_laws._source: Dana 8th ed. hemimorphite section. Orthorhombic {001} contact twin reported in fan-sheaf specimens on smithsonite substrates. Rare; conservative p=0.01.

hemimorphite.acid_dissolution: Dissolves in dilute HCl below pH 5.

colorless_white.trigger: default — colorless to white pure Zn end

mapimi_blue.trigger: Cu > 5 ppm — pale blue-green from Cu²⁺ trace (the Mapimi/Wenshan signature)

hemimorphite.audit_status: new v98 (2026-05-19). Closes the Zn-silicate supergene slot (Tsumeb fan sheaves are world reference). Substrate priority: smithsonite > sphalerite (replacement) > goethite/gossan. Hemimorphic polar crystals are the diagnostic visual signature.
hemimorphite._late_stage_note: Late Zn supergene — sphalerite → smithsonite → hemimorphite (Boni & Mondillo 2015 paragenesis).

habit_variants.name: hexagonal_prism_franklin
habit_variants.trigger: T ≥ 500°C primary/metamorphic — hexagonal prisms terminated by rhombohedra (Franklin classic), troostite Mn-rich variety reddish

habit_variants.name: pseudomorph_after_sphalerite_skorpion
habit_variants.trigger: on sphalerite substrate — replacement pseudomorph, the Skorpion supergene nonsulfide habit

habit_variants.name: fibrous_radiating_skorpion
habit_variants.trigger: excess > 1.0 supergene mode — fibrous-radiating green-yellow

habit_variants.name: massive_granular
habit_variants.trigger: low σ — massive granular intergrown with franklinite/zincite (Franklin ore mode)

willemite.description: Zn₂SiO₄ — trigonal R-3̄ NESOSILICATE, PHENAKITE-GROUP (isostructural with phenakite Be₂SiO₄). Hardness 5.5, density 3.89-4.20 g/cm³ (highest of the Zn supergene triad — useful field clue). Color: pure willemite colorless; Mn²⁺-substituted troostite variety apple-green to yellow-green to honey-yellow (Skorpion) to red-brown (Franklin troostite); orange-red rare. Dominated by Mn²⁺ + Fe²⁺ trace substitution in Zn site. FAMOUSLY UV-FLUORESCENT BRIGHT GREEN under SW (254 nm) UV — Mn²⁺ activator, even traces (~0.1%) produce strong response. The Franklin 'fluorescent rocks' trade rests on this single fact. Bimodal formation: PRIMARY/metamorphic at 500-600°C in the Franklin-Sterling NJ Zn-Mn skarn (Frondel 1972 troostite); OR SUPERGENE 'nonsulfide' at 50-200°C in Skorpion-Vazante-Beltana-style deposits (Hitzman et al. 2003; Brugger et al. 2003 Skorpion thermometry). The two modes are kinetically + thermodynamically distinct — supergene mode replaces sphalerite directly when SiO₂ activity is sustained high; primary mode crystallizes in Zn-Mn protolith metamorphism. Type locality Stolberg, Belgium. Substrate priority: sphalerite (supergene replacement) > smithsonite > vug wall. Refs: research dossier 2026-05; Hitzman et al. 2003 (Skorpion type section); Boni & Mondillo 2015 (nonsulfide Zn synthesis); Brugger et al. 2003; Frondel 1972 (Franklin troostite); Dana 7th v.II p.499; Handbook of Mineralogy v.III.
willemite._ingredients_note: v98 (2026-05-19): Zn ≥ 50, SiO₂ ≥ 100. pH 6.0-9.0 (wider than hemimorphite). O₂ ≥ 0.3. BIMODAL T sweet spots: 80-150°C (Skorpion supergene) OR 500-600°C (Franklin primary). Intermediate T (150-500) gets factor 0.6 (rarer regime). Anhydrous structure means CO₃-tolerance is broader than hemimorphite; willemite can fire above smithsonite's CO₃ window in elevated-T regimes (Skorpion atypical CO₃-rich willemite per Hitzman 2003 fig. 10).

trace_ingredients.Mn: activator for bright green SW-UV fluorescence; > 0.005 fluoresces strongly
trace_ingredients.Fe: color modifier — Fe-rich varieties darker, less fluorescent

willemite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Stable across the full geological T range up to ~1500°C. No dissolution path above pH 4. The terminal Zn-silicate phase.

fluorescence.status: DIAGNOSTIC bright green SW-UV (254 nm) fluorescence, often with persistent phosphorescence at Franklin/Sterling. Mn²⁺ in Zn²⁺ site is the activator; even traces produce strong response. Fe quenches in Fe-rich varieties.

twin_laws.name: contact_0001
twin_laws._source: Anthony Handbook v.II willemite section; Dana 8th ed. Trigonal {0001} basal contact twin reported very rarely in Franklin/Sterling Hill specimens. Data sparse — conservative p=0.005.

willemite.acid_dissolution: Slow in dilute HCl below pH 4.

green_yellow.trigger: default Mn-trace — apple-green to honey-yellow (Skorpion supergene mode)

troostite_red.trigger: Mn > 50 ppm + T ≥ 500 — troostite Franklin variety, reddish-brown

colorless.trigger: pure Zn end (Mn < 0.005 ppm) — colorless, weakly fluorescent

willemite.audit_status: new v98 (2026-05-19). Phenakite-group Zn-silicate, bimodal formation (primary metamorphic OR supergene). The DIAGNOSTIC UV-fluorescent Franklin/Sterling reference mineral. Substrate priority: sphalerite > smithsonite > vug wall.
willemite._late_stage_note: Bimodal — primary Franklin willemite is pre-everything; Skorpion-supergene is mid-stage replacement of sphalerite. propensity:0.5 averages the two modes.

habit_variants.name: alteration_after_smithsonite
habit_variants.trigger: on smithsonite substrate — chalky crust in-situ alteration product (Hitzman 2003)

habit_variants.name: cave_floor_chalky_coating
habit_variants.trigger: excess > 1.5 — centimeter-thick chalky white coatings, the Iglesiente Sardinia cave-floor signature

habit_variants.name: spherulitic_crust
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ — spherulitic crusts ~0.5-5 mm spherules with radiating fibrous internal structure

habit_variants.name: botryoidal_film
habit_variants.trigger: low σ — thin botryoidal film on weathered Zn ore

hydrozincite.description: Zn₅(CO₃)₂(OH)₆ — monoclinic C2/m carbonate-hydroxide. Hardness 2-2.5 (soft — fingernail-scratchable, diagnostic), density 3.5-4.0 g/cm³ (variable with porosity). Color CHALK-WHITE to creamy white, occasionally pale yellow or pink (trace Cd or Co). Forms strictly low-T (<30°C) in alkaline (pH 7.5-9) supergene Zn zones with high CO₃ and LOW SiO₂ (<50 — otherwise hemimorphite wins). Suppressed by Cu (Cu/(Cu+Zn) < 0.15, otherwise aurichalcite wins the Zn-Cu carbonate-hydroxide solid solution). The LATEST + COOLEST member of the Zn supergene suite (Hitzman 2003 fig. 4). Wet-dry cycling (humid + evaporative seasons) produces the textbook cave-floor coatings. Type locality Iglesiente, Sardinia (Naracauli + Monteponi); also Mežica Slovenia (rare crystals), Tsumeb (minor on smithsonite), Broken Hill NSW. The Naracauli cyanobacteria-mediated biofilm hydrozincite (Preisig et al. 2014) is independently important as a natural bioremediator of Zn-contaminated drainage. Field discriminator: pale-blue SW-UV fluorescence (DEFECT-related, not Mn²⁺ activator like willemite); H 2 (scratches with fingernail, distinguishing from smithsonite H=5); SG 3.5-4 lighter than smithsonite (4.4-4.5). Often mistaken for calcite efflorescence at hand-specimen scale — UV resolves it. Refs: research dossier 2026-05; Hitzman et al. 2003 (Table 2 supergene Zn stabilities); Boni & Mondillo 2015 (Iglesiente + Naracauli sections); Preisig et al. 2014 (biofilm Naracauli); Ghaderi et al. 1999 (Mežica crystals); Dana 7th v.II p.252; Handbook of Mineralogy v.II.
hydrozincite._ingredients_note: v98 (2026-05-19): Zn ≥ 5, CO₃ ≥ 100. CRITICAL: SiO₂ < 50 (hemimorphite wins above), Cu/(Cu+Zn) < 0.15 (aurichalcite wins above), high sulfate (S > 100 in oxidizing fluid) suppresses (gypsum + Zn-sulfates win). pH 7.0-9.5, sweet spot 7.5-8.5. O₂ ≥ 0.5 (oxidizing supergene). T strictly < 30°C (sharp).

trace_ingredients.Cu: small Cu allowed; high Cu (> 15% of Zn+Cu) routes to aurichalcite

hydrozincite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Acid dissolution at pH < 6 — reverts toward smithsonite + Zn²⁺. Thermal dehydration at higher T converts to smithsonite + ZnO.

fluorescence.status: DIAGNOSTIC pale-blue SW-UV fluorescence (defect-related, not Mn²⁺ activator like willemite). Distinguishes from calcite (variable orange/pink/none) and aragonite (green-blue but different intensity).

hydrozincite._twin_laws_note: Intentionally empty — hydrozincite (monoclinic, Zn5(CO3)2(OH)6) habit code 'spherulitic_crust' signals non-euhedral aggregate. Forms white spherulitic + reniform coatings in supergene Zn zones (Mendip Hills, Linares Spain, Sardinian deposits). No individual euhedral crystals to twin. v141 final batch.
hydrozincite.acid_dissolution: Dissolves readily in dilute acid — Zn + CO3 + H2O released.

chalk_white.trigger: default — chalk-white to creamy white, the characteristic cave-floor color

hydrozincite.audit_status: new v98 (2026-05-19). Latest+coolest Zn supergene mineral, the cave-floor + smithsonite-alteration coating. Substrate priority: smithsonite (alteration) > aurichalcite (intergrown) > calcite (cave-floor). Per research dossier 2026-05.
hydrozincite._late_stage_note: Latest+coolest+highest-pH Zn supergene niche. propensity:0.95 reflects very-late evaporative cave-floor signature.

habit_variants.name: epitactic_on_conichalcite
habit_variants.trigger: on conichalcite substrate — same structure type (adelite-descloizite group), Cu↔Zn substitution

habit_variants.name: powder_puff
habit_variants.trigger: excess > 1.2 — hemispherical 'powder-puff' radial sprays of needles, the Tsumeb classic

habit_variants.name: acicular_sprays
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ — drusy crusts of tiny acicular crystals, 1-5 mm radial sprays

habit_variants.name: bladed
habit_variants.trigger: low σ — bladed prismatic, less aggregated

austinite.description: CaZn(AsO₄)(OH) — orthorhombic adelite-descloizite group, Ca-Zn arsenate; the direct analog of conichalcite (Ca-Cu) and mottramite (Pb-Cu-V). Hardness 4-4.5, density 4.13 g/cm³. Color colorless to pale yellow-green to bright apple-green when cuprian (toward conichalcite series — continuous solid solution per Cu/Zn substitution). Forms supergene at <60°C in Ca-buffered pore fluids with active Zn²⁺ + As(V) at pH 6.5-8.0. The cation-ratio fork from conichalcite: Cu/(Cu+Zn) < 0.5 → austinite; > 0.5 → conichalcite; sweet spot < 0.3 (pure Zn-end) with sigma multiplier 1.2. Type locality Gold Hill, Utah (Staples 1935). Substrate priority: conichalcite (epitactic) > smithsonite > dolomite. Habit dispatch: epitactic on conichalcite / hemispherical powder-puffs / acicular sprays / bladed. Looks like a paler thinner conichalcite in hand; SG ~4.1 + apple-green cuprian color when present are the field marks. Refs: research dossier 2026-05; Staples 1935 (type description); Gebhard 1999 Tsumeb pp. 155-157; Keller 1977 MinRec 8(3); Magalhães et al. 1988 (log Ksp ~–22.6 at 25°C); Handbook of Mineralogy v.IV.
austinite._ingredients_note: v97 (2026-05-19): Ca ≥ 30, Zn ≥ 20, As(V) ≥ 5. Cation fork: Cu/(Cu+Zn) < 0.5 (conichalcite wins above). Pb > 50 blocks (duftite/bayldonite take precedence). pH 6.0-8.5, sweet spot 6.5-8.0. O₂ ≥ 0.5 (oxidizing supergene). T 5-60°C.

trace_ingredients.Cu: Cu < Zn for austinite; Cu > Zn → conichalcite; intermediate Cu gives cuprian-austinite apple-green color

austinite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Acid dissolution at pH < 4.5 — Ca²⁺ + Zn²⁺ + AsO₄³⁻ released.

fluorescence.status: Non-fluorescent. Pure Zn-end has no UV response; cuprian variety quenched by Cu²⁺.

twin_laws.name: contact_101
twin_laws._source: Anthony Handbook v.IV. Austinite (orthorhombic P2₁2₁2₁, CaZn(AsO₄)(OH)) is the Ca-Zn member of the adamite-group, often forming acicular sprays at Mapimí + Tsumeb + Gold Hill (Utah). {101} contact twins reported but data sparse — acicular habit dominates over visible twin morphology. Conservative p=0.02.

austinite.acid_dissolution: Slow in dilute HCl below pH 4.5 — Ca + Zn + As released to fluid.

pale_yellow_green.trigger: default — colorless to pale yellow-green, the pure Zn-end

apple_green_cuprian.trigger: Cu > 5 ppm — apple-green from Cu²⁺ chromophore, transitional toward conichalcite

austinite.audit_status: new v97 (2026-05-19). Companion to conichalcite (v87) — closes the Ca-Zn arsenate slot in the adelite-descloizite group. Substrate priority: conichalcite > smithsonite > dolomite. Per research dossier 2026-05.
austinite._late_stage_note: Late Tsumeb supergene — follows the Zn-arsenate hierarchy adamite (anhydrous) → austinite (when Ca enters) → legrandite (hydrous, Ca-free).

habit_variants.name: aztec_sun_spray
habit_variants.trigger: excess > 1.3 — the radiating sprays + fans, wedge-shaped prismatic terminations, the Tsumeb iconic 'Aztec sun' habit (Smithsonian classic)

habit_variants.name: wedge_prismatic
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ — acicular wedge prisms, divergent groups, the typical 1-10 cm Tsumeb habit

habit_variants.name: on_adamite
habit_variants.trigger: on adamite substrate — Zn arsenate epitactic growth

habit_variants.name: solitary_prismatic
habit_variants.trigger: low σ — solitary wedge prisms on limonite-coated dolomite (Tsumeb 30-44 level)

legrandite.description: Zn₂(AsO₄)(OH)·H₂O — monoclinic hydrated Zn arsenate, BRIGHT CANARY-YELLOW (the diagnostic Tsumeb color). Hardness 4.5-5, density 4.01 g/cm³. Vitreous-brilliant luster, almost adamantine on prism faces; brittle, fragile acicular crystals snap easily. Forms supergene at <50°C in mildly acidic (pH 4.5-6.5) Zn-rich Ca-poor pore fluids — distinguishes from austinite (pH 6.5-8, Ca-Zn co-present) by the Ca-FREE + acidic condition. The structural H₂O makes it sensitive to dehydration above ~120°C. Type locality Flor de Peña mine, Nuevo León, Mexico (Drugman & Hey 1932), but TSUMEB IS UNRIVALED in size + color — the 25 cm 'Aztec sun' specimen (Smithsonian) is canonical. Substrate priority: adamite (most common — same Zn-As chemistry) > willemite > limonite-coated dolomite. Habit dispatch: Aztec-sun radiating sprays / wedge-prismatic / on adamite / solitary. Field discriminator: UNMISTAKABLE — bright canary yellow + acicular wedge habit + brilliant vitreous luster. No other Tsumeb arsenate has that color and habit combination. Refs: research dossier 2026-05; Drugman & Hey 1932 Mineralog. Mag. 23:175; Gebhard 1999 Tsumeb pp. 197-202; Keller 1977 MinRec 8(3); Wilson & Keller 2001 MinRec 32(3); Handbook of Mineralogy.
legrandite._ingredients_note: v97 (2026-05-19): Zn ≥ 100 (Zn-RICH gate distinguishes from adamite's lower threshold), As(V) ≥ 10. CRITICAL: Ca < 20 (austinite competes above), Cu < 50 (olivenite/conichalcite compete), Pb < 20 (duftite/bayldonite compete). pH 4.5-7.0 — mildly acidic, sweet spot 5.0-6.0 (the lower end of the arsenate window). Dehydrates above 120°C (loses structural H₂O).
legrandite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Dehydration above ~120°C — loses structural H₂O, transforms to anhydrous Zn arsenate phases. Acid dissolution at pH < 4: Zn²⁺ + AsO₄³⁻ released.

fluorescence.status: Non-fluorescent under standard UV. The bright canary yellow is intrinsic ligand-field, not luminescence.

twin_laws.name: contact_010
twin_laws._source: Drugman & Hey 1932 type description; Handbook of Mineralogy. Legrandite's monoclinic structure permits {010} contact twins that produce sub-parallel wedge-prismatic pairs in the Aztec-sun sprays. Data sparse — conservative p=0.02. Visible in some Tsumeb specimens as paired wedges along the radiating fan.

legrandite.acid_dissolution: Dissolves in dilute HCl below pH 4.

canary_yellow.trigger: the diagnostic Tsumeb color — bright canary to lemon yellow

legrandite.audit_status: new v97 (2026-05-19). Tsumeb iconic — the canary-yellow 'Aztec sun' is the world reference. Distinguishes from adamite via the Ca-FREE + mildly-acidic + hydrous condition (legrandite has the extra H₂O). Substrate priority: adamite > willemite > limonite-dolomite.
legrandite._late_stage_note: Late Tsumeb supergene — sequence adamite (anhydrous) → austinite (Ca-bearing) → legrandite (hydrous, acidic-tolerant).

habit_variants.name: spherulitic
habit_variants.trigger: excess > 1.2 — spherulitic radiating bladed, vivianite-group rosettes

habit_variants.name: bladed_lath
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ — bladed lath-like along [010] with perfect {010} cleavage on flakes

habit_variants.name: drusy_crust
habit_variants.trigger: low σ — thin drusy crusts in damp seams

koettigite.description: Zn₃(AsO₄)₂·8H₂O — monoclinic VIVIANITE-GROUP, Zn end-member (series with erythrite Co, annabergite Ni, hörnesite Mg, parasymplesite Fe²⁺). Hardness 2.5-3 (soft — fingernail-scratchable, diagnostic), density 3.33 g/cm³. Color pale rose-pink (cobaltian-zinc-tinge), carmine-red (with erythrite component), peach, pale lilac, or near-colorless when pure Zn; pure Zn end-member is white to very pale pink. Perfect {010} cleavage (vivianite-group signature). Forms strictly supergene at <35°C in damp Zn-rich oxidation pockets — the 8 H₂O is fragile, dehydrating rapidly above 50°C. Discriminator from erythrite/annabergite: Zn dominates over Co + Ni in fluid; if Co > 10 or Ni > 10, those win the vivianite-group slot. Substrate priority: erythrite > annabergite (epitactic — same structure) > smithsonite. Type locality Schneeberg, Saxony (Daubrée 1850) but Tsumeb produces well-formed crystals from deep workings. Field discriminator: pink-to-colorless soft (H 2.5-3) bladed crystals with perfect one-direction cleavage = vivianite-group; SG 3.3 lighter than austinite (4.1) or adamite (4.4). Refs: research dossier 2026-05; Daubrée 1850; Gebhard 1999 Tsumeb p. 190; Hill 1979 (structure); Magalhães et al. 1988; Handbook of Mineralogy v.IV.
koettigite._ingredients_note: v97 (2026-05-19): Zn ≥ 50, As(V) ≥ 10. CRITICAL: Co < 10 (erythrite wins), Ni < 10 (annabergite wins). pH 6-8, sweet spot 6.5-7.5. Sharp T cap — 35°C max, with smooth penalty above 25°C (the 8 H₂O dehydrates rapidly).

trace_ingredients.Co: trace gives cobaltoan-koettigite pink tinge (more common in nature than pure Zn end)

koettigite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Dehydrates above ~50°C — loses structural H₂O, converts to lower hydrates and ultimately anhydrous Zn₃(AsO₄)₂ phases.

fluorescence.status: Non-fluorescent.

twin_laws.name: contact_010
twin_laws._source: Anthony Handbook v.IV. Koettigite (monoclinic C2/m, Zn₃(AsO₄)₂·8H₂O) is the Zn endmember of the vivianite-group. Less common in field collections than erythrite/annabergite; documented at Mapimí + Schneeberg. Same {010} contact twin behavior but rarer in observed specimens (data sparser); conservative p=0.02.

koettigite.acid_dissolution: Slowly soluble in dilute acid — Zn + AsO₄ released.

carmine_red_cobaltoan.trigger: Co > 0.5 — cobaltoan-koettigite, transitional toward erythrite carmine

koettigite.audit_status: new v97 (2026-05-19). Vivianite-group Zn end-member — completes the erythrite-annabergite-koettigite trio. Damp-niche cool-temperature mineral.
koettigite._late_stage_note: Latest+dampest supergene niche — forms in cool damp pockets after erythrite/annabergite chemistry runs out of Co/Ni.

habit_variants.name: botryoidal_crust
habit_variants.trigger: on Pb-Cu carbonate substrate (malachite/cerussite/mimetite) — olive-green botryoidal crust, the textbook Tsumeb form

habit_variants.name: spheroidal_aggregate
habit_variants.trigger: excess > 1.2 — 1-5 mm hemispherical aggregates

habit_variants.name: mammillary_crust
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ — mammillary crusts lining vugs

habit_variants.name: drusy_coating
habit_variants.trigger: low σ — drusy olive-green coating

duftite.description: PbCu(AsO₄)(OH) — orthorhombic ADELITE-DESCLOIZITE group, Pb-Cu analog of conichalcite (Ca-Cu) and mottramite (Pb-Cu vanadate). Hardness 3, density 6.40 g/cm³ (HEAVY — Pb is diagnostic in hand). Color olive-green to grass-green to grey-green, sometimes apple-green. Forms supergene at <60°C from Pb + Cu + As(V) in carbonate-buffered fluid at pH 5.5-7.5. Discriminator from bayldonite (the Cu-enriched sibling): duftite at Pb:Cu near 1:1 in fluid (Cu:Pb < 2.0); bayldonite at Pb:Cu near 1:3 (Cu:Pb > 2.0). Discriminator from mottramite: As vs V in fluid — duftite if As(V) > V; mottramite if V > As. One of the MOST COMMON Tsumeb arsenates, occurring on nearly every Pb-Cu oxide assemblage. Substrate priority: malachite > cerussite > mimetite > azurite. Pseudomorphs after azurite and mimetite documented. Habit dispatch: botryoidal crust (textbook) / spheroidal / mammillary / drusy. Field discriminator: olive-drab green + very heavy in hand (SG 6.4) + no carbonate fizz vs azurite. Refs: research dossier 2026-05; Pufahl 1920 (type description, Tsumeb); Gebhard 1999 Tsumeb pp. 173-177; Keller 1977 MinRec 8(3); Wilson & Keller 2001 MinRec 32(3); Kharisun et al. 1998 Mineralog. Mag. 62 (structure); Handbook of Mineralogy v.II.
duftite._ingredients_note: v97 (2026-05-19): Pb ≥ 50, Cu ≥ 20, As(V) ≥ 5. Fork from bayldonite: Cu:Pb < 2.0 (above = bayldonite wins). Fork from mottramite: V < As (above = mottramite wins). pH 5.0-8.0, sweet spot 6.0-7.5. Pb:Cu near 1:1 sweet spot (Cu:Pb 0.7-1.5 → sigma * 1.2).

trace_ingredients.V: competes via duftite/mottramite fork — if V > As, mottramite wins

duftite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Acid dissolution at pH < 4 — Pb²⁺ + Cu²⁺ + AsO₄³⁻ released.

fluorescence.status: Non-fluorescent. Pb²⁺ + Cu²⁺ both quench.

duftite._twin_laws_note: Intentionally empty — duftite (orthorhombic P2₁2₁2₁, PbCu(AsO₄)(OH)) habit code 'botryoidal_crust' signals microcrystalline aggregate. The Tsumeb supergene specimens are pistachio-green crusts/botryoidal coatings on bayldonite/mimetite, not individual euhedral crystals. No documented growth twinning at this habit scale. v139 arsenate batch.
duftite.acid_dissolution: Dissolves in dilute HCl below pH 4.

olive_green.trigger: default — olive-drab green to grass-green, the Tsumeb signature

apple_green_brighter.trigger: Cu-enriched local fluid — slight transition toward bayldonite's brighter green

duftite.audit_status: new v97 (2026-05-19). One of the most common Tsumeb arsenates. Substrate priority: malachite > cerussite > mimetite > azurite. Cation-ratio fork Cu:Pb 1:1 with bayldonite (1:3).
duftite._late_stage_note: Mid-to-late Tsumeb supergene — follows the Pb-Cu-arsenate hierarchy conichalcite (Ca-Cu, no Pb) → duftite (Pb:Cu 1:1) → bayldonite (Pb:Cu 1:3) → olivenite/cornubite (Cu-only).

habit_variants.name: pseudomorph_or_overgrowth
habit_variants.trigger: on mimetite/duftite substrate — epitactic overgrowth, sometimes pseudomorph after mimetite

habit_variants.name: spheroidal_mammillary
habit_variants.trigger: excess > 1.2 — 1-10 mm mammillary hemispheres, apple-green to yellow-green

habit_variants.name: botryoidal_druze
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ — botryoidal druses on Pb-Cu oxide matrix

habit_variants.name: powdery_crust
habit_variants.trigger: low σ — earthy powdery yellow-green coating, the Cornwall Penberthy Croft texture

bayldonite.description: PbCu₃(AsO₄)₂(OH)₂ — monoclinic Cu-ENRICHED Pb-Cu arsenate, the Cu-rich sibling of duftite. Hardness 4.5, density 5.65 g/cm³ (lighter than duftite at 6.4 — distinguishing). Color apple-green to yellow-green, grass-green, sometimes paler than duftite; rarely emerald-green. Forms supergene at <60°C where Cu activity exceeds Pb (Cu:Pb molar ~3:1) — typically near the Cu-sulfide source after Pb has been partly leached out as cerussite upstream. Type locality Penberthy Croft, Cornwall (Church 1865); Tsumeb material is more crystalline + brighter green, often as crusts on mimetite or duftite. Discriminators: vs duftite — Cu:Pb > 2.0 (above 1:1); vs olivenite — at least some Pb present (Pb ≥ 30); vs cornubite (Cu₅, no Pb) — Pb ≥ 30. Substrate priority: mimetite (provides Pb-As template, pseudomorphs documented) > duftite (epitactic, Cu-richer than substrate) > olivenite > malachite. Habit dispatch: pseudomorph_or_overgrowth / spheroidal_mammillary / botryoidal_druze / powdery_crust. Field discriminator: paler more yellow-green than duftite + lighter in hand (SG 5.7 vs 6.4) + often grungier/powdery texture (Cornwall material especially). Refs: research dossier 2026-05; Church 1865 (type description); Gebhard 1999 Tsumeb pp. 161-164; Keller 1977 MinRec 8(3); Wilson & Keller 2001 MinRec 32(3); Ghose & Wan 1979 (structure); Handbook of Mineralogy v.IV.
bayldonite._ingredients_note: v97 (2026-05-19): Pb ≥ 30, Cu ≥ 100 (Cu-rich threshold is the distinguisher from duftite), As(V) ≥ 10. Fork from duftite: Cu:Pb > 2.0 (below = duftite wins). Fork from mottramite: V < As. pH 5.0-7.5, sweet spot 5.5-7.0. Cu:Pb 2.5-4.0 sweet spot (sigma * 1.2).
bayldonite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Acid dissolution at pH < 4 — Pb²⁺ + Cu²⁺ + AsO₄³⁻ released.

fluorescence.status: Non-fluorescent — Cu²⁺ quenches.

bayldonite._twin_laws_note: Intentionally empty — bayldonite (monoclinic C2/c, PbCu₃(AsO₄)₂(OH)₂) habit code 'spheroidal_mammillary' signals non-euhedral aggregate. Forms green spheroidal/mammillary masses at Tsumeb + Wheal Carpenter (Cornwall type), classic supergene Pb-Cu-As habit. No individual euhedral crystals to twin. v139 arsenate batch.
bayldonite.acid_dissolution: Dissolves in dilute HCl below pH 4.

apple_green.trigger: default — apple-green to yellow-green, paler + brighter than duftite

bayldonite.audit_status: new v97 (2026-05-19). Cu-enriched sibling of duftite — completes the Pb-Cu arsenate cation-fork pair. Substrate priority: mimetite > duftite > olivenite > malachite.
bayldonite._late_stage_note: Late Tsumeb supergene — forms after duftite as fluid becomes Cu-enriched; sequence conichalcite → duftite → bayldonite → olivenite/cornubite.

habit_variants.name: acute_scalenohedral
habit_variants.trigger: excess > 1.4 — {0221} acute scalenohedron, cm-scale Jáchymov / Chañarcillo habit, the museum-grade crystals

habit_variants.name: acute_prismatic
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ — {1010} acute prism with rhombohedral termination

habit_variants.name: reniform_crust
habit_variants.trigger: low σ late vug filling — scarlet reniform coating

habit_variants.name: massive_disseminated
habit_variants.trigger: very low σ — massive granular vermilion in vein gangue

proustite.description: Ag₃AsS₃ — the 'LIGHT RUBY SILVER' (Lichtrotgültigerz), trigonal R3c sulfosalt, scarlet-vermilion to cochineal red. Hardness 2-2.5, density 5.57-5.64 g/cm³, distinct {1011} rhombohedral cleavage, brittle-to-sectile, adamantine luster. STREAK BRICK-RED to vermilion — the field discriminator from pyrargyrite (purplish-red). PHOTODECOMPOSES — within hours to days of bright light, surface darkens to black via formation of acanthite + arsenic-bearing residue. Irreversible. Curatorial standard: dark storage. Forms late in epithermal Ag systems at 150-300°C, post-arsenide + post-main-sulfide stage, syn-to-post-native silver. Type localities Jáchymov + Chañarcillo (Chile, world-class crystals). Discriminates from pyrargyrite via the As:Sb FORK MECHANISM in fluid: X_As = mol(As)/(mol(As)+mol(Sb)). X_As > 0.5 → proustite; sweet spot X_As > 0.7 (pure As-end). Below the solvus (~200°C) the system fractionates: proustite first (As more stable per mol), then pyrargyrite from Sb-enriched residue — meaning proustite tends to be EARLIER in zoned veins. Trigonal R3c isostructural with pyrargyrite; complete solid solution above ~300°C, discrete grains below. Supergene: oxidation releases 50% native_Ag + 50% acanthite by mol + AsO₄³⁻ (→ scorodite if Fe present, else arsenolite). Refs: research dossier 2026-05; Sack & Loucks 1985 Am. Min. 70:1270-1289 (canonical Cu-As-Sb-S sulfosalt thermodynamics); Ondruš et al. 2003 J. Geosci. 48:157-192 (Jáchymov paragenesis); Keighin & Honea 1969 (proustite-pyrargyrite phase diagram); Dana 7th vol. I; Handbook of Mineralogy.
proustite._ingredients_note: v96 (2026-05-19): Ag ≥ 0.1 (very low Ag tolerance), As(III) ≥ 1 (via arseniteAvailablePpm), S ≥ 10. CRITICAL: X_As = As/(As+Sb) > 0.5 in fluid — pyrargyrite wins below this. Sweet spot X_As > 0.7 with sigma multiplier 1.4. pH 5-8 (low-sulfidation epithermal, NOT acidic like enargite). T 100-350°C, sweet spot 180-250°C. sulfideRedoxAnoxic threshold 1.5 (moderately reducing, less restrictive than skutterudite's 0.5). Photodecomposition flag crystal._light_sensitive set in grow engine (future LIGHT_TRANSITIONS integration).

trace_ingredients.Sb: antithesis — competes via the As:Sb fork; if Sb > As, pyrargyrite wins instead

proustite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Photodecomposition (light exposure): 2 Ag₃AsS₃ + hν → 3 Ag₂S (acanthite) + As + ½ S₂. Acidic oxidation: releases 50% native_Ag + 50% acanthite by mol + AsO₄³⁻ → scorodite (if Fe present) or arsenolite.

fluorescence.status: Non-fluorescent — opaque adamantine sulfosalt. But darkens visibly under light (photodecomposition signature).

proustite.acid_dissolution: Soluble in nitric acid; HCl produces white AgCl precipitate (silver-test diagnostic).

scarlet_vermilion.trigger: fresh material — scarlet to cochineal red, adamantine

darkened_black.trigger: exposed to light > hours-days — photodecomposes to black acanthite surface

proustite.audit_status: new v96 (2026-05-19). Closes the Ag-sulfosalt As-end of the five-element vein supersaturation manifold. Substrate priority: native_silver > acanthite > arsenopyrite > cobaltite. Fork mechanism via X_As > 0.5 with sweet spot > 0.7. Per research dossier 2026-05.
proustite._late_stage_note: Late-stage epithermal Ag — post-arsenide, post-main-sulfide, syn-to-post-native_silver. propensity:0.85 matches Jáchymov/Chañarcillo paragenetic position.

habit_variants.name: large_prismatic_hemimorphic
habit_variants.trigger: excess > 1.4 — hemimorphic prism (different terminations), Andreasberg / San Cristobal / Guanajuato classic, crystals to 5 cm+

habit_variants.name: scalenohedral
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ — {0221} scalenohedron with rhombohedral termination

habit_variants.name: massive_xenomorphic
habit_variants.trigger: low σ — cherry-red to red-black mass in vein selvages

habit_variants.name: compact_granular
habit_variants.trigger: very low σ — compact masses in vein gangue, the historical ore mode (Comstock Lode etc.)

pyrargyrite.description: Ag₃SbS₃ — the 'DARK RUBY SILVER' (Dunkelrotgültigerz), trigonal R3c sulfosalt, deep cherry-red to red-black (often appears opaque-black until fresh-fractured). Hardness 2.5, density 5.82-5.86 g/cm³ (denser than proustite — Sb is heavier than As). STREAK PURPLISH-RED — the field discriminator from proustite (brick-red/scarlet). Photodecomposes (Ag₂S surface, slower than proustite — weeks to months under light). The MORE COMMON of the two ruby silvers (Sb is more abundant than As in most epithermal Ag systems); roughly an order of magnitude more abundant in collections. Forms late in epithermal Ag systems at 100-300°C, syn-to-post-proustite (Sb fractionation residue — proustite drains As first, then pyrargyrite from residual Sb). Type locality Andreasberg (Harz, Germany); spectacular crystals from San Cristóbal Bolivia, Guanajuato Mexico, Pribram Czechia, Schneeberg Saxony, Comstock Lode NV. Discriminates from proustite via the As:Sb fork: X_As < 0.5 in fluid; sweet spot X_As < 0.3 (pure Sb-end). Has a rare monoclinic dimorph: pyrostilpnite (low-T Ag₃SbS₃) — analogous to xanthoconite (proustite's dimorph); rare in nature, engine defaults to the trigonal phase. Supergene: oxidation releases 50% native_Ag + 50% acanthite by mol + Sb oxides (cervantite Sb³⁺Sb⁵⁺O₄, stibiconite Sb₂O₆(OH), valentinite/senarmontite Sb₂O₃). Refs: research dossier 2026-05; Sack & Loucks 1985; Ondruš et al. 2003 (Jáchymov); Keighin & Honea 1969 (phase diagram); Dana 7th vol. I; Handbook of Mineralogy.
pyrargyrite._ingredients_note: v96 (2026-05-19): Ag ≥ 0.1, Sb ≥ 1, S ≥ 10. CRITICAL: X_As = As/(As+Sb) < 0.5 — proustite wins above 0.5. Sweet spot X_As < 0.3 with sigma multiplier 1.4. pH 5-8 (low-sulfidation epithermal). T 100-320°C, sweet spot 150-230°C (slightly cooler than proustite — pyrargyrite is the later, lower-T member). sulfideRedoxAnoxic threshold 1.5. Growth rate slightly faster than proustite (2.8 vs. 2.5 × excess) reflecting typically larger crystal sizes in nature.

trace_ingredients.As: antithesis — competes via the As:Sb fork; if As > Sb, proustite wins instead

pyrargyrite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Photodecomposition (slow, weeks-months under light): 2 Ag₃SbS₃ + hν → 3 Ag₂S + Sb + ½ S₂. Acidic oxidation: releases 50% native_Ag + 50% acanthite + Sb (→ cervantite/stibiconite Sb oxides; no Fe-arsenate cousins since Sb-arsenate analogs don't exist).

fluorescence.status: Non-fluorescent. Darkens under light but slower than proustite.

pyrargyrite.acid_dissolution: Soluble in nitric acid; releases Sb (test: SbCl₃ on iron coin → black Sb deposit).

cherry_red.trigger: fresh material — cherry red to deep red-black

darkened_black.trigger: exposed to light > weeks-months — slow photodecomposition to Ag₂S surface

pyrargyrite.audit_status: new v96 (2026-05-19). Closes the Ag-sulfosalt Sb-end of the five-element vein supersaturation manifold. THE more common ruby silver (Sb > As in most epithermal Ag fluids). Substrate priority: native_silver > acanthite > tetrahedrite > stibnite. Fork mechanism via X_As < 0.5 with sweet spot < 0.3. Per research dossier 2026-05.
pyrargyrite._late_stage_note: Late-stage epithermal Ag — syn-to-post-proustite (Sb fractionation residue). propensity:0.9 reflects its slightly later paragenetic position vs. proustite (0.85).

habit_variants.name: rosette_core
habit_variants.trigger: on native_bismuth or native_silver substrate — cubo-octahedral core in Cobalt-Ontario rosette texture

habit_variants.name: cubic
habit_variants.trigger: excess > 1.0 — {100} cubes, the Bou Azzer (Aghbar/Irhtem) habit; crystals to 9 cm

habit_variants.name: cubo_octahedral
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ — {100} cube + {111} octahedron, silver-tin-white

habit_variants.name: massive_granular
habit_variants.trigger: low σ — massive granular 'smaltite' variety, the Cobalt-Ontario ore mode

skutterudite.description: (Co,Ni,Fe)As₃ — cubic Im-3̄ skutterudite-group triarsenide; complete solid solution with nickel-skutterudite (NiAs₃₋ₓ). Hardness 5.5-6, density 6.5-6.82 g/cm³. Color tin-white to silver-gray, tarnishing iridescent then BLACK (the most reactive of the diarsenide quartet — distinctive). Cleavage distinct on {001} and {111}. The defining HIGHEST-temperature member of the five-element vein arsenide stage (Kissin 1992 Geosci. Canada 19:113-124). Triarsenide stoichiometry demands the highest As activity of any phase — X_As = 0.96-0.99 in natural samples (Markl et al. 2016 Min. Dep. 51:703-718, Odenwald compositional study). NO sulfur tolerance — fluid S > 5 ppm pushes the system to cobaltite (CoAsS) instead. Forms FIRST in zoned arsenide rosettes, nucleating directly on native Bi-Ag dendrites at 280-290°C (the Markl 'natural fracking' model: CH₄ or graphite contact rapidly reduces an oxidized As(III)-bearing brine far from equilibrium). Substrate priority: native_bismuth > native_silver > native_arsenic > vug wall. Field discriminator from safflorite/rammelsbergite/loellingite: highest hardness, conchoidal fracture, BLACK iridescent tarnish (the others stay silver/gray longer); in thin section, ISOTROPIC (cubic — the only one of the four). Refs: research dossier 2026-05; Roseboom 1962 Am. Min. 47:310-327; Radcliffe 1971 Can. Min. 9:559-563; Mandel & Donohue 1971 Acta Cryst. 27:2288; Kissin 1992; Markl et al. 2016; Ondruš et al. 2003 J. Geosci. 48:157-192 (Jáchymov three-stage scheme); Anthony et al. Handbook of Mineralogy.
skutterudite._ingredients_note: v95 (2026-05-19): Co ≥ 5, As(III) ≥ 30 (via arseniteAvailablePpm). Critical: S MUST be < 5 ppm — skutterudite has essentially zero sulfur tolerance (Markl X_As = 0.96-0.99). Above S=5, the system flips to cobaltite (CoAsS, sulfarsenide). Ni co-incorporation enables triarsenide stoichiometry (factor 1.1 when Ni > 5). pH 5.0-7.5 (carbonate-buffered). T 280-500°C, sweet spot 320-420°C. sulfideRedoxAnoxic threshold 0.5 — VERY reducing (Bou Azzer / Cobalt-Ontario logfO2 4-6 below HM buffer).

trace_ingredients.Fe: iron-skutterudite endmember at the Fe-rich end

skutterudite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Oxidative dissolution at O₂ > 0.5: releases Co²⁺ + Ni²⁺ + AsO₄³⁻. Co fraction → erythrite Co₃(AsO₄)₂·8H₂O (crimson 'cobalt bloom'). Ni fraction → annabergite Ni₃(AsO₄)₂·8H₂O (apple-green 'nickel bloom'). Fe fraction → scorodite + goethite.

fluorescence.status: Non-fluorescent — opaque metallic arsenide.

tin_white_iridescent.trigger: tin-white fresh, IRIDESCENT-to-black tarnish on aging — the most distinctive tarnish in the quartet

skutterudite.audit_status: new v95 (2026-05-19). The deepest+hottest of the five-element vein diarsenide quartet. Closes a glaring Schneeberg/Jáchymov gap — the simulator previously had only cobaltite + nickeline + arsenopyrite + native_arsenic from the 10+ canonical primary arsenide assemblage. Substrate priority encodes Markl 2016 rosette zonation: native_Bi/Ag seed → skutterudite core. Fires at T 320-420°C sweet spot, S < 5 strict, As(III) > 30, Co > 5. Per research dossier 2026-05.
skutterudite._late_stage_note: Very early primary stage — first arsenide in the rosette zonation, nucleates on native-metal seeds. propensity:0.1.

habit_variants.name: mantle
habit_variants.trigger: on rammelsbergite/skutterudite substrate — silvery thick rim on Ni-rich core, the Cobalt-Ontario rosette mantle

habit_variants.name: star_fiveling
habit_variants.trigger: excess > 1.4 — five-pointed star twin on {011}, the diagnostic safflorite habit when present

habit_variants.name: pseudo_orthorhombic_prism
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ — elongate [010] orthorhombic prisms with {101}+{310} faces, pseudo-tetragonal outline

habit_variants.name: radial_fibrous
habit_variants.trigger: low σ — spherical-radiating bowtie textures, silvery anisotropic

safflorite.description: (Co,Fe)As₂ — orthorhombic Pnnm (löllingite group), marcasite-type structure. Hardness 4.5-5, density 7.20-7.47 g/cm³. Color tin-white, readily tarnishes to dark gray (intermediate reactivity between skutterudite's iridescent-black and löllingite's dull gray). The Co-sink phase of the five-element vein arsenide stage — natural compositions span Co:Fe from ~5:1 (Schneeberg) to ~1:2 (Radcliffe & Berry 1968 Am. Min. 53:1856 solid-solution series with löllingite). Famous for STAR-SHAPED FIVELINGS on {011} (twin law, fivefold pseudo-symmetry). Dimorphous with clinosafflorite (monoclinic). Forms in the rim sequence Ni → Co → Fe as fluid evolves: typically MANTLES rammelsbergite or skutterudite cores at 200-350°C, fO₂ slightly less reducing than skutterudite's regime. Tolerates up to ~0.9 wt% S in the solid before flipping to cobaltite sulfarsenide. Field discriminator: lower hardness than skutterudite/rammelsbergite (4.5-5 vs. 5.5-6); star twins under hand lens pathognomonic when present; tarnishes dull gray (slower than skutterudite's iridescent, faster than rammelsbergite's silver). Practically indistinguishable from rammelsbergite without EMPA — the classic identification problem. Supergene → erythrite crimson bloom. Refs: research dossier 2026-05; Radcliffe & Berry 1968 Am. Min. 53:1856-1881; Handbook of Mineralogy; Kraus & Pollmann 2002 MinRec 33(6) (Schneeberg); Kissin 1992; Markl et al. 2016.
safflorite._ingredients_note: v95 (2026-05-19): Co ≥ 5, As(III) ≥ 15. S < 15 ppm (~0.9 wt% S in solid tolerance per Handbook chemistry). Fe substitution allowed (X_Fe up to ~0.67 in löllingite-safflorite series). pH 5.0-7.5. T 200-380°C, sweet spot 230-320°C. sulfideRedoxAnoxic threshold 1.0 (less restrictive than skutterudite's 0.5). Markl Odenwald X_Co up to 0.76 — the Co-sink phase.

trace_ingredients.Fe: incorporated as essential into safflorite-löllingite solid solution

safflorite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Oxidative dissolution at O₂ > 0.5: Co²⁺ + AsO₄³⁻ released. Co-dominant supergene → ERYTHRITE Co₃(AsO₄)₂·8H₂O (crimson cobalt bloom). The Schneeberg-Annaberg type signature.

fluorescence.status: Non-fluorescent — opaque arsenide.

twin_laws.name: safflorite_011_fiveling
twin_laws.trigger: DIAGNOSTIC star-shaped fiveling on {011}; fires at excess > 1.4

tin_white.trigger: tin-white fresh, dull gray tarnish — intermediate reactivity

safflorite.audit_status: new v95 (2026-05-19). Middle T-range member of the five-element vein diarsenide quartet. Mantles rammelsbergite/skutterudite cores per Cobalt-Ontario rosette texture (Kissin 1992). Star-fiveling habit is the diagnostic visual signature when conditions permit.
safflorite._late_stage_note: Middle arsenide stage — mantles innermost rammelsbergite/skutterudite cores. propensity:0.2.

habit_variants.name: acicular_spray
habit_variants.trigger: excess > 1.0 — radiating prismatic [010] needles, the Bou Azzer + Schneeberg signature acicular habit

habit_variants.name: prismatic
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ — {010} prismatic, pinkish-white anisotropic

habit_variants.name: massive_radial
habit_variants.trigger: low σ — massive fibrous-radiating, the bulk-ore mode

rammelsbergite.description: NiAs₂ — orthorhombic Pnnm (löllingite group), marcasite-type structure. Hardness 5.5-6, density 7.09-7.10 g/cm³. Color tin-white WITH A FAINT PINKISH HUE — the only pink-cast member of the diarsenide quartet (Ni-coordination chromophore), diagnostic. TRIMORPHOUS with pararammelsbergite (orthorhombic Pbca, lower-T polymorph) and krutovite (cubic): rammelsbergite is the higher-T form, kinetically persistent down to surface T in nature. Type locality Schneeberg, Saxony. Strong anisotropism in polished section, especially in blues — strongest of the quartet. Demands Ni-rich, Co-depleted fluid; typically forms after early Co has been stripped into skutterudite cores. Tolerates up to ~3 wt% S in solid, but above that flips to gersdorffite (NiAsS sulfarsenide, not in catalog). Sb substitution up to ~1 wt%. Innermost arsenide when Ni dominates the residual fluid post-native-metal nucleation event (Markl et al. 2016). At Schneeberg [type locality] often forms cores with safflorite mantles — the 'Geyer-type' rosette. Field discriminator: PINKISH-WHITE tint distinguishes from safflorite (pure tin-white), löllingite (steel-gray), and skutterudite (silvery → black-tarnishing). Stays silvery longest of the four — Ni component is passive. Supergene → ANNABERGITE Ni₃(AsO₄)₂·8H₂O (apple-green nickel bloom — type locality Annaberg, Saxony). Refs: research dossier 2026-05; Kjekshus, Rakke & Andresen 1974 Acta Chem. Scand. 28:996; Ramdohr 1969 'The Ore Minerals' 839-845; Handbook of Mineralogy; Ondruš et al. 2003.
rammelsbergite._ingredients_note: v95 (2026-05-19): Ni ≥ 5, As(III) ≥ 15. S < 20 ppm (~3 wt% S in solid tolerance, beyond which → gersdorffite). pH 5.0-7.5. T 250-420°C, sweet spot 280-380°C. Co/Ni ratio matters — if Co > Ni in fluid, safflorite/skutterudite outcompete (engine multiplies σ by 0.5 in that regime). Co-substitution typically < 2 wt% in solid.
rammelsbergite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Oxidative dissolution at O₂ > 0.5: Ni²⁺ + AsO₄³⁻ released. Supergene → ANNABERGITE Ni₃(AsO₄)₂·8H₂O (apple-green 'nickel bloom').

fluorescence.status: Non-fluorescent — opaque arsenide.

twin_laws.name: rammelsbergite_101
twin_laws.trigger: {101} twins reported, less common than safflorite's

pinkish_white.trigger: tin-white WITH FAINT PINK HUE — the only pink-cast diarsenide, diagnostic

rammelsbergite.audit_status: new v95 (2026-05-19). Type locality Schneeberg, Saxony. Ni-dominant diarsenide, innermost arsenide rim in zoned crystals. Pink tint is the unique field discriminator within the quartet. Engine suppresses when Co > Ni (safflorite/skutterudite win that fluid). Per research dossier 2026-05.
rammelsbergite._late_stage_note: Early-to-middle arsenide stage — innermost when Ni dominates. propensity:0.15.

habit_variants.name: outermost_rim
habit_variants.trigger: on skutterudite/safflorite/rammelsbergite substrate — steel-gray rim on Co-Ni arsenide core, the Markl 2016 outermost zonation

habit_variants.name: striated_prism
habit_variants.trigger: excess > 1.4 — {001} prism with DEEP longitudinal striations (deeper + more regular than arsenopyrite's, the diagnostic), doubly terminated to 4 cm

habit_variants.name: radial_spray
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ — radial divergent sprays, steel-gray pyramidal

habit_variants.name: massive_dense
habit_variants.trigger: low σ / ore mode — 'dense löllingite' massive granular, the mesothermal vein bulk

loellingite.description: FeAs₂ — orthorhombic Pnnm (löllingite group — the namesake). Hardness 5-5.5 (Mohs) but VHN 859-920 — HIGHEST microhardness of the quartet despite lowest Mohs (characteristic of marcasite-group structure). Density 7.40-7.47 g/cm³. Color STEEL-GRAY TO SILVER-WHITE — more gray than the other three (Fe pigmentation), the only non-silvery member of the quartet. Strong anisotropism. Solid solution with safflorite (Radcliffe & Berry 1968 Am. Min. 53:1856) but structural rather than complete; natural Co-Fe arsenides show a miscibility gap around (Co₀.₅Fe₀.₅)As₂. WIDEST temperature range of the quartet (150-450°C) — Fe is rarely limiting, so löllingite forms across the full arsenide-stage T window. In five-element veins specifically, forms the OUTERMOST RIMS at 200-280°C as fluid Fe activity rises after Co and Ni depletion (Markl X_Fe → 1.0 at youngest rims). CRITICAL phase boundary with arsenopyrite (FeAsS): at fS₂ > ~10⁻¹² atm (T ~300°C), the system flips to arsenopyrite instead of löllingite (Kretschmar & Scott 1976 Can. Min. 14:364; Heinrich & Eadington 1986). The cleanest binary switch in the four-mineral system — modeled explicitly here as the S < 1 ppm gate. Löllingite + arsenopyrite oscillatory intergrowths are pathognomonic of fluctuating fS₂. Field discriminator: STEEL-GRAY color (the only non-silvery diarsenide); tarnishes dull gray (not iridescent like skutterudite). Striated prisms — striations DEEPER and more regular than arsenopyrite's, a hand-lens-grade distinction. Supergene → SCORODITE FeAsO₄·2H₂O (pale-green to bluish — distinct from cobalt/nickel blooms). NO erythrite/annabergite from pure löllingite — the supergene fingerprint that separates Fe-end from Co/Ni-end arsenides at outcrop. Refs: research dossier 2026-05; Radcliffe & Berry 1968; Pearson 1965 Zeits. Krist. 121:449-462; Kretschmar & Scott 1976 (the fS₂ boundary); Handbook of Mineralogy; Markl et al. 2016.
loellingite._ingredients_note: v95 (2026-05-19): Fe ≥ 10 (almost never limiting), As(III) ≥ 15. CRITICAL: S < 1 ppm — above this, flips to arsenopyrite FeAsS (Kretschmar & Scott 1976 phase boundary). pH 5.0-7.5. T 150-450°C (widest of quartet), sweet spot 200-350°C. sulfideRedoxAnoxic threshold 1.2 (less restrictive than skutterudite's 0.5, since Fe-As tolerance is broader).

trace_ingredients.Co: incorporated as essential into safflorite-löllingite solid solution at Co-rich end

loellingite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Oxidative dissolution at O₂ > 0.5: Fe²⁺ + AsO₄³⁻ released. Fe-only supergene → SCORODITE FeAsO₄·2H₂O (pale-green). No Co/Ni bloom — the supergene fingerprint that separates Fe-arsenide from Co/Ni-arsenide outcrops.

fluorescence.status: Non-fluorescent — opaque arsenide.

steel_gray.trigger: STEEL-GRAY (the only non-silvery diarsenide; Fe pigmentation)

loellingite.audit_status: new v95 (2026-05-19). Type locality Lölling, Carinthia, Austria. Widest T-range member of the diarsenide quartet (150-450°C). The S < 1 ppm gate encodes the sharp Kretschmar & Scott (1976) fS₂ boundary with arsenopyrite — above the gate, system flips to arsenopyrite. Substrate priority: rim on skutterudite/safflorite > intergrown with arsenopyrite > vug wall. Per research dossier 2026-05.
loellingite._late_stage_note: Latest arsenide rim in zoned crystals (X_Fe → 1.0 at youngest growth per Markl 2016). propensity:0.3.

habit_variants.name: striated_prismatic
habit_variants.trigger: default at moderate-high σ — elongate [001] orthorhombic prisms with diagnostic c-axis striations; the Butte / Quiruvilca / Bisbee primary habit, crystals to 10 cm

habit_variants.name: pseudo_hexagonal_trilling
habit_variants.trigger: very high σ (excess > 1.4) — 60° twin on {320}, pseudo-hexagonal star outline. The diagnostic enargite twin.

habit_variants.name: tabular
habit_variants.trigger: lower σ — {001} tabular tablets, iron-black metallic

habit_variants.name: massive_granular
habit_variants.trigger: lowest σ / ore-mode — compact massive granular, the bulk-ore texture

enargite.description: Cu₃AsS₄ — orthorhombic high-sulfidation Cu-As-S sulfosalt, the primary Cu mineral at Butte MT, Chuquicamata Chile, Bisbee AZ (Sacramento-Lavender Pit), Tsumeb upper sulfide zone, Lepanto Philippines, El Indio Chile, Goldfield NV, Quiruvilca Peru. Hardness 3, density 4.46 g/cm³, perfect {110} prismatic cleavage (THE field discriminator from tennantite — tennantite has no cleavage). Steel-gray to iron-black with bright metallic luster on fresh fracture; tarnishes dull. Diagnostic 60° twin on {320} produces pseudo-hexagonal trilling outlines. The defining environment is HIGH-SULFIDATION: acidic (pH 1-3), advanced argillic alteration assemblage (pyrophyllite, alunite, kaolinite, dickite, native sulfur) where SO₂ disproportionation drives both the acidity and the high f(S₂). Below ~320°C the same composition forms luzonite (tetragonal I-42m) — they're polymorphs, not a solid-solution series. The simulator engine fires the same supersaturation logic across the full T range but flags the polymorph for narrative. Supergene oxidation releases a large As budget (the AMD As-load signature of Butte/Bisbee/Chuquicamata) plus brochantite + cuprite + chalcocite + scorodite. Field discriminator from tennantite: metallic luster + perfect prismatic cleavage + striations + darker color + larger crystals; tennantite is submetallic, no cleavage, equant tetrahedral crystals, with cherry-red thin-edge transmission. Refs: research dossier 2026-05; Einaudi/Hedenquist/Inan 2003 SEG SP10:285-313 (the canonical f(S₂)-T sulfidation diagram); Sack & Loucks 1985 Am. Min. 70:1270-1289; Brimhall 1979,1980 (Butte paragenesis); Posfai & Buseck 1998 (enargite/luzonite phase relations); Anthony et al. Handbook of Mineralogy.
enargite._ingredients_note: v94 (2026-05-19): Cu ≥ 20, As ≥ 5, S ≥ 100. CRITICAL: enargite is the HIGH-SULFIDATION endmember of the Cu-As-S system (Einaudi/Hedenquist/Inan 2003). Discriminator from tennantite: pH < 4.5 AND sulfidation_proxy = log10(S+1) - pH > 0.5. Both gates must trip for enargite to fire; otherwise tennantite (intermediate-sulfidation, pH 3-7) wins. The simulator lacks f(S₂) tracking, so pH + total S serves as the proxy — high S + low pH correlates with the SO₂ disproportionation environment that defines high-sulfidation Cu deposits. Polymorph: T ≥ 320°C → enargite (orthorhombic); T < 320°C → luzonite (tetragonal) at the same composition. As(III) consumed via arseniteAvailablePpm (matches the v92 As-state-split helper — enargite uses As(III) in [AsS₃]³⁻ groups, not arsenate As(V)).

trace_ingredients.Sb: trace 0-10% substitution toward famatinite (Cu₃SbS₄), the Sb-isostructural analog; high Sb routes to famatinite instead
trace_ingredients.Fe: trace 0-5% substitution

enargite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Oxidative dissolution above O₂ > 1.0 (supergene): Cu₃AsS₄ + O₂ + H₂O → Cu²⁺ + AsO₄³⁻ + SO₄²⁻ + H⁺. Releases the big AMD As budget — feeds brochantite/antlerite (Cu sulfate), cuprite/chalcocite (Cu enrichment blanket), scorodite (Fe-AsO₄), and acidifies local pore water. The Butte / Chuquicamata acid mine drainage signature.

thermal_decomp_products.Cu: as Cu²⁺ → brochantite Cu₄(SO₄)(OH)₆ in sulfate-rich supergene, or chalcocite Cu₂S in enrichment blanket
thermal_decomp_products.As: as AsO₄³⁻ → scorodite FeAsO₄·2H₂O if Fe available, else olivenite/conichalcite
thermal_decomp_products.S: as SO₄²⁻ → drives gypsum + brochantite formation downstream
thermal_decomp_products.acid: H⁺ release acidifies local pore water — the AMD signature

fluorescence.status: Non-fluorescent — Cu²⁺ + opaque metallic sulfide. No characteristic emission.

twin_laws.name: enargite_320
twin_laws.trigger: 60° trilling on {320} — the diagnostic enargite/luzonite twin, pseudo-hexagonal outline. Fires at excess > 1.4.

iron_black.trigger: default — steel-gray to iron-black with bright metallic luster on fresh fracture

dark_tarnish.trigger: exposed to air — tarnishes dull, develops brown-violet film

enargite.audit_status: new v94 (2026-05-19). Closes the high-sulfidation Cu-As-S endmember — Bisbee has documented primary enargite (Sacramento-Lavender Pit), and supergene_oxidation (Tsumeb-style) has enargite in the upper sulfide zone. The discriminator from tennantite via pH + sulfidation proxy is the load-bearing mechanic; per Einaudi/Hedenquist/Inan 2003. Polymorph dispatch (enargite ≥ 320°C / luzonite < 320°C) per Posfai & Buseck 1998. Substrate priority: pyrite > chalcopyrite > vug wall. RNG-cascade guard via sigma < 1.0 early-out in nucleation.
enargite._late_stage_note: Early-to-middle primary stage in high-sulfidation Cu systems. Contemporaneous with pyrite + alunite + native sulfur + covellite. Followed by lower-T overprint with tennantite + chalcocite + bornite when f(S₂) drops. propensity:0.15 matches its position as one of the earliest primary sulfides.

habit_variants.name: acicular_tuft
habit_variants.trigger: default at moderate σ — radiating azure needles 1-5 mm, the classic Bisbee vug habit

habit_variants.name: spherulitic_rosette
habit_variants.trigger: high σ or on chrysocolla — concentric fibrous balls 2-15 mm, the Kaokoveld signature; growth controlled by diffusion through pre-existing Cu-silicate gel

habit_variants.name: pseudomorph_after_malachite
habit_variants.trigger: on dissolving malachite substrate — Cu(OH)/CO₃ layers preserved as Cu-O sheets; CO₃ displaced as CO₂(g) and replaced by SiO₃ chains. The Bisbee 'pseudomorph after malachite' signature.

habit_variants.name: pseudomorph_after_azurite
habit_variants.trigger: on dissolving azurite substrate — sharp blocky azurite morphology preserved in fibrous blue shattuckite. The Mesopotamia mine Kaokoveld habit.

habit_variants.name: massive_granular
habit_variants.trigger: low σ replacement fill — fibrous massive blue, no individual crystals visible

shattuckite.description: Cu₅(SiO₃)₄(OH)₂ — deep azure-to-greenish-blue inosilicate (single-chain Cu-silicate; pyroxene-type chains on Cu-O sheets — NOT to be confused with plancheite's amphibole-type double chains; Evans & Mrose 1977 Am. Min. 62:491). Type locality Shattuck mine, Bisbee (Warren district), Arizona (Schaller 1915 J. Wash. Acad. Sci. 5:7). Hardness 3.5, density 4.11 g/cm³ (heavy for a non-metallic silicate due to high Cu content), perfect cleavage on {010} and {100}. Forms strictly supergene at 25-90°C in mildly alkaline (pH 7.5-9.0) oxidizing pore fluids — distinctly more alkaline than dioptase, requiring substantial OH⁻ activity. The defining mechanism is REPLACEMENT OF MALACHITE when CO₂ escapes from a vadose vug: 5 Cu₂(CO₃)(OH)₂ + 8 SiO₂(aq) → 2 Cu₅(SiO₃)₄(OH)₂ + 5 CO₂↑ + 3 H₂O. Higher Cu:Si ratio than dioptase (5:4 vs. 1:1) — shattuckite is the high-Cu, low-Si Cu-silicate. Field discriminator vs. chrysocolla: shattuckite H 3.5 (vs. chrysocolla 2-4 but softer), density 4.1 (vs. chrysocolla 2.0-2.4 — heavy in hand), shows fibrous/acicular radial structure under loupe (chrysocolla is amorphous/conchoidal). Refs: research dossier 2026-05; Schaller 1915 (type description); Evans & Mrose 1977 (structure); Keller 1977 MinRec 8(3) (Tsumeb plancheite-shattuckite-dioptase sequence); Cairncross & Bahmann 2006 MinRec 37:137 (Kaokoveld); Anthony et al. Handbook of Mineralogy v.II.
