shattuckite._ingredients_note: v93 (2026-05-19): Cu ≥ 5, SiO₂ ≥ 20 — stoichiometry 5:4 (Cu/Si), needs sustained Cu activity. CO₃ < 30 ppm (sharper than dioptase's gate — the OH-bearing structure needs CO₂ outgassing complete). Cl < 1000, SO₄ < 500 (brochantite/antlerite compete on SO₄). pH window 7.5-9.5, sweet spot 8.0-8.5 (mildly alkaline). O₂ > 0.5 (oxidizing). Substrate priority: malachite (replacement, the type texture) > azurite (replacement) > chrysocolla > vug wall.

trace_ingredients.CO3: < 30 ppm gate — needs CO₂ outgassing complete; sharper than dioptase
trace_ingredients.Al: > 1 ppm drives chrysocolla instead of shattuckite (per research dossier — Al pulls Cu into amorphous Cu-Si gel)

shattuckite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Stable to ~100°C; above this, dehydrates and recrystallizes to anhydrous Cu-silicate.

fluorescence.status: Non-fluorescent under SW/MW/LW UV. Cu²⁺ quenches; shattuckite shows no characteristic emission.

twin_laws.name: contact_010
twin_laws._source: Anthony Handbook v.II shattuckite section. Orthorhombic {010} contact twin reported very rarely in acicular-tuft and spherulitic-rosette specimens (Bisbee, Tantara). Data sparse — conservative p=0.005.

shattuckite.acid_dissolution: Slow dissolution in dilute HCl at pH < 4.5. Cu²⁺ + silicic acid released.

deep_azure.trigger: Cu²⁺ chromophore + OH coordination — deep saturated azure-to-cobalt blue, distinct from chrysocolla's cyan and dioptase's emerald

greenish_blue.trigger: pH near 7.5 (lower end of stability) — bluer-greenish shattuckite, slightly less saturated

shattuckite.audit_status: new v93 (2026-05-19). Type locality Shattuck mine, Bisbee — its absence was geologically embarrassing (the mineral is literally named after a Bisbee mine). Five habit dispatch paths: acicular tuft (default Bisbee vug), spherulitic rosette (Kaokoveld + high σ), pseudomorph after malachite (Bisbee signature replacement), pseudomorph after azurite (Mesopotamia Kaokoveld), massive granular (low-σ fill). Per research dossier 2026-05 + Schaller 1915 + Evans & Mrose 1977.
shattuckite._late_stage_note: Very late supergene — shattuckite post-dates malachite/azurite (replacement mechanism requires their dissolution), post-dates plancheite (Cu-rich rim on plancheite cores), pre-dates only chrysocolla and minor late calcite. propensity:0.8 matches its position as the latest non-chrysocolla Cu-silicate.

habit_variants.name: cubic_galena_structure
habit_variants.trigger: default — small lead-gray cubes (galena structure inherited)

habit_variants.name: massive_granular
habit_variants.trigger: low T — massive granular disseminated grain

habit_variants.name: exsolution_lamellae_in_galena
habit_variants.trigger: cooling from PbS-PbSe solid solution below 300°C — lamellar exsolution within galena host

clausthalite.description: PbSe — the selenide analog of galena. Forms in low-sulfur hydrothermal veins where Se is enriched relative to S, typically alongside other selenides (tiemannite HgSe, klockmannite Cu₂Se, naumannite Ag₂Se). Above 300°C it forms continuous solid solution with galena; below 300°C the miscibility gap opens and the two unmix into separate phases — high-T clausthalite/galena solid solutions exsolve on cooling, producing diagnostic lamellar textures. Type locality Clausthal-Zellerfeld (Harz Mountains, Germany, 1832). Important in some sandstone-type uranium deposits and in Cu-Au-U systems like Olympic Dam.

trace_ingredients.Cu: klockmannite Cu2Se co-precipitation
trace_ingredients.S: above 300°C → solid solution with galena; below 300°C → exsolution lamellae

clausthalite.thermal_decomp_reaction: PbSe melts at 1065°C; solid-solution exsolution with galena below 300°C

twin_laws.name: spinel_law
twin_laws._source: Ramdohr 1980 §4.2 selenide section. PbSe is isostructural with galena (cubic Fm3̄m). Twinning data sparse — most clausthalite occurrences are massive replacement bodies. Conservative p=0.005 (rare-twin floor) mirroring galena (where spinel-law twins are documented but uncommon).

acid_dissolution.reaction: PbSe + 4HNO3 -> Pb(NO3)2 + Se + 2NO2 + 2H2O

clausthalite.audit_status: new (brief-16 batch 2026-05-07). First Pb-selenide. Pairs with naumannite (Ag-Se) and tiemannite (Hg-Se, not in spec) for selenide assemblages. schneeberg + colorado_plateau scenarios are uranium-bearing low-S settings where Se is enriched. Both broths need Se ≥1 ppm. No engine yet.

habit_variants.name: hexagonal_pyramidal
habit_variants.trigger: default — hemimorphic six-sided pyramid (different terminations top/bottom)

habit_variants.name: powdery_coating
habit_variants.trigger: high σ + low T — bright yellow earthy coating on sphalerite

habit_variants.name: colloform_crust
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ — encrusting colloform aggregate

habit_variants.name: radial_aggregate
habit_variants.trigger: low σ + slow growth — radial bundle of pyramidal crystals

greenockite.description: CdS — the hexagonal (wurtzite-structure) cadmium sulfide. The 'cadmium yellow' pigment, mineral form. Forms by supergene oxidation of Cd-bearing sphalerite: Cd substitutes for Zn in primary sphalerite up to ~1%, gets liberated when sphalerite oxidizes, and re-precipitates as CdS when Cd²⁺ encounters residual sulfide in the supergene zone. Ksp ~10⁻²⁸ — extreme insolubility means trace Cd precipitates readily. Hemimorphic crystals (different terminations at top vs. bottom) are diagnostic. Type locality Bishopton, Scotland (1840 railway tunnel cut). Tsumeb (Namibia) is the modern reference. Hexagonal greenockite is the high-T polymorph (>200°C); cubic hawleyite is the low-T form.

trace_ingredients.Zn: Zn substitution stabilizes hexagonal form; activates UV fluorescence

greenockite.thermal_decomp_reaction: CdS oxidizes to CdO + SO2 above ~600°C in air; melts at 980°C

acid_dissolution.reaction: CdS + 8HNO3 (hot) -> Cd(NO3)2 + H2SO4 + 6NO2 + 2H2O

greenockite.audit_status: new (brief-16 batch 2026-05-07). Adds new broth field Cd to FluidChemistry (commit pending). Pairs with hawleyite (cubic dimorph) — same chemistry, different polymorph gated by T. supergene_oxidation + mvt broths need Cd ≥0.5 ppm (currently 0). No engine yet.

habit_variants.name: powdery_coating
habit_variants.trigger: default — bright yellow earthy dust on sphalerite/siderite (the only known habit)

habit_variants.name: earthy_crust
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ — slightly thicker yellow crust

hawleyite.description: CdS — the cubic (sphalerite-structure) polymorph of greenockite. Identical chemistry, different lattice. Hawleyite is the kinetically favored form at low T (<100°C); it is thermodynamically metastable relative to hexagonal greenockite but persists indefinitely once formed. Always microcrystalline — no specimen anywhere has shown a discrete crystal. Bright cadmium yellow powdery coatings on sphalerite, siderite, galena in oxidation zones. Indistinguishable from greenockite by sight; XRD is the only test. Type locality Hector-Calumet mine, Yukon (1955). Most 'greenockite' in old collections is probably hawleyite.
hawleyite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Same as greenockite — CdS oxidizes to CdO + SO2 above ~600°C
hawleyite._twin_laws_note: Intentionally empty — hawleyite (cubic F4̄3m, CdS) is the powdery cubic CdS dimorph of greenockite, occurring only as yellow powdery coatings on sphalerite/marcasite in oxidation zones. Never forms individual euhedral crystals; habit code 'powdery_coating' signals non-individual. v137 sulfide batch.

acid_dissolution.reaction: CdS + 8HNO3 (hot) -> Cd(NO3)2 + H2SO4 + 6NO2 + 2H2O

hawleyite.audit_status: new (brief-16 batch 2026-05-07). Cubic dimorph of greenockite — gated by T (< ~100°C). Habit is always powdery; no crystal habit logic needed. supergene_oxidation broth needs Cd ≥0.5 ppm. No engine yet.

habit_variants.name: cubic_high_T
habit_variants.trigger: default at T > 155°C — small lead-gray cubes

habit_variants.name: monoclinic_low_T_lamellae
habit_variants.trigger: T < 155°C — monoclinic phase with phase-transformation lamellae from cubic→monoclinic transition

habit_variants.name: granular_massive
habit_variants.trigger: high σ — granular disseminated aggregate

hessite.description: Ag2Te — the silver-telluride end-member of the silver chalcogenide family. Forms in low-sulfidation epithermal veins where Te is enriched and S is suppressed; wins over acanthite (Ag2S) only when S²⁻ is locally depleted. Phase transition at 155°C (cubic ↔ monoclinic) leaves diagnostic lamellar textures in slow-cooled specimens — a game-readable thermometer. Cripple Creek (Colorado), Sacarîmb (Romania), Kalgoorlie (Western Australia) are the type telluride districts. Often a major silver carrier in epithermal Au-Ag deposits. Forms a continuous spectrum with naumannite (Ag2Se) where Se substitutes for Te.

trace_ingredients.Cu: petzite (CuAg3Te2) substitution

twin_laws.name: contact_110
twin_laws._source: Ramdohr 1980 §4.8 telluride section + Anthony Handbook v.I. Hessite (Ag₂Te, monoclinic at room T, cubic above 150°C) shows {110} polysynthetic twin lamellae from the inversion + growth twins on contact planes. Documented at Săcărâmb (Romania) and Calaveras (California). Conservative p=0.02 — visible twins moderate in well-formed specimens.

acid_dissolution.reaction: Ag2Te + HNO3 -> Ag+ + TeO2 + NO + H2O

hessite.audit_status: new (brief-16 batch 2026-05-07). Pairs with calaverite (Au-Te), sylvanite (Au-Ag-Te), naumannite (Ag-Se). gem_pegmatite + porphyry are placeholders until an epithermal_telluride scenario lands. Both need Te ≥1 ppm. No engine yet.

habit_variants.name: cubic_high_T
habit_variants.trigger: default at T > 133°C — small iron-gray cubes (high ionic conductivity 2 S/cm)

habit_variants.name: orthorhombic_low_T
habit_variants.trigger: T < 133°C — orthorhombic phase

habit_variants.name: granular_massive
habit_variants.trigger: supergene — disseminated micrograins

naumannite.description: Ag2Se — the silver-selenide end-member, sister to hessite (Ag2Te). Forms in selenium-enriched epithermal veins where S is suppressed; wins over acanthite (Ag2S) when Se ≫ S, which is rare globally. Phase transition at 133°C (orthorhombic ↔ cubic) — the high-T cubic form has unusual ionic conductivity (2 S/cm). Type region: Harz Mountains (Germany), where the selenide-bearing low-S hydrothermal veins were first characterized in the 19th century. De Lamar Mine (Idaho) is the major US producer of selenide-type silver ore. Forms a continuous Te ↔ Se substitution series with hessite.

twin_laws.name: inversion_110
twin_laws._source: Ramdohr 1980 §4.2 selenide section. Naumannite (Ag₂Se) has an inversion analogous to hessite (orthorhombic at room T, pseudo-cubic above ~133°C). Twin behavior data-sparse — Ramdohr notes inversion lamellae but no detailed twin-law characterization. Conservative p=0.005 (rare-twin floor).

acid_dissolution.reaction: Ag2Se + 4HNO3 -> 2AgNO3 + H2SeO3 + 2NO + H2O

naumannite.audit_status: new (brief-16 batch 2026-05-07). Selenide pair to hessite. schneeberg scenario already enriched in Se for clausthalite — needs Se ≥1 ppm. No engine yet.

habit_variants.name: tabular_thin_001
habit_variants.trigger: default — paper-thin {001} tablets

habit_variants.name: pulverulent_crust
habit_variants.trigger: high σ + low T — crusty/pulverulent supergene coating

habit_variants.name: dipyramidal_rare
habit_variants.trigger: rare — equant dipyramid

powellite.description: CaMoO4 — the supergene oxidation product of molybdenite. Forms when MoS2 weathers in the oxidation zone: S²⁻ is lost as sulfate, Mo⁶⁺ pairs with available Ca²⁺ from host carbonate, and powellite crystallizes as thin tabular plates with adamantine luster. Same crystal structure as scheelite (CaWO4); powellite-scheelite forms a continuous solid-solution series, and intermediate compositions (yellow with blue/black W-rich zones) are common. Diagnostic UV fluorescence: bright yellow under SW (vs. scheelite's blue) — the difference is W vs. Mo activator in the same lattice. Nasik (India) Deccan basalt cavities are the modern aesthetic reference; Bingham Canyon (Utah) is the canonical porphyry-Cu-Mo oxidation occurrence.

trace_ingredients.W: scheelite component in solid solution; blue/black zones in W-rich material

powellite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Refractory; same lattice stability as scheelite

fluorescence.activator: self-activated CaMoO4 lattice (charge-transfer in MoO4 tetrahedra)

acid_dissolution.reaction: CaMoO4 + 2H+ -> Ca2+ + H2MoO4

powellite.audit_status: new (brief-16 batch 2026-05-07). Mo end-member of powellite-scheelite SS. supergene_oxidation broth needs Mo ≥1 ppm; deccan_zeolite supports basalt-cavity habit. Reuses same engine logic as scheelite with mo_fraction → 1.0. No engine yet.

habit_variants.name: dipyramidal
habit_variants.trigger: default — pseudo-octahedral dipyramid

habit_variants.name: tabular
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ — flat tablets

habit_variants.name: octahedral_pseudo
habit_variants.trigger: low σ — pseudo-octahedral habit (looks cubic but is tetragonal)

habit_variants.name: massive_granular
habit_variants.trigger: high σ — massive granular skarn texture

scheelite.description: CaWO4 — the calcium tungstate, one of the principal W ores and a UV-fluorescence classic. Forms in granitic-intrusion-related W-Sn skarns and in mesothermal vein systems where W concentrates from late-stage granitic fluids and Ca comes from the wallrock. Brilliant blue-white SW UV fluorescence is diagnostic — used since the 19th century to prospect for tungsten in the field. Forms continuous solid-solution series with powellite (CaMoO4); intermediate compositions show duller / yellower fluorescence as Mo replaces W. Panasqueira (Portugal) and Pine Creek (California) are skarn type localities. Recent Chinese tungsten production (Hunan, Jiangxi) dominates global supply.

trace_ingredients.Mo: powellite component in solid solution; shifts fluorescence from blue to yellow
trace_ingredients.REE: substitute for Ca; some scheelite is REE-bearing

fluorescence.activator: self-activated CaWO4 lattice (charge-transfer in WO4 tetrahedra)
fluorescence.quencher: Fe²⁺ + Mo (shifts toward yellow as W → Mo)

scheelite.audit_status: new (brief-16 batch 2026-05-07). W end-member of powellite-scheelite SS. gem_pegmatite + porphyry broths need W ≥1 ppm (currently 0). Pairs with wolframite — Ca → scheelite, Fe/Mn → wolframite. No engine yet.

habit_variants.name: prismatic
habit_variants.trigger: default — slender orthorhombic prism

habit_variants.name: acicular_fibrous
habit_variants.trigger: low T + high σ — fibrous needle

habit_variants.name: radial_spray
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ — radiating fan/spray

habit_variants.name: stout_prismatic
habit_variants.trigger: higher T — short stocky prism

habit_variants.name: twinned_pseudohexagonal
habit_variants.trigger: almost always — pseudohex via cyclic twinning (aragonite-group habit)

strontianite.description: SrCO3 — the strontium carbonate, type locality Strontian (Scotland, 1790) and the namesake of the element strontium itself. Aragonite-group orthorhombic carbonate; almost always twinned cyclically into pseudohexagonal forms. Forms in low-T hydrothermal veins where Sr-bearing fluids encounter alkaline carbonate conditions; loses to celestine (SrSO4) when sulfate is the dominant Sr-pairing anion, which is most of the time globally — celestine is far more common because oceans deliver SO4 more freely than CO3. Almost always UV-fluorescent yellow-white and persistently phosphorescent. Münsterland (Germany) acicular sprays and Meckley's Quarry (Pennsylvania) fluorescent specimens are the collectors' references.

trace_ingredients.Ca: Ca substitutes up to 27% of Sr

twin_laws.name: pseudohex_cyclic_110
twin_laws.trigger: moderate σ — almost always cyclic twinned

acid_dissolution.reaction: SrCO3 + 2H+ -> Sr2+ + H2O + CO2

strontianite.audit_status: new (brief-16 batch 2026-05-07). Carbonate end-member for Sr. Pairs with celestine (SrSO4) — sulfate vs. carbonate competition gates which forms. marble_contact_metamorphism + deccan_zeolite broths support carbonate chemistry; need Sr ≥30 ppm. No engine yet.

habit_variants.name: bladed_graphic
habit_variants.trigger: default — intergrown bladed crystals like cuneiform script ('graphic tellurium')

habit_variants.name: tabular_striated
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ — striated tabular plates

habit_variants.name: granular_massive
habit_variants.trigger: low T — granular aggregate

sylvanite.description: (Au,Ag)Te2 with Au:Ag typically 1:1 to 3:1 — the gold-silver telluride that sits between calaverite (pure Au) and hessite (pure Ag) on the telluride spectrum. Photosensitive: prolonged light exposure produces a dark surface tarnish (the gold is still in the lattice but the surface oxidizes). Type locality Sacarîmb / Nagyág (Transylvania, Romania); also Cripple Creek (Colorado), Kalgoorlie (Western Australia), Emperor mine (Vatukoula, Fiji). Miners called it 'graphic tellurium' for the bladed-cuneiform intergrowth pattern. Note: sylvanite ≠ sylvite (KCl) — they share no chemistry, just a Latin root for the same Carpathian forest range.

twin_laws.name: graphic_twin_100
twin_laws._source: Ramdohr 1980 §4.8 telluride section + Anthony Handbook v.I. Sylvanite ((Au,Ag)Te₂, monoclinic P2/c) takes its name from the diagnostic "graphic" intergrowth at Sacarîmb (formerly Nagyág) — bladed crystals whose {100} contact twins produce hieroglyph-like outlines. The graphic twin is the species visual signature; p=0.05 reflects that the morphology is common in well-formed specimens but the underlying twin probability per nucleation is still moderate.

sylvanite.audit_status: new (brief-16 batch 2026-05-07). Au-Ag-Te bridge between calaverite (Au-Te) and hessite (Ag-Te). Photosensitive flag is a unique cosmetic-decay mechanic — could drive a 'specimen darkens with display history' feature. gem_pegmatite + porphyry placeholders. No engine yet.

habit_variants.name: cubic
habit_variants.trigger: default — perfect cubic crystal (cubic cleavage at 90°)

habit_variants.name: hopper_cube
habit_variants.trigger: high fill or rapid evaporation — stepped/terraced 'hopper' faces from boundary-layer diffusion

habit_variants.name: granular_massive
habit_variants.trigger: high σ — granular salt-pan fabric

sylvite.description: KCl — the potassium chloride evaporite. Forms in the late stage of evaporite sequences, after halite (NaCl) has already removed most of the sodium and chloride from the brine; sylvite is what crystallizes from the K-enriched residual liquor. More soluble than halite (344 g/L at 25°C vs. halite's 360 g/L is incorrect — sylvite is actually slightly LESS soluble than halite at 25°C, but sylvite's solubility increases sharply with T while halite's barely changes, so cooling brines favor sylvite precipitation late). Deliquescent in humid air. Type locality Stassfurt (Germany, Zechstein evaporites); Carlsbad (New Mexico, Permian basin) and Solikamsk (Russia, Upper Kama) are the major commercial sources. Note: sylvite ≠ sylvanite — different chemistry, similar Latin root.

trace_ingredients.Na: halite co-precipitates first; sylvite forms from residual K-rich brine
trace_ingredients.Mg: high Mg → carnallite (KMgCl3·6H2O) forms after sylvite
trace_ingredients.Br: Br substitutes for Cl in some sylvite (used as paleo-environment indicator)

twin_laws.name: penetration_111
twin_laws._source: Anthony Handbook v.III (halides) + Dana 8th. Sylvite (cubic Fm-3m, KCl) is halite-isostructural; rare {111} penetration twins are documented in some Carlsbad (NM) potash specimens. Most sylvite occurs as cubic massive in evaporite K-salt beds (Stassfurt Germany, Saskatchewan basin) where visible twinning is uncommon. p=0.005 matching halite's rare-twin floor (halite has its own twin_laws entry at v9).

sylvite.audit_status: new (brief-16 batch 2026-05-07). Late-stage evaporite K-Cl. Pairs with existing halite — Na precipitates first, K precipitates second from residual brine. searles_lake + sabkha_dolomitization broths need K bumped to ≥50 ppm. No engine yet.
sylvite._late_stage_note: Proposal C (2026-05): propensity:1. Late-stage K evaporite (Stassfurt / Carlsbad / Solikamsk); same as fill_exempt set.
sylvite._retune_note_fill_exempt: Backlog K (2026-05): added fill_exempt:true. Sylvite is the textbook late-stage evaporite — its description literally says 'Forms in the late stage of evaporite sequences, after halite has already removed most of the sodium and chloride from the brine.' Sylvite-after-halite is the canonical Stassfurt / Carlsbad / Solikamsk paragenesis. The global vugFill cap was structurally preventing the late half of that sequence from running. max_nucleation_count:4 keeps total count modest.

habit_variants.name: pseudohexagonal_twinned
habit_variants.trigger: default — almost always cyclic-twinned into pseudohexagonal pyramid (Settlingstones aesthetic)

habit_variants.name: prismatic
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ — single orthorhombic prism (rare without twinning)

habit_variants.name: botryoidal_white
habit_variants.trigger: low T + high σ — white botryoidal balls (Settlingstones type specimens)

habit_variants.name: columnar
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ — columnar fibrous aggregate

witherite.description: BaCO3 — the barium carbonate, sister to barite (BaSO4). Almost always cyclically twinned into pseudohexagonal pyramidal forms — a single un-twinned witherite crystal is unusual. Forms in low-T hydrothermal veins where Ba²⁺ encounters carbonate-rich fluids and sulfate is suppressed; loses to barite when SO4 is dominant, which is most environments. Type locality Alston Moor (Cumbria, England, 1784); William Withering (also discoverer of digitalis) named the species. Settlingstones Mine (Northumberland, England) is the world's reference for botryoidal white masses. Cave-in-Rock (Illinois) is the classic American MVT-fluorite-witherite-barite locality. Strongly UV-fluorescent (bluish-white) and persistently phosphorescent.

acid_dissolution.reaction: BaCO3 + 2H+ -> Ba2+ + H2O + CO2

witherite.audit_status: new (brief-16 batch 2026-05-07). Carbonate end-member for Ba — Cave-in-Rock fluorite-barite-witherite assemblage. Pairs with existing barite (BaSO4). mvt scenario already supports the Ba+CO3+F+Pb+Zn assemblage; should fire alongside fluorite + barite + galena + sphalerite. No engine yet.

habit_variants.name: bladed_tabular
habit_variants.trigger: default — bladed flattened crystal (Panasqueira aesthetic)

habit_variants.name: prismatic_striated
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ — striated prism

habit_variants.name: equant_high_T
habit_variants.trigger: high T (> 400°C) — equant blocky crystal

habit_variants.name: massive_granular
habit_variants.trigger: high σ — granular vein aggregate

wolframite.description: (Fe,Mn)WO4 — the Fe-Mn tungstate, intermediate member of the ferberite (FeWO4) ↔ hübnerite (MnWO4) solid-solution series. Specific gravity 7.0–7.5 — three times denser than quartz — is the field diagnostic. Forms in W-Sn vein systems associated with granitic intrusions, at 300–400°C in mildly acidic, reducing fluids carrying hundreds of ppm dissolved W. Loses to scheelite (CaWO4) when Ca is available, which means wolframite indicates Ca-poor conditions. Refractory: stable to very high T and chemically resistant. Does not fluoresce (diagnostic distinction from scheelite which glows blue-white under SW). Panasqueira (Portugal) is the world reference for bladed crystals on quartz; Yaogangxian (Hunan, China) supplies the modern collector market. Etymology: 'wolf's froth' — medieval miners noted that the ore consumed tin yields like a predator.
wolframite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Refractory; does not decompose at vug temperatures
wolframite.audit_status: new (brief-16 batch 2026-05-07). Pairs with scheelite — Ca → scheelite, Fe/Mn → wolframite. Fe/(Fe+Mn) ratio drives ferberite-hübnerite solid solution and color. gem_pegmatite + porphyry broths need W ≥1 ppm. Non-fluorescence is diagnostic vs. scheelite. No engine yet.

habit_variants.name: rhombohedral_cochineal
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ, hot-spring T, deep red rhombohedron habit (Sulphur Bank, Almadén, Idria)

habit_variants.name: massive_red
habit_variants.trigger: high σ, hot-spring vent, vermillion massive aggregate

habit_variants.name: default_rhombohedral
habit_variants.trigger: default — cochineal-red rhombohedron

cinnabar.description: HgS — trigonal mercury sulfide, the principal ore of mercury and the deepest cochineal red of all minerals. Nicknamed 'dragon's blood' for the pigment ancient Romans extracted from Almadén. Specific gravity 8.0-8.2 is the highest of any common ore mineral (the heft is unmistakable in hand). Decomposes at ~580°C (releases Hg vapor); destroyed by oxidation above O₂ ~1.2 (sublimes as Hg⁰ + SO₄²⁻). Type locality Almadén (Spain, mined for 2000+ years). The pigment vermillion is synthetic cinnabar.

trace_ingredients.Se: minor Se substitution (selenium cinnabar at Sulphur Bank)

cinnabar.thermal_decomp_reaction: HgS → Hg(vapor) + S above 580°C (sublimation route; below this, stable)

twin_laws.name: contact_0001
twin_laws._source: Dana 8th + Anthony Handbook v.I. Cinnabar (trigonal P3₂21) shows {0001} simple contact twins ("penetration twins" of older usage) and twins on {10-11}, well documented in Almadén (Spain) and Idrija (Slovenia) specimens. The contact twin on basal pinacoid is the most-cited variety; p=0.05 reflects the minor-common field frequency at the classic localities.

acid_dissolution.reaction: Cinnabar is acid-resistant under normal vug pH ranges. Aqua regia required for dissolution; ordinary HCl/H2SO4 leave it untouched. The reason cinnabar accumulates in oxidation zones intact while other Hg minerals weather away.

cinnabar.audit_status: new (2026-05-18). Lit by sulphur_bank scenario — the eponymous Mercury Mine commodity. Future scenarios: Almadén (Spain), Idria (Slovenia), New Almaden (CA).

habit_variants.name: prismatic_red
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ, hot-spring T, the classic Allchar / Shimen elongated prism

habit_variants.name: granular_orange
habit_variants.trigger: high σ, hot-spring vent, granular massive orange-red aggregate

habit_variants.name: sublimation_crust_red
habit_variants.trigger: fumarole T (~150°C), sublimation crust on vent walls (Yellowstone Norris Geyser Basin)

habit_variants.name: default_prismatic
habit_variants.trigger: default — orange-red prismatic

realgar.description: AsS — monoclinic arsenic sulfide (α-realgar; the lower-S phase of the As–S system). Orange-red to vermillion red, brilliant resinous luster, soft (1.5-2 hardness). Low-T hot-spring + epithermal-vein habit; co-occurs with orpiment (As₂S₃) in the same fluids. Type locality Allchar, North Macedonia (also a thallium-anomaly site). World-largest producer is Shimen / Hunan, China. Active hot-spring depositional sites include Yellowstone Norris Geyser Basin + Carbazo Springs. Photo-degrades slowly to pararealgar (yellow) under UV — a known museum-curation problem.

trace_ingredients.Tl: thallium-rich realgar at Allchar (TlAs₅S₈ avicennite/lorandite paragenesis)

realgar.thermal_decomp_reaction: AsS → As₂S₃ + As at ~320°C; melts at ~321°C in air

twin_laws.name: contact_100
twin_laws._source: Anthony Handbook v.I (sulfide section). Realgar (monoclinic P2₁/n) shows {100} contact twins, documented in Mercur (Utah), Allchar (North Macedonia), and Shimen (China) low-T epithermal specimens. Conservative p=0.02 — twins visible in prismatic specimens but rare.

acid_dissolution.reaction: Realgar is generally acid-resistant in the vug-pH range. Strong alkali (pH > 9) dissolves it via sulfide complex formation.

pararealgar_yellow.trigger: UV exposure (post-deposition alteration; not modeled in run-time)

realgar.audit_status: new (2026-05-19). Co-deposits with orpiment in hot-spring + epithermal scenarios. Lit by sulphur_bank.

habit_variants.name: foliated_golden
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ, hot-spring T, the classic foliated golden-yellow plates

habit_variants.name: columnar_yellow
habit_variants.trigger: lower σ + steady growth, columnar habit (Getchell mine specimens)

habit_variants.name: granular_yellow
habit_variants.trigger: high σ, granular massive aggregate

habit_variants.name: default_foliated
habit_variants.trigger: default — foliated golden plates

orpiment.description: As₂S₃ — monoclinic arsenic sulfide (the higher-S phase of the As–S system). Brilliant golden-yellow to lemon-yellow, pearly luster on cleavage. Soft (1.5-2 hardness), perfect {010} cleavage producing the characteristic foliated 'gilded plates'. Low-T hot-spring + epithermal habit; co-occurs with realgar (AsS) in the same fluid systems. The 'aurum pigmentum' of antiquity (the source of the name; ancient/medieval alchemists believed it contained gold). Type locality Allchar, North Macedonia. Major producers Getchell + Twin Creeks (Nevada Carlin-type deposits), Shimen (China), Hunan. Photo-stable (unlike realgar).

trace_ingredients.Au: Au-bearing orpiment at Carlin-type deposits (invisible gold trace)

orpiment.thermal_decomp_reaction: As₂S₃ melts at ~310°C; volatilizes above 250°C in air

twin_laws.name: contact_100
twin_laws._source: Anthony Handbook v.I. Orpiment is monoclinic P2₁/n, foliated golden masses dominated by {010} cleavage. {100} contact twinning is reported but less common than in realgar because orpiment crystals are typically foliated/columnar rather than equant. Conservative p=0.01 (data sparse — most field orpiment is foliated/massive).

acid_dissolution.reaction: Orpiment is acid-resistant in the vug-pH range. Strong alkali (pH > 9.5) dissolves it as thioarsenite complexes (AsS₃³⁻).

orpiment.audit_status: new (2026-05-19). Co-deposits with realgar in hot-spring + epithermal scenarios. Lit by sulphur_bank.

habit_variants.name: yellow_pseudomorph
habit_variants.trigger: default — bright yellow pseudomorph after realgar (light-induced isomerization)

habit_variants.name: default_yellow_pseudomorph
habit_variants.trigger: default

pararealgar.description: As4S4 — monoclinic arsenic sulfide. STRUCTURAL ISOMER of realgar (same As4S4 composition, different As-cage symmetry: realgar D2d, pararealgar Cs). Bright yellow (vs realgar's orange-red), softer (Mohs 1-1.5 vs 1.5-2). Forms EXCLUSIVELY as a light-induced transformation product of realgar over weeks to years of visible-light (>500 nm) exposure. The transformation is IRREVERSIBLE — once realgar becomes pararealgar, it doesn't go back. The yellow powder that crumbles out of old realgar specimens in museum drawers IS pararealgar. The only light-induced transformation modeled in the simulator. Storage in the dark preserves realgar indefinitely; exposure to ordinary room light slowly destroys it. (Bonazzi et al. 1996 Mineralogical Magazine; Roberts et al. 1980.)
pararealgar.thermal_decomp_reaction: Pararealgar (As4S4) — same thermal decomposition as realgar; melts ~321°C in air.
pararealgar._twin_laws_note: Intentionally empty — pararealgar (monoclinic P2₁/c, As₄S₄) is a photodecomposition pseudomorph after realgar, forming as a yellow surface alteration crust rather than as primary euhedral crystals. Habit code 'yellow_pseudomorph' signals non-individual. v137 sulfide batch.

acid_dissolution.reaction: Same acid/alkali behavior as realgar — resistant to dilute acids, dissolves in strong alkali via thioarsenite complex.

pararealgar.audit_status: new (2026-05-19). Post-formation transformation of realgar via light-induced isomerization; not a nucleation. Per research-meta-minerals-pararealgar.md. Engine path: LIGHT_TRANSITIONS in js/75-transitions.ts.

habit_variants.name: pseudomorph
habit_variants.trigger: inherited from parent autunite crystal

meta-autunite.description: Ca(UO₂)₂(PO₄)₂·8H₂O — autunite with three water molecules gone. Tetragonal uranyl phosphate; appears in the simulator only as a paramorph product of autunite, after heat (>80°C) or sustained dry-air exposure drives the irreversible dehydration. External crystal shape is preserved (the square tabular {001} habit, the lemon-yellow color, the field marks all inherited from the parent autunite); the lattice has lost ~27% of its structural water and partially collapsed. Specific gravity slightly higher (3.45 vs autunite's 3.14); luster duller; fluorescence weaker (still present — uranyl chromophore survives the dehydration, just dampened). MOST AUTUNITE SPECIMENS IN MUSEUMS are actually meta-autunite — fresh autunite has typically been on a shelf for years before reaching a collector. The dehydration is irreversible: once water leaves the structure, it doesn't come back, even in 100% humidity. Sources: research/research-autunite.md §Variants 1-2 (boss canonical); Roberts, Ansell, Bonardi 1980 — group structural studies. (Bonazzi et al. — autunite-group dehydration thermodynamics.)
meta-autunite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Meta-autunite I (8 H₂O) → meta-autunite II (~3 H₂O) on further heating; >200°C structural collapse.

fluorescence.quencher: Fe (Fe²⁺ + Fe³⁺ both suppress uranyl emission); dehydration weakens but does not eliminate fluorescence
fluorescence.status: weakened uranyl fluorescence relative to autunite — partial structural collapse dampens but does not extinguish the (UO₂)²⁺ emission. Still visibly green-yellow under LW UV (365nm), just less intense than fresh autunite.

meta-autunite._twin_laws_note: Intentionally empty — meta-autunite (Ca(UO₂)₂(PO₄)₂·8H₂O, tetragonal P4₂/n) is the dehydration paramorph of autunite at T > 80°C. It inherits autunite's basal {001} contact twin geometry through the paramorph transition (PARAMORPH_TRANSITIONS dispatch in 16-paramorph-transitions.ts) rather than nucleating fresh — so adding its own twin_laws entry would double-count and isn't engine-correct. The parent autunite carries the twin signal. v137 sulfide convention extended to v138 phosphate paramorphs.

acid_dissolution.reaction: Inherits autunite's acid sensitivity — Ca(UO₂)₂(PO₄)₂·8H₂O + 6H⁺ → Ca²⁺ + 2(UO₂)²⁺ + 2H₃PO₄ + 8H₂O

pale_yellow.trigger: freshly-dehydrated specimen — partial collapse but still visibly yellow

meta-autunite.audit_status: new (2026-05-19). Paramorph product of autunite via DEHYDRATION_TRANSITIONS; not a nucleation. Per research-autunite.md §Variants for Game §1. Mirrors the tincalconite/thenardite pattern. No engine path.
meta-autunite._late_stage_note: Paramorph after autunite — only ever late by definition (dehydration product). Mirrors tincalconite/thenardite.

habit_variants.name: pseudomorph
habit_variants.trigger: inherited from parent torbernite crystal

metatorbernite.description: Cu(UO₂)₂(PO₄)₂·8H₂O — torbernite with four water molecules gone. Tetragonal uranyl phosphate; appears in the simulator only as a paramorph product of torbernite, after heat (>75°C) or sustained dry-air exposure drives the irreversible loss of 4 of the 12 structural H₂O. The emerald-green color persists but pales toward apple-green / yellow-green as the lattice collapses; specific gravity rises (3.7 vs torbernite's 3.22); crystals become slightly more friable along the {001} cleavage. Critically: in the Schneeberg / Cornwall museum collections, very few specimens are now fresh torbernite — the trip from a damp mine adit to a dry display case is exactly what triggers the transformation. Like meta-autunite, the conversion is IRREVERSIBLE: even returning a metatorbernite to 100% humidity will not re-hydrate the lattice. Sources: research/research-torbernite.md §Dehydration Transformation (boss canonical 3bfdf4a).
metatorbernite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Further heating drives loss of remaining water; >200°C structural collapse to anhydrous Cu uranyl phosphate.
metatorbernite._twin_laws_note: Intentionally empty — metatorbernite (Cu(UO₂)₂(PO₄)₂·8H₂O, tetragonal P4/nmm) is the dehydration paramorph of torbernite at T > 75°C. Inherits torbernite's documented twin geometry through paramorph transition rather than nucleating fresh. Same rationale as meta-autunite (v138).

acid_dissolution.reaction: Inherits torbernite's acid sensitivity — Cu(UO₂)₂(PO₄)₂·8H₂O + 6H⁺ → Cu²⁺ + 2(UO₂)²⁺ + 2H₃PO₄ + 8H₂O

apple_green.trigger: freshly-dehydrated specimen — partial collapse but still bright

metatorbernite.audit_status: new (2026-05-19). Paramorph product of torbernite via DEHYDRATION_TRANSITIONS; not a nucleation. Per research-torbernite.md §Dehydration. Non-fluorescent (inherits Cu²⁺ quenching from parent torbernite). No engine path.
metatorbernite._late_stage_note: Paramorph after torbernite — only ever late by definition (dehydration product).

habit_variants.name: pseudomorph
habit_variants.trigger: inherited from parent zeunerite crystal

metazeunerite.description: Cu(UO₂)₂(AsO₄)₂·8H₂O — zeunerite with four water molecules gone. Tetragonal uranyl arsenate; the As-anion analog of metatorbernite (the two are isostructural just as the parents are). Appears in the simulator only as a paramorph product of zeunerite, after heat (>75°C) or dry-air exposure drives the irreversible 12→8 H₂O collapse. Color paler than zeunerite's emerald green, trending grass-green or yellow-green; specific gravity rises (3.85 vs zeunerite's ~3.4). Same museum-storage problem as zeunerite — most cabinet specimens labeled 'zeunerite' from Schneeberg, Cornwall, or Wittichen are actually metazeunerite, dehydrated decades ago. Cu²⁺ still quenches the uranyl emission, so non-fluorescent. The Schneeberg cation+anion fork mechanic still applies at nucleation time; only the dehydration follows. Sources: research/research-zeunerite.md §Dehydration Transformation; Pinch & Wilson 1977.
metazeunerite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Further heating drives loss of remaining water; >200°C structural collapse to anhydrous Cu uranyl arsenate.
metazeunerite._twin_laws_note: Intentionally empty — metazeunerite (Cu(UO₂)₂(AsO₄)₂·8H₂O, tetragonal P4/nmm) is the dehydration paramorph of zeunerite at T > 75°C. Inherits zeunerite's documented {001} basal contact twin geometry through PARAMORPH_TRANSITIONS dispatch (16-paramorph-transitions.ts) rather than nucleating fresh. Adding own twin_laws would double-count the cascade. Same paramorph-inheritance rationale as meta-autunite + metatorbernite (v138 phosphate batch).

acid_dissolution.reaction: Inherits zeunerite's acid sensitivity — Cu(UO₂)₂(AsO₄)₂·8H₂O + 6H⁺ → Cu²⁺ + 2(UO₂)²⁺ + 2H₃AsO₄ + 8H₂O

yellow_green.trigger: advanced dehydration — Cu²⁺ pales without uranyl support

metazeunerite.audit_status: new (2026-05-19). Paramorph product of zeunerite via DEHYDRATION_TRANSITIONS; not a nucleation. Per research-zeunerite.md §Dehydration. Non-fluorescent (inherits Cu²⁺ quenching from parent zeunerite). No engine path.
metazeunerite._late_stage_note: Paramorph after zeunerite — only ever late by definition (dehydration product).

habit_variants.name: massive_sooty
habit_variants.trigger: default supergene habit — soils-the-fingers black powder, the field-recognizable Mn-weathering texture (65% of natural occurrences)

habit_variants.name: botryoidal_reniform
habit_variants.trigger: Mode A supergene, higher Mn (>5 ppm), pH 7-8, stable groundwater table — mammillary surfaces, classic 'manganese rind'

habit_variants.name: radiating_fibrous
habit_variants.trigger: Mode B hydrothermal vein (T > 100), replacement of manganite needles — cleavage perpendicular to vug wall, up to 8 cm; the Ilfeld 'polianite-style' habit

habit_variants.name: prismatic_crystal
habit_variants.trigger: rare Mode B slow-growth on clean vug wall — long-prismatic ‖ [001], {110} tetragonal prism, square cross-section (Platten / Ilmenau type material)

habit_variants.name: pseudomorph_after_rhodochrosite
habit_variants.trigger: supergene oxidation of rhodochrosite — the 'rotted rhomb' texture, MnCO3 + 0.5 O2 → MnO2 + CO2, preserves rhomb outline as sooty black

habit_variants.name: pseudomorph_after_manganite
habit_variants.trigger: the polianite paramorph — γ-MnOOH → β-MnO2 + 0.5 H2O via 15% b-axis contraction (Champness 1971), inherits manganite's prismatic-radiating morphology

pyrolusite.description: MnO₂ — tetragonal Mn(IV) oxide with the rutile structure (P4₂/mnm, a=4.39 Å, c=2.87 Å), the default Mn⁴⁺ supergene endmember. Iron-black to dark steel-gray; black streak (NOT brown — this is the field discriminator from manganite, hardness bimodal at 6 well-crystallized vs 2 massive earthy 'soils-the-fingers' aggregate). Forms by oxidation of Mn²⁺ in two modes: low-T continental weathering / lacustrine / bog (mode A, ~95% of occurrences, T 5-40°C, pH 6.5-9, strongly oxidizing per the Hem 1963 Pourbaix diagram) and low-T hydrothermal vein (mode B, <250°C, replaces manganite in cooling vugs, source of the rare prismatic Ilfeld/Ilmenau crystals). Pyrolusite occupies the HIGH-Eh corner of the Mn⁴⁺ field; the discriminator from its catalog cousins is canonical: romanechite (Ba > 100 ppm tunnel cation), cryptomelane (K > 50 ppm), coronadite (Pb > 30 ppm), hausmannite (T > 250°C, the spinel-distorted Mn²⁺/Mn³⁺ phase), manganite (γ-MnOOH, lower Eh / cooler, dehydrates TO pyrolusite as the polianite pseudomorph). Fe-Mn supergene separation: when Fe > 2*Mn, goethite captures the oxidation budget first and pyrolusite is residual (Hem 1963 Eh sequence — Fe oxidizes ~+0.2 V earlier than Mn). IMPORTANT (Potter & Rossman 1979 Am.Min. 64:1219): the classic 'dendritic pyrolusite' in moss agate and limestone is NOT pyrolusite — it's cryptomelane / romanechite / birnessite microcrystalline aggregates. The engine refuses to ship a dendritic habit; dendrites route to those cousins when their engines land. Type-locality folklore correction: most references cite Horní Blatná (Platten) Czech Republic, but Haidinger 1827 originally used material from Eiserfeld (Siegen, Westphalia), Elgersburg, and Ilmenau (Thuringia, Germany) — Ilmenau is the defensible canonical type. Famous localities: Imini Morocco (high-grade karst-hosted ore, two-stage paragenesis per Dekoninck et al. 2016 Min.Dep. 51:13), Ilfeld Harz (classic radiating-fibrous polianite pseudomorphs), Cuyuna Range Minnesota, Mapimí Ojuela Mexico (botryoidal on calcite). Sources: Anthony et al. Handbook v.III; Dana 7th v.I pp.555-561; Potter & Rossman 1979; Birkner & Navrotsky 2017 PNAS 114:E1046 (Mn-oxide cascade thermodynamics: Mn²⁺ → hausmannite → groutite → bixbyite → pyrolusite is the thermodynamic endpoint); Champness 1971 Min.Mag. 38:245 (polianite mechanism); Hem 1963 USGS WSP 1667-A (Pourbaix + autocatalysis on existing MnO₂ surfaces); Post 1999 PNAS 96:3447 (tunnel-structure classification, pyrolusite as 1×1 tunnel endmember of the rutile-hollandite-romanechite-todorokite series).
pyrolusite._ingredients_note: Mn ≥ 0.2 ppm matches Hem's (1963) lower-bound supergene threshold; autocatalysis on existing MnO₂ surfaces (Hem 1963) means once the field fires it stays lit. Oxidizing required (Eh +0.4 to +1.0 V at pH 7) — pyrolusite is the highest-Eh Mn(IV) field endmember; uses oxideRedoxAvailable gate threshold 0.5 like hematite/magnetite/cuprite. Discriminator fork via soft suppressors (not hard cutoffs to allow weak co-deposition): Fe > 2*Mn → sigma × 0.3 (goethite/lepidocrocite capture the oxidation budget first); Ba > 100 → sigma × 0.5 (would route to romanechite); K > 50 → sigma × 0.4 (cryptomelane); Pb > 30 → sigma × 0.3 (coronadite); SiO₂ > 200 → sigma × 0.7 (todorokite + Mn-silicates compete). Hard cutoff T > 250 (hausmannite field). pH sweet spot 7-9 (Hem 1963 kinetic optimum at pH 8.5).

trace_ingredients.Ba: trace tunnel cation; partitions weakly into pyrolusite compared to romanechite
trace_ingredients.K: trace tunnel cation; partitions weakly into pyrolusite compared to cryptomelane
trace_ingredients.Pb: trace tunnel cation; partitions weakly into pyrolusite compared to coronadite
trace_ingredients.Fe: Fe-Mn weathering rind cohabitant; high Fe drives goethite to win the oxidation budget

pyrolusite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Above ~535°C in air: 4 MnO2 → 2 Mn2O3 + O2 (bixbyite formation, the thermal-reduction path per Birkner & Navrotsky 2017). Above 900°C: further reduction to Mn3O4 (hausmannite). The geological setting in the simulator (T_max 250°C) is well below the first reduction step; included for catalog completeness.

fluorescence.status: Non-fluorescent. Mn⁴⁺ is the d³ Mn oxidation state with no visible-luminescence transitions (unlike Mn²⁺ which is the bright orange-red fluorescence activator in willemite/calcite/sphalerite). Light-absorbing iron-black metallic crystals quench any ambient phosphorescence.

twin_laws.name: pyrolusite_031
twin_laws.trigger: rare polysynthetic twin on {031} — encountered in well-crystallized Ilfeld 'polianite' specimens; not common enough to be diagnostic

acid_dissolution.reaction: MnO2 + 4H⁺ + 2e⁻ → Mn²⁺ + 2H2O — the canonical Mn-oxide AMD signature; pyrolusite dissolves readily in acidic mine drainage releasing Mn²⁺ back to fluid. Stable to strong alkali (no upper pH dissolution).

iron_black.trigger: well-crystallized habit (radiating_fibrous, prismatic) — metallic luster, dark steel-gray to iron-black

sooty_black.trigger: massive_sooty / botryoidal_reniform / pseudomorph habits — dull-earthy black, the field 'soils-the-fingers' powder (hardness 2 vs 6 well-crystallized)

pyrolusite.audit_status: new v102 (2026-05-19). Default Mn⁴⁺ supergene endmember; discriminator-fork engine encodes the canonical Mn-oxide family decision tree (Ba → romanechite, K → cryptomelane, Pb → coronadite, T>250 → hausmannite, lower Eh → manganite, Fe-dominated → goethite). Sister Mn-oxide engines (romanechite/cryptomelane/coronadite/hausmannite/manganite) not yet wired — gates encode the suppressor so pyrolusite doesn't claim the full Mn(IV) budget at tunnel-cation localities. Substrate priority: rhodochrosite (epitactic replacement — 'rotted rhomb' supergene texture) > manganite (polianite pseudomorph — reserved for when manganite lands) > siderite (Mn-bearing → Fe-Mn 'wad') > dolomite (Imini karst) > calcite (Mapimí) > goethite (Fe-Mn rind) > wall. Per Potter & Rossman 1979 NO dendritic habit (the textbook dendrites are cryptomelane). First dogfood test of the vugg-add-mineral skill.
pyrolusite._late_stage_note: Supergene weathering endmember — by definition late-stage (the terminal Mn(IV) oxidation product). High propensity matches its position as the final phase in the Mn²⁺ → hausmannite → groutite → bixbyite → pyrolusite oxidation cascade (Birkner & Navrotsky 2017).

habit_variants.name: pseudomorph_after_pyromorphite
habit_variants.trigger: on pyromorphite substrate — the iconic Roughten Gill cabinet aesthetic. Cobalt-blue botryoidal crust preserves the hexagonal-prism outline of underlying pyromorphite; residual green pyromorphite tips often project through the blue crust.

habit_variants.name: pseudomorph_after_mimetite
habit_variants.trigger: on mimetite substrate — less common but documented at Roughten Gill; same hexagonal-prism preservation mechanism as pyromorphite pseudomorph.

habit_variants.name: botryoidal_mammillary
habit_variants.trigger: default crust on vug wall / cerussite / anglesite / galena substrate — cobalt-blue mammillary surface with concentric layered interior.

habit_variants.name: crystallized_rhombohedral
habit_variants.trigger: rare crystallized habit at very low supersaturation (excess < 0.3) — sub-millimeter {001} basal + {012} rhombohedron, alunite-supergroup trigonal symmetry.

plumbogummite.description: PbAl₃(PO₄)₂(OH)₅·H₂O — trigonal Pb-Al-PO₄ supergene endmember, alunite supergroup (beudantite group). The terminal phase of the Pb-PO₄ supergene paragenesis: forms LATE, after pyromorphite has had time to nucleate, then pseudomorphs it as Al accumulates from continued silicate-wallrock weathering. Type locality Roughten Gill (Caldbeck Fells, Cumbria, England) — Hartley J. (1882) MinMag 5:21 original description; Förtsch E.B. (1967) MinMag 36:530 re-examined the type material by X-ray + IR + optical methods and showed it to be a plumbogummite-hinsdalite-hidalgoite mix-crystal (alunite-supergroup endmember nomenclature; a 7.018 Å, c 16.784 Å trigonal; ω 1.680, ε 1.698). The headline cabinet aesthetic — known to UK mineral collectors as the standard Caldbeck specimen — is cobalt-blue/sky-blue/lavender/turquoise botryoidal crusts pseudomorphing pyromorphite, with residual green pyromorphite hexagonal-prism tips visible through the blue crust. The Cu trace at typical supergene levels (1-15 ppm) drives the diagnostic blue color; pure Pb-Al-PO₄ would be lavender-to-white. Found at many other UK supergene localities (Force Crag, Saddleback Old Mine, Sweet Home Mine analogs) but Roughten Gill remains the type and the most-cited specimen source. References: Hartley J. (1882) MinMag 5:21 type description; Förtsch E.B. (1967) MinMag 36:530 type-material correction; Russell A. (1925) MinMag 20:257 revisit; Bridges et al. (2011) JRS 14:3 modern Roughten Gill review; Cooper M.P. & Stanley C.J. (1990) Minerals of the English Lake District: Caldbeck Fells (canonical monograph).
plumbogummite._ingredients_note: Pb ≥ 30 (supergene Pb-rich environment) + Al ≥ 3 (KEY discriminator — aluminous silicate wallrock required, distinguishes plumbogummite from other Pb-PO4 phases) + P ≥ 2 (biological / meteoric PO4 source). Cl > 30 mildly suppresses plumbogummite via pyromorphite stability — high Cl keeps Pb-PO4 as pyromorphite rather than replacing to Al-PO4. T 5-50°C oxidizing supergene window per Hartley 1882 + Bridges 2011.

trace_ingredients.Cu: 1-15 ppm trace gives the characteristic cobalt-blue / sky-blue Roughten Gill color (the diagnostic specimen aesthetic); higher Cu drifts toward deep blue Cu-bearing alunite-supergroup end-members
trace_ingredients.Fe: 5+ ppm shifts color toward pale yellow-tan (drift toward beudantite, the Fe-PO4 endmember of the alunite supergroup)
trace_ingredients.Sr: common minor substitution at Pb²⁺ site (alunite-supergroup Sr-bearing varieties)

plumbogummite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Above ~350°C: dehydration loss of structural H2O followed by Pb-Al-PO4 → PbO + Al2O3 + P2O5 + H2O. Below the geological range of the scenarios that fire it (supergene T < 50); included for catalog completeness.

fluorescence.status: Non-fluorescent under standard SW/MW/LW UV. The Pb²⁺ + Al³⁺ + Cu²⁺ trace combination provides no fluorescence activator (Pb²⁺ at high concentrations in mineral lattice is a quencher rather than activator; Cu²⁺ trace amounts are likewise non-fluorescent). Visible cobalt-blue color is absorption + electronic transitions in Cu²⁺-Pb²⁺-O charge transfer, not fluorescence.

plumbogummite._twin_laws_note: Intentionally empty — plumbogummite (PbAl₃(PO₄)₂(OH)₅·H₂O, trigonal R3̄m) habit code 'pseudomorph_after_pyromorphite' captures its typical occurrence as a supergene pseudomorph replacing earlier pyromorphite. Doesn't nucleate fresh as euhedral individuals at vugg-scale; the pseudomorph inherits external habit from pyromorphite but no twin behavior is documented for either the replacement or the rare primary occurrences. v138 phosphate batch.

acid_dissolution.reaction: PbAl3(PO4)2(OH)5·H2O + 11H⁺ → Pb²⁺ + 3Al³⁺ + 2H3PO4 + 6H2O — full acid breakdown below pH 4; releases Pb + Al + PO4 back to fluid (slow kinetics; geologically rare since supergene window stays neutral-to-mildly-acidic).

cobalt_blue.trigger: Cu trace 1-10 ppm — the characteristic diagnostic Roughten Gill color

sky_blue.trigger: Cu 10-15 ppm — deeper blue, possible Cu-bearing end-member drift

lavender_white.trigger: Cu < 1 ppm — pure Pb-Al-PO4, rare among Roughten Gill specimens

pale_yellow_tan.trigger: Fe > 5 ppm — drift toward beudantite (Fe-PO4 alunite-group endmember)

plumbogummite.audit_status: new v108 (2026-05-20). Pb-Al-PO4 alunite-supergroup supergene endmember; completes the type-locality story for the v107 Roughten Gill scenario. Substrate priority: pyromorphite (the iconic pseudomorph substrate — cobalt-blue crust on hexagonal-prism outline) > mimetite (less common Roughten Gill substrate) > cerussite > anglesite > galena_dissolving > wall. Headline cabinet aesthetic IS the pseudomorph_after_pyromorphite habit. Discriminator from pyromorphite: pyromorphite needs Cl ≥ 5; plumbogummite needs Al ≥ 3 + Cl < 30 (high Cl favors pyromorphite stability). Per Hartley 1882 + Förtsch 1967 + Bridges 2011 + Cooper & Stanley 1990. Co-eligible alunite-supergroup endmembers (hinsdalite + hidalgoite + beudantite + corkite) noted as future add-mineral candidates per the v107 dossier — Förtsch 1967 demonstrated Hartley's type material is actually a mix-crystal of the first three. Plumbogummite ships first as the dominant species.
plumbogummite._late_stage_note: Terminal Pb-Al-PO4 of the supergene paragenesis — by definition late-stage. High propensity matches its position as the final phase that replaces pyromorphite after sufficient wallrock weathering has accumulated Al in the fluid (geologically requires 10^4-10^6 years of supergene window persistence).

habit_variants.name: gemmy_vitreous_terminated
habit_variants.trigger: high supersaturation (excess > 1.4) — sharp-faced monoclinic crystals to several cm, vitreous to adamantine luster. The best-of-Jeffrey cabinet aesthetic; also Lake Superior basalt-amygdale gem material. Dominant forms {110} prism modified by {011} pyramid and {102}.

habit_variants.name: crystallized_gem
habit_variants.trigger: default crystallized habit at moderate supersaturation (0.4 < excess ≤ 1.4) — colorless to pale-yellow monoclinic prismatic crystals, often gemmy but smaller than gemmy_vitreous_terminated. The mainstream Lake Superior + Jeffrey specimen aesthetic.

habit_variants.name: botryoidal_white
habit_variants.trigger: low supersaturation (excess ≤ 0.4) — porcelaneous botryoidal coating, reniform white-to-pale-tan crusts. Common Lake Superior basalt-amygdale filling; less collectable than the crystallized forms.

habit_variants.name: pseudomorph_after_calcite
habit_variants.trigger: rare; nucleation on dissolving calcite substrate (per Bernardini 1981 MR 12(5):277 — Jeffrey Mine specimens occasionally show calcite-scalenohedron outlines preserved with porcelaneous datolite fill).

datolite.description: CaB(SiO₄)(OH) — calcium boronosilicate (sorosilicate with B replacing Si in one tetrahedral site; Hawthorne FC, Burns PC, Grice JD 1996 Can. Min. 34:1255). Monoclinic P2₁/c, H 5-5.5, vitreous to adamantine luster, conchoidal fracture, colorless to pale-yellow / pale-pink-brown when Cu-trace, pale-yellow canary tint when Fe-trace. Low-T (50-350°C) alkaline (pH 7-12) hydrothermal vug filling in TWO distinct geological settings — both alkaline, both producing gemmy cabinet material: (1) Basaltic amygdales of the Lake Superior native-copper district (Keweenaw Peninsula MI, Isle Royale; Butler GM & Burbank WS 1929 USGS Prof. Paper 144; Bornhorst TJ 2017 GSA Memoir 213) — gemmy colorless to pale-yellow crystals with prehnite + calcite + native copper as the canonical assemblage; (2) Rodingite metasomatic contact zones in ultramafic-hosted ophiolite complexes (Jeffrey Mine, Val-des-Sources Quebec — Bernardini GP 1981 MR 12(5):277 is the canonical anchor; Val Malenco Italian Alps; New Idria CA; Coleman RG 1977 Ophiolites: Ancient Oceanic Lithosphere? Springer for the global rodingite framework) — gemmy colorless crystals with vesuvianite (often cyprine variety) + grossular + prehnite + pectolite + chrysotile gangue. The Jeffrey Mine produced the best-known cabinet datolite worldwide 1880s through mine closure 2011 — sharp gemmy crystals to several cm, often with Pectolite + Vesuvianite + Grossular associations. Anthony JW, Bideaux RA, Bladh KW, Nichols MC Handbook of Mineralogy v.IV (Arsenates, Phosphates, Vanadates) is the standard reference. Discriminator from analogous Ca-borates: datolite has Si, no other Ca-borate (colemanite, ulexite, inyoite) does. Discriminator from prehnite Ca₂Al₂Si₃O₁₀(OH)₂: prehnite has Al, datolite has B; datolite is the bonded-B borosilicate variant of the Ca-silicate family.
datolite._ingredients_note: Ca ≥ 60 (calcium from basalt amygdale plagioclase OR rodingite-metasomatic Ca-rich fluid) + B ≥ 1 (boron from late hydrothermal fluid; minor at most sites but datolite concentrates it) + SiO2 ≥ 50 (silica from host basalt or sorosilicate framework). T 50-350°C; sweet spot 100-250°C. pH 7-12 wide alkaline tolerance — Lake Superior amygdales sit ~pH 7-9, Jeffrey rodingite contacts sit pH 10-12. Activity-correction factor applied.

trace_ingredients.Cu: 1+ ppm trace gives pale pink-brown copper-bearing datolite (Lake Superior aesthetic — Cu inclusions in growth zones per Bornhorst 2017 GSA Memoir 213; sometimes called 'copper-bearing datolite' by Keweenaw collectors)
trace_ingredients.Fe: 5+ ppm shifts color toward pale-yellow / canary (Italian Alps Val Malenco aesthetic; Fe³⁺-O charge transfer)
trace_ingredients.Mn: rare trace produces faint pink tint (Massachusetts traprock localities)

datolite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Above ~400°C: datolite breaks down to wollastonite (CaSiO₃) + B(OH)₃ (boric acid, volatile in the hydrothermal regime) + H₂O. The thermal stability ceiling is bracketed by Jeffrey's higher-T (300°C) early datolite generations and the absence of datolite in regional-metamorphic-grade rodingites that exceed 400°C.

fluorescence.status: Non-fluorescent under standard SW/MW/LW UV. Pure CaB(SiO₄)(OH) has no fluorescence activator. Some Lake Superior datolite shows weak phosphorescence after SW exposure (likely U/Th trace at sub-ppm), not diagnostic; ignored in the engine.

twin_laws.name: contact_001
twin_laws._source: Anthony Handbook v.II datolite section; Dana 8th ed. Monoclinic {001} contact twin reported in Lake Superior copper-country specimens and Bor (Russia) gemmy datolite. Rare; conservative p=0.02.

acid_dissolution.reaction: CaB(SiO₄)(OH) + 4H⁺ → Ca²⁺ + B(OH)₃ + H₄SiO₄ — releases Ca + boric acid + silicic acid to fluid below pH 5. Slow kinetics in the supergene window (datolite is stable in most surface weathering environments — only acidic groundwater attack drives dissolution).

colorless_gem.trigger: Cu < 1 ppm and Fe < 5 ppm — pure colorless gem datolite (Jeffrey best material)

pale_pink_brown.trigger: Cu > 1 ppm — Lake Superior copper-bearing datolite aesthetic

pale_yellow.trigger: Fe > 5 ppm — canary tint (Italian Alps datolite)

porcelaneous_white.trigger: low supersaturation (excess ≤ 0.4) → botryoidal_white habit; the basalt-amygdale massive form

datolite.audit_status: new v110 (2026-05-20). Calcium boronosilicate, first mineral of the Jeffrey Mine rodingite arc. Substrate priority: prehnite (when wired v113) > wollastonite (v113) > vesuvianite (v111) > calcite > native_copper > magnetite > wall. Lake Superior basalt-amygdale + Jeffrey rodingite-contact paragenesis both routed through the same engine — wide pH (7-12) and T (50-350°C) windows accommodate both. B field already in FluidChemistry (pre-v89 speculative add for tourmaline) so no add-broth infra commit needed; v110 is a normal vugg-add-mineral. CALIBRATION expectation: datolite does not currently fire in any existing scenario (no scenario carries enough B + Ca + SiO2 at pH 7-12 to trigger σ > 1.2); first firing expected when jeffrey_mine ships in v115. Per Anthony Handbook v.IV silicates; Bernardini 1981 MR 12(5):277 (Jeffrey canonical); Hawthorne et al. 1996 Can.Min. 34:1255 (crystal chemistry); Bornhorst 2017 GSA Memoir 213 (Lake Superior).
datolite._late_stage_note: Mid-to-late stage in both routes. Lake Superior basalt amygdales: datolite forms after prehnite + early calcite, contemporaneous with native_copper + analcime, before terminal calcite. Jeffrey rodingite: datolite is one of the LATER Ca-silicates after vesuvianite + grossular + diopside + prehnite, contemporaneous with pectolite. Propensity 0.6 = mid-to-late but not terminal.

habit_variants.name: prismatic_tetragonal
habit_variants.trigger: default — tetragonal prismatic {100} crystals with square cross-section. The classic idocrase morphology. Vesuvius type material + most rodingite + skarn occurrences.

habit_variants.name: blocky_dipyramidal
habit_variants.trigger: high supersaturation (excess > 1.4) — chunky blocky {100} + {111} dipyramidal crystals. The cabinet idocrase aesthetic at Jeffrey + Crestmore.

habit_variants.name: cyprine_botryoidal
habit_variants.trigger: rare; low supersaturation (excess < 0.6) + Cu trace 0.5-5 ppm. Botryoidal aggregate with concentric sky-blue banding. The most-collected Jeffrey cyprine specimens.

habit_variants.name: gemmy_crystallized
habit_variants.trigger: moderate excess (0.4-1.4) + cooler T (< 280°C) — gemmy tetragonal {100} with terminated {001}, fine acicular to short prismatic. Best of the gem-grade Jeffrey specimens.

vesuvianite.description: Ca₁₀(Mg,Fe)₂Al₄(SiO₄)₅(Si₂O₇)₂(OH)₄ — tetragonal P4/nnc Ca-Mg-Al sorosilicate, also widely called IDOCRASE (the older mineralogical name, still used in jewelry trade; vesuvianite was the original Werner 1795 name from Vesuvius type locality). H 6.5, density 3.32-3.43, vitreous luster, tetragonal {100} prismatic habit with square cross-section as default. Forms in three settings, all sharing high-Ca + Mg-or-Al + Si chemistry under alkaline conditions: (1) Rodingite metasomatism — Ca-Al-rich fluid from serpentinization-driven dehydration alters cross-cutting mafic dikes in ultramafic host (Jeffrey Mine Val-des-Sources Quebec — Bernardini GP 1981 MR 12(5):277 is the canonical anchor; Italian Alps Val di Fassa + Val Malenco; New Idria CA; Cassiar BC). Jeffrey's CYPRINE variety — sky-to-deep-blue Cu-bearing vesuvianite — is the cabinet-collector standard for the species; Cu²⁺-O charge transfer at 0.5-5 ppm Cu gives sky-blue, > 5 ppm deepens to azure (Bernardini 1981 figs 12-15; the world reference cyprine material). (2) Contact metamorphism of impure limestone (skarns) — Vesuvius Italy type locality 1795 (the brown-yellow Werner material); Crestmore CA (chromian green); Tellemark Norway; Wessels Mine South Africa (pink manganvesuvianite). (3) Carbonatite-syenite alteration zones — Kovdor Russia + Magnet Cove AR (rare, yellow-brown varieties). Cyprine + green chromian + yellow-brown + pink manganvesuvianite are the four primary color varieties, all driven by trace-cation dispatch (Cu / Cr / Fe / Mn read off existing FluidChemistry fields in the engine — structurally analogous to v103 Y→fluorite + v62-era Cr→ruby pattern). References: Werner AG (1795) original type description; Allen FM & Burnham CW (1992) Am. Min. 77:268; Groat LA, Hawthorne FC, Ercit TS (1992) Can. Min. 30:19 vesuvianite crystal chemistry; Bernardini GP (1981) MR 12(5):277 Jeffrey canonical; Anthony et al. Handbook v.II; Deer Howie Zussman Rock-Forming Minerals v.1A.
vesuvianite._ingredients_note: Ca ≥ 100 (rodingite Ca-rich metasomatic OR skarn limestone-derived Ca) + Mg ≥ 30 (serpentinite-derived Mg for rodingite, or Mg-dolomite skarn) + Al ≥ 10 (mafic-dike Al for rodingite, or pelitic-impurity Al for skarn) + SiO2 ≥ 200 (host silicate framework). T 180-500°C; sweet spot 250-400°C. pH 8.5-12 strict alkaline window — distinguishes rodingite/skarn vesuvianite from any neutral-pH analog. Activity-correction factor applied.

trace_ingredients.Cu: 0.5-5 ppm trace gives sky-blue CYPRINE variety (the diagnostic Jeffrey Mine aesthetic — Cu²⁺-O charge transfer); > 5 ppm gives deep azure cyprine (the best Jeffrey cabinet material per Bernardini 1981)
trace_ingredients.Cr: 1+ ppm trace gives chromian green vesuvianite (Crestmore CA aesthetic — Cr³⁺ d-d electronic transitions)
trace_ingredients.Fe: 30+ ppm shifts color toward yellow-brown (Vesuvius classic + Magnet Cove)
trace_ingredients.Mn: 5+ ppm gives pinkish manganvesuvianite (rare; Wessels Mine SA + N'Chwaning II are the type localities)
trace_ingredients.Ti: trace substitution at Al site; produces deeper brown coloration (some Italian Alps + Russian Kola material)

vesuvianite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Above ~600°C: vesuvianite breaks down to grossular + diopside + wollastonite + H2O. The decomposition products are the higher-T equilibrium Ca-Mg-Al-Si silicate triplet — vesuvianite is thermodynamically stable as the hydrated low-T endmember of this assemblage.

fluorescence.status: Generally non-fluorescent. Some Cr-bearing chromian vesuvianite (Crestmore) shows weak red SW UV fluorescence from Cr³⁺ emission; cyprine and Fe-yellow varieties are non-fluorescent. Not diagnostic; ignored in the engine.

twin_laws.name: contact_110
twin_laws._source: Anthony Handbook v.II vesuvianite section. Tetragonal {110} contact twin reported in Alpine + Russian skarn specimens. Rare; conservative p=0.02.

acid_dissolution.reaction: Ca10Mg2Al4(SiO4)5(Si2O7)2(OH)4 + acid → Ca²⁺ + Mg²⁺ + Al³⁺ + H4SiO4 — releases all cations to fluid below pH 6. Slow kinetics; vesuvianite is geologically stable in most weathering environments and only breaks down under strong-acid attack (e.g., supergene acid-mine drainage).

brown_yellow_idocrase.trigger: no significant chromophore — pure Ca-Mg-Al-Si — default Vesuvius classic

sky_blue_cyprine.trigger: Cu 0.5-5 ppm — the diagnostic Jeffrey Mine aesthetic (Cu²⁺-O charge transfer)

deep_azure_cyprine.trigger: Cu > 5 ppm — best Jeffrey cabinet material per Bernardini 1981

chromian_green.trigger: Cr > 1 ppm — Crestmore CA aesthetic (Cr³⁺ d-d transitions)

yellow_brown_iron.trigger: Fe > 30 ppm — Magnet Cove + Vesuvius classic

pink_mangan_vesuvianite.trigger: Mn > 5 ppm — rare; Wessels Mine SA type-locality

vesuvianite.audit_status: new v111 (2026-05-20). Tetragonal Ca-Mg-Al sorosilicate, Jeffrey Mine cabinet headline aesthetic via CYPRINE variety (Cu trace dispatch). Substrate priority: grossular (epitactic — when wired v112) > diopside (v112) > wollastonite (v113) > magnetite > calcite > wall. Color dispatch via existing FluidChemistry fields (Cu/Cr/Fe/Mn) — no new field needed; analogous to v103 Y→fluorite + v62-era Cr→ruby trace-cation pattern. CALIBRATION expectation: vesuvianite does not currently fire in any existing scenario (no scenario carries Ca≥100 + Mg≥30 + Al≥10 + SiO2≥200 at pH 8.5-12 with T 180-500°C — rodingite chemistry is novel); first firing expected when jeffrey_mine ships in v115. Per Bernardini 1981 MR 12(5):277 (Jeffrey canonical); Allen & Burnham 1992 Am. Min. 77:268; Groat et al. 1992 Can. Min. 30:19; Anthony Handbook v.II; Werner 1795 type description.
vesuvianite._late_stage_note: Mid-stage in rodingite paragenesis — vesuvianite forms after early grossular + diopside but before the Ca-silicate late-stage trio (pectolite + wollastonite + prehnite). In skarns, vesuvianite is contemporaneous with grossular + diopside in the prograde sequence. Propensity 0.5 = mid; not strongly biased to early or late.

habit_variants.name: dodecahedral
habit_variants.trigger: default — classic 12-faced {110} garnet form. Most rodingite + skarn occurrences.

habit_variants.name: trapezohedral
habit_variants.trigger: high supersaturation (excess > 1.4) — 24-faced {211} crystal form, often combined with dodecahedral. Best cabinet specimens (Jeffrey + Asbestos Hill NL + Italian Alps).

habit_variants.name: massive_granular
habit_variants.trigger: low supersaturation (excess < 0.4) — massive aggregate, no terminations. The skarn workhorse texture; common but less collectable.

grossular.description: Ca₃Al₂(SiO₄)₃ — cubic Ia-3d Ca-Al endmember of the garnet group (orthosilicate). H 6.5-7.5, density 3.59, vitreous to adamantine luster, no cleavage, conchoidal fracture. Forms in three settings: (1) RODINGITE METASOMATISM — Ca-Al-rich fluid from serpentinization-driven dehydration alters cross-cutting mafic dikes in ultramafic host (Jeffrey Mine Quebec — Bernardini 1981 MR 12(5):277 documents chromian green + hessonite varieties; Asbestos Hill NL; Italian Alps Val d'Ala IS the type-locality for HESSONITE; Outokumpu Finland). (2) SKARN contact-metamorphism of impure limestone (Vesuvius IT type 1795 area; Crestmore CA chromian variety; Sierra de Cruces MX hessonite; De Kalb NY). (3) Carbonatite alteration (rare; late-magmatic; Kovdor RU). VARIETIES via trace-cation dispatch (no new field needed — reads existing FluidChemistry Cr/Mn/Fe): chromian green (Cr trace > 1 ppm, sometimes called 'tsavorite sensu lato' though strict tsavorite is the Tsavo East National Park material; Cr³⁺ d-d transitions), hessonite (orange-pink — Mn²⁺ + Fe³⁺ crystal-field combination per Manning JE 1967 Min. Mag. 36:572 + Geiger CA 2004 RIMG 56:213), leuco-hessonite intermediate (Fe trace), colorless/pale-yellow pure (rare; Jeffrey best material). Discriminator from analogous garnets: ANDRADITE Ca₃Fe₂(SiO₄)₃ (Fe replaces Al — would route via separate Andradite engine when wired); SPESSARTINE Mn₃Al₂(SiO₄)₃ (Mn replaces Ca — separate engine); UVAROVITE Ca₃Cr₂(SiO₄)₃ (Cr replaces Al — high-Cr endmember, rare). The chromian-grossular variety encoded HERE is Cr substituting trace in Al-rich grossular, NOT the uvarovite endmember. References: Anthony JW et al. Handbook of Mineralogy v.IA Orthosilicates; Deer WA, Howie RA, Zussman J Rock-Forming Minerals v.1A; Manning & Bird 1990 J. Petrol. 31:1; Bernardini GP 1981 MR 12(5):277; Manning 1967 Min. Mag. 36:572; Geiger 2004 RIMG 56:213; Werner AG 1797 ('granat' nomenclature).
grossular._ingredients_note: Ca ≥ 80 (rodingite Ca-rich OR skarn limestone Ca) + Al ≥ 15 (mafic-dike OR pelitic-impurity Al) + SiO2 ≥ 150 (silicate framework). T 250-600°C wide window; sweet spot 300-450°C. pH 7-12 broad alkaline tolerance — distinguishes from acid-pH garnet-group cousins (almandine). Activity-correction factor applied.

trace_ingredients.Cr: 1+ ppm gives chromian green grossular (sometimes called 'tsavorite sensu lato'; Cr³⁺ d-d transitions at 14000 + 22000 cm⁻¹)
trace_ingredients.Mn: 5+ ppm in combination with Fe drives hessonite (orange-pink; Manning 1967)
trace_ingredients.Fe: 30+ ppm with Mn drives hessonite; 5-30 ppm alone drives leuco-hessonite intermediate
trace_ingredients.V: rare trace at high abundance gives uvarovite-like green (the Cr/V analog substitution)

grossular.thermal_decomp_reaction: Above ~800°C: grossular breaks down to anorthite + gehlenite + wollastonite or returns to melt depending on bulk chemistry. Geologically rare in vug-formation regime; included for catalog completeness.

fluorescence.status: Generally non-fluorescent. Some Mn-bearing hessonite shows weak red fluorescence from Mn²⁺ (very faint, not diagnostic). Ignored in the engine.

twin_laws.name: contact_111
twin_laws._source: Dana 8th ed. garnet group. Cubic {111} contact twin reported very rarely in grossular — garnet group is one of the least-commonly-twinned cubic mineral families. Data sparse; conservative p=0.01.

acid_dissolution.reaction: Ca3Al2(SiO4)3 + 12H⁺ → 3Ca²⁺ + 2Al³⁺ + 3H4SiO4 — releases Ca + Al + Si to fluid below pH 5. Slow kinetics; grossular is geologically very stable.

colorless_pale_yellow.trigger: no significant chromophore — pure Ca-Al endmember; rare best Jeffrey material

chromian_green.trigger: Cr > 1 ppm — Cr³⁺ d-d transitions; 'tsavorite sensu lato' or Crestmore chromian green

hessonite.trigger: Mn > 5 ppm AND Fe > 30 ppm (or combination) — orange-pink Mn²⁺/Fe³⁺ crystal-field combination per Manning 1967

leuco_hessonite.trigger: Fe 5-30 ppm without Mn — pale orange-brown intermediate

grossular.audit_status: new v112 (2026-05-20). Cubic Ca-Al garnet endmember, paired commit with diopside (shared rodingite/skarn family). Substrate priority: diopside > wollastonite (v113) > calcite > magnetite > wall. Color dispatch via existing FluidChemistry fields (Cr/Mn/Fe) — no new infra. CALIBRATION expectation: grossular does not currently fire in any existing scenario; first firing expected when jeffrey_mine ships in v115. Wired but not yet firing — third of three Jeffrey-arc minerals to follow this pattern (with v110 datolite, v111 vesuvianite). Per Anthony Handbook v.IA; Deer Howie Zussman v.1A; Manning & Bird 1990; Bernardini 1981; Manning 1967.
grossular._late_stage_note: Early-stage in the rodingite + skarn prograde sequence. Grossular forms BEFORE vesuvianite + pectolite + wollastonite + prehnite — the first major Ca-Al silicate to crystallize as the Ca-Al-rich metasomatic fluid hits the mafic dike. Propensity 0.3 = early.

habit_variants.name: prismatic_square
habit_variants.trigger: default — short {100} prismatic crystals with square cross-section. The classic clinopyroxene morphology.

habit_variants.name: tabular
habit_variants.trigger: high supersaturation (excess > 1.4) — flat tabular {010} tablets. Less common cabinet form.

habit_variants.name: acicular
habit_variants.trigger: low T (< 280°C) + moderate excess — elongated acicular needles. Alpine-fissure habit, also late-stage rodingite at Jeffrey.

habit_variants.name: massive_granular
habit_variants.trigger: low supersaturation (excess < 0.4) — granular aggregate, the skarn-workhorse texture.

diopside.description: CaMgSi₂O₆ — monoclinic C2/c Ca-Mg endmember of the clinopyroxene group (single-chain inosilicate). H 5.5-6.5, density 3.27, vitreous luster, prismatic {100} cleavage at ~87°/93°. Forms in three settings: (1) RODINGITE METASOMATISM — Ca-Al-Mg-rich fluid from serpentinization-driven dehydration alters cross-cutting mafic dikes in ultramafic host; CHROME-DIOPSIDE (emerald green Cr-trace variety) is the Jeffrey Mine cabinet aesthetic per Bernardini 1981 MR 12(5):277; Italian Alps Val Malenco; Outokumpu Finland. (2) SKARN contact-metamorphism of dolomitic limestone (Crestmore CA, De Kalb NY, Pakistan, the Roopnarine 2017 review covers North American skarns); the white-grey colorless form dominates. (3) KIMBERLITE / PERIDOTITE XENOLITHS — gem-quality chrome-diopside in mantle-derived rocks (Tanzania Mahenge, Russia Yakutia Mir mine, Outokumpu shield-derived). Trace-cation color dispatch: Cr > 0.5 ppm → chrome-diopside emerald-green (Cr³⁺ in M2 site, d-d transitions at 14500 + 22500 cm⁻¹); Fe > 20 ppm → grey-green-brown (the common skarn shade); pure → colorless/white. Refs: Anthony Handbook v.IIB Single-Chain Silicates; Deer Howie Zussman v.2A; Cameron M & Papike JJ (1981) 'Structural and chemical variations in pyroxenes', Reviews in Mineralogy 7:5-92; Bernardini GP (1981) MR 12(5):277 Jeffrey canonical; Manning 1968 Am. Min. 53:1187 (chrome-diopside crystal field).
diopside._ingredients_note: Ca ≥ 60 (rodingite OR skarn Ca) + Mg ≥ 40 (serpentinite-derived Mg for rodingite; Mg-dolomite for skarn) + SiO2 ≥ 150 (silicate framework). T 200-600°C wide window; sweet spot 280-450°C. pH 7-12 broad alkaline tolerance. Activity-correction factor applied.

trace_ingredients.Cr: 0.5+ ppm gives chrome-diopside emerald green (gem grade; Jeffrey + Outokumpu + Tanzania kimberlite aesthetic; Cr³⁺ M2-site d-d transitions per Manning 1968)
trace_ingredients.Fe: 20+ ppm gives grey-green-brown diopside (the common skarn/rodingite shade — Fe²⁺ partial M1 substitution)
trace_ingredients.Mn: rare trace gives violan (violet-blue) — Italian variety; M2-site Mn²⁺
trace_ingredients.Na: trace substitution at M2 site shifts toward augite end-member; ignored at typical rodingite levels

diopside.thermal_decomp_reaction: Above ~1400°C: diopside melts congruently. Below melting, stable across all geological vug regimes; no decomposition reaction needed at relevant T.

fluorescence.status: Non-fluorescent under standard UV. Mn-bearing diopside (violan) may show weak orange fluorescence at high Mn but not diagnostic; ignored in the engine.

twin_laws.trigger: common simple twinning on {100} in skarn + rodingite diopside

acid_dissolution.reaction: CaMgSi2O6 + 4H⁺ → Ca²⁺ + Mg²⁺ + 2H4SiO4 — releases Ca + Mg + Si to fluid below pH 5. Slow kinetics; pyroxene-group minerals are relatively stable.

colorless_white.trigger: no significant chromophore — pure Ca-Mg endmember; Italian Alps + Pakistan pure material

chrome_diopside_emerald_green.trigger: Cr > 0.5 ppm — gem grade; Cr³⁺ M2-site d-d transitions; Jeffrey + Outokumpu + Tanzania kimberlite

grey_green_brown.trigger: Fe > 20 ppm — the common skarn/rodingite shade

diopside.audit_status: new v112 (2026-05-20). Monoclinic Ca-Mg clinopyroxene endmember, paired commit with grossular (shared rodingite/skarn family). Substrate priority: serpentinite/chrysotile (the rodingite host matrix, when wired v114) > grossular > wollastonite (v113) > calcite > wall. Color dispatch via existing FluidChemistry fields (Cr/Fe/Mn) — no new infra. CALIBRATION expectation: diopside does not currently fire in any existing scenario; first firing expected when jeffrey_mine ships in v115. Wired but not yet firing per the v110-v112 pattern. Per Anthony Handbook v.IIB; Deer Howie Zussman v.2A; Cameron & Papike 1981 RIMG 7; Bernardini 1981 MR 12(5):277; Manning 1968 Am. Min. 53:1187.
diopside._late_stage_note: Early-to-mid stage in the rodingite + skarn prograde sequence. Diopside forms early alongside grossular as the Ca-Mg-Si chemistry crystallizes from the metasomatic fluid; both precede vesuvianite + pectolite + wollastonite + prehnite. Propensity 0.35 = early-mid.

habit_variants.name: spray_radiating
habit_variants.trigger: high supersaturation (excess > 0.8) — radiating spray aggregate, acicular crystals from common center. The Jeffrey Mine cabbage-petal cabinet aesthetic; spray-on-grossular is the iconic substrate.

habit_variants.name: acicular_white
habit_variants.trigger: moderate excess (0.3-0.8) — parallel acicular needles, white silky luster.

habit_variants.name: massive_fibrous
habit_variants.trigger: low excess (< 0.3) — compact fibrous aggregate, Larimar-similar massive form (without the Cu coloration).

pectolite.description: NaCa₂Si₃O₈(OH) — triclinic Na-Ca single-chain inosilicate. H 4.5-5, density 2.84-2.90, silky to vitreous luster, perfect {100} + {001} cleavage, conchoidal fracture. The CABBAGE-PETAL / RADIATING-SPRAY habit is the species signature — acicular crystals fanning from a common center. Three settings: (1) Rodingite metasomatism (Jeffrey Mine Quebec — Bernardini 1981 MR 12(5):277 documents the iconic spray-on-grossular habit; Italian Alps; Outokumpu FI; New Idria CA), (2) Late-stage basalt-amygdale fillings (Bay of Fundy NS; New Jersey diabase; Iceland; with native copper at Lake Superior), (3) LARIMAR (Cu-bearing blue gem variety) — Filipos & Frantz 1979 Bahoruco Range Dominican Republic; the Cu²⁺-O charge transfer at trace levels gives the diagnostic sky-blue. Larimar fires through this same engine but with Cu > 0.5 ppm dispatch. Discriminator from analogous fibrous Ca-silicates: wollastonite has no Na, no OH; pectolite has both. Refs: Anthony JW et al. Handbook of Mineralogy v.IIB Single-Chain Silicates; Deer WA, Howie RA, Zussman J Rock-Forming Minerals v.2A; Filipos PJ & Frantz JD (1979) Larimar — A blue pectolite from Hispaniola. Geological Magazine 116:323; Bernardini GP (1981) MR 12(5):277.
pectolite._ingredients_note: Na ≥ 30 (the diagnostic — pectolite is the Na-Ca silicate of the trio; differs from wollastonite which has no Na) + Ca ≥ 80 + SiO2 ≥ 100. T 100-350°C; sweet spot 150-280°C (late-stage rodingite + amygdale window). pH 8.5-12 strict alkaline. Activity correction applied.

trace_ingredients.Cu: 0.5+ ppm trace gives blue larimar-tinted pectolite (Cu²⁺-O charge transfer; Dominican Republic gem variety per Filipos & Frantz 1979)
trace_ingredients.Mn: rare trace produces pinkish tint at very high Mn

pectolite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Above ~500°C: pectolite breaks down to wollastonite + diopside + Na-bearing aqueous phase. Geologically sets the upper-T ceiling for pectolite stability.

fluorescence.status: Some pectolite from New Jersey shows weak orange-pink SW UV fluorescence (Mn²⁺ activator at higher Mn levels). Larimar from Dominican is non-fluorescent. Ignored in the engine; not diagnostic.

twin_laws.name: polysynthetic_100
twin_laws._source: Anthony Handbook v.II pectolite section; Liebau (1985) Structural Chemistry of Silicates.
